questions.question: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation.
It may be represented by H2O (l) ⇌ H2O (vap)
The double half arrows indicate that the processes in both the directions are going on simultaneously. The mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture.

questions.question: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.explanation: Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in an equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features. The mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant. However, the equilibrium is not static. The intense activity can be noticed at the boundary between ice and water. Molecules from the liquid water collide against ice and adhere to it and some molecules of ice escape into the liquid phase. There is no change of mass of ice and water, as the rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmosphere pressure and 273K.

questions.question: During the process of transformation from liquid to vapour, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant. This is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The number of water molecules from the gaseous state into the liquid state also increases till the equilibrium is attained.
i.e.rate of evaporation = rate of condensation
H2O(l) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex}  H2O (vap)
At equilibrium, the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant and is called the equilibrium vapour pressure of water increases with temperature.

questions.question: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.explanation: We place solid iodine in a closed vessel, after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour and the intensity of colour increases with time. After a certain time, the intensity of colour becomes constant and at this stage, equilibrium is attained. Hence solid iodine sublimes to give iodine vapour and the iodine vapour condenses to give solid iodine. The equilibrium can be represented as I2 (solid) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} I2 (vapour).

questions.question: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: Water and water vapour are in equilibrium position at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) and at 100°C in a closed vessel. The boiling point of water is 100°C at 1.013 bar pressure. For any pure liquid at one atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), the temperature at which the liquid and vapours are at equilibrium is called the normal boiling point of the liquid. The boiling point of the liquid depends on the atmospheric pressure.

questions.question: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature for physical processes. When equilibrium is attained for a physical process, it is characterized by the constant value of one of its parameters at a given temperature.

questions.question: For liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour equilibrium, at a given temperature, the constant is ________.
questions.explanation: The vapour pressure is constant at a given temperature for liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour equilibrium. The equilibrium vapour pressure of a compound at a given temperature is an intrinsic property of the compound, meaning it does not depend on variables like volume, the quantity of the compound, etc.

questions.question: At a particular temperature and atmospheric pressure, the solid and liquid phases of a pure substance can exist in equilibrium. Which of the following term defines this temperature?

options.option: Normal melting point and Freezing point

questions.explanation: These are normal melting point and freezing point since they are measured at atmospheric pressure.

questions.question:
On applying pressure to the equilibrium,
{tex}\text { ice } \rightleftharpoons \text { water }{/tex}
Which phenomenon will happen?
questions.explanation: Volume of ice > volume of water and thus, increase in pressure favours forward reaction showing decrease in volume.

questions.question: Which one is reversible process?

options.option: Melting of ice at 1°C in a open container
Evaporation of water at 100°C and 1 atm pressure in a closed container
Melting of ice at 10°C in a closed container
Mixing of two gases by diffusion in a closed container

questions.explanation: At 100°C and 1 atm: water {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} vapour

questions.question: In liquid-gas equilibrium, the pressure of vapours above the liquid is constant at:
questions.explanation:
For liquid {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} gas
Kp = Pgas
Vapour pressure of a liquid is constant at constant temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a general characteristic of equilibria involving physical processes?

options.option: All measurable properties of the system remain constant.
Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature.
All the physical processes stop at equilibrium.
The opposing processes occur at the same rate and there is dynamic but stable condition.

questions.explanation: A physical equilibrium is dynamic in nature, both forward and reverse process occur at equal rates. The some kind of motion is always present. The individual molecules continuously move from one phase to another.

questions.question: If pressure is applied to the equilibrium of solid {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} liquid. The melting point of the solid:

options.option: may increase or decrease depending upon the nature of solid

questions.explanation: may increase or decrease depending upon the nature of solid

questions.question: The increase of pressure on ice {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} water system at constant temperature will lead to

options.option: a decrease in the entropy of the system
an increase in the Gibb’s energy of the system
a shift of the equilibrium in the forward direction

questions.explanation:
The volume of ice is greater than that of water. The direction in which the reaction will proceed can be predicted by applying Le-Chatelier's principle
{tex}\text { Pressure } \propto \frac{1}{\text { Volume }}{/tex}
So equilibrium will shift forward.

questions.question: If KC > 10x, the products predominate over reactants. Then what is the value of x?
questions.explanation: If KC > 10x, the products are predominant, over the reactants in a chemical reaction because if KC is very large the reaction proceeds nearly to completion an example for this is at 300K, H2(g) + Cl2(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2HCl(g), KC = 4 x 1031.

questions.question:
Consider two sets of an experiment carried out with the same amount of reactants:

  1. Steam passed over heated iron filings in a closed tube.
  2. Steam passed over heated iron filings in an open tube.
    At the end of the reaction ________.

options.option: both tubes will contain the same amount of Fe_3O_4
amount of unreacted Fe in (i) < amount of unreacted Fe in (ii)
amount of hydrogen in (i) > amount of hydrogen in (ii)
amount of unreacted Fe in (i) > amount of unreacted Fe in (ii)

questions.explanation:
In a closed tube, the reaction becomes reversible and attains a state of equilibrium.
{tex}\mathop {3{\text{Fe}}}\limits_{Solid} + \mathop {4{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}}\limits_{Steam} \rightleftharpoons {\text{F}}{{\text{e}}_3}{{\text{O}}_4} + 4{{\text{H}}_2}{/tex}

questions.question:
In the process of melting of sulphur, which of the following is TRUE?

  1. Less sulphur will melt at high temperature
  2. Less sulphur will melt at high pressure.
  3. More sulphur will melt at low temperature.
  4. Less sulphur will melt at low pressure.
questions.explanation:
On increasing the pressure less sulphur will melt as melting of sulphur results in an increase in the volume.
Sulphur(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Sulphur(l) .... Endothermic

questions.question: Diagram X represents molecules of carbon dioxide trapped in a closed cola bottle. Which of the following option CORRECTLY represents what happens after the bottle is opened?
questions.explanation: Solubility of gas in solution decreases with a decrease in pressure. As the partial pressure of carbon dioxide decreases, its solubility decrease. Hence, fewer molecules of carbon dioxide will be present in the cola bottle after the cap is opened. This is a simple demonstration of Henry’s law.

questions.question: In which of the following flasks does a state equilibrium exist between ice and water?
questions.explanation: ii - closed system and iii - isolated system. A state of equilibrium between two phases can exist in a system in which there is no transfer of mass, i.e., ii and iii.

topics.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium

questions.question:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:

options.option: The concentrations of the reactants are decreased.
The concentrations of the products are increased.
Rates of the forward reaction is faster.
No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.

questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium
questions.explanation: There is no change in the conc of reactant and product. At dynamic equilibrium, reactants are converted to products, and products are converted to reactants at an equal and constant rate. Reactions do not necessarily—and most often do not—end up with equal concentrations. Equilibrium is the state of equal, opposite rates, not equal concentrations.

questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium
questions.explanation:
N2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2 

t = 0 1 0
t = t 1 - x 2x

total moles at eqm(t = t) = 1 - x + 2x = 1+ x

Pure ammonia is placed in a vessel at a temperature where its dissociation constant ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) is appreciable. At equilibrium:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}

options.option: concentration of hydrogen is less than that of nitrogen
concentration of NH_3 does not change with pressure
K_p does not change significantly with pressure

questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium
questions.explanation: Statement (a) is correct and the rest statements are wrong. Kp depends only on temperature, hence at constant temp. Kp will not change.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a reversible reaction?

options.option: It proceeds only in forward direction
Number of moles of reactants and products are equal
It can be influenced by a catalyst

questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium
questions.explanation:
A characteristic feature of reversible reactions is that they never proceed to completion.
Reversible reactions always attain equilibrium and never go for completion. Reversible reactions, however, a static equilibrium is not being established. In most of the reactions when carried out in closed vessels do not go to completion, under a given set of conditions of temperature and pressure. In fact in all such cases, in the initial state, only the reactants are present but as the reaction proceeds, the concentration of reactants decreases and that of products increases. Finally, a stage is reached when no further change in the concentration of the reactants and products is observed.

questions.question: In the gas phase reaction {tex}{C_2}{H_2} + {H_2} \rightleftharpoons {C_2}{H_6},{/tex} the equilibrium constant can be expressed in
questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{[C_2H_6]}{[C_2H_4][H_2]}= \frac{mole.litre^{-1}}{(mole.litre^{-1}).(mole.litre^{-1})} = \frac{1}{mole.litre^{-1}} = \frac{litre}{mole}{/tex}

questions.explanation: A reaction may be exothermic or endothermic. It may be reversible or irreversible depending upon the nature of the reaction and experimental conditions.

questions.question: For an equilibrium change involving gaseous phase, the forward reaction is first order while the reverse reaction is second order. The unit of KP for the forward equilibrium is:
questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium

r1 = kf [Reactant]
r2 = kb [Product]
{tex}\because \quad r_{1}=r_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{c}=\frac{k_{f}}{k_{b}}=\frac{[\text { Product }]^{2}}{[\text { Reactant }]}{/tex}
{tex}\because {/tex} P {tex}\propto{/tex} concentration
Thus, unit will be unit of P i.e., atm.

questions.question: One mole of N2O4 (g) at 300 K is kept in a closed container under 1 atm. It is heated to 600 K when 20% by mass of N2O4 (g) decomposes to NO2 (g). The resultant pressure is:
questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium

{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_1 {O_4}}\limits_{1. - 0.20} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2\mathop N\limits_0 {O_2}}\limits_{2 \times 0.20} {/tex}
Mass of N2O4 {tex}=1 \times 92 \mathrm{~g}{/tex} = 1 mole
Dissociated weight {tex}=\frac{92 \times 20}{100}{/tex} = 18.4 g = 0.2 mole
{tex}P_{\mathrm{N}_{{{2}}} O_{4}}{/tex} at 300 K = 1 atm. {tex}\therefore P_{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}}{/tex} at 600 K = 2 atm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total mole present = 1 - 0.20 + 0.40 = 1.20
{tex}\because \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}{/tex} at constant T, V
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{2}{P_{2}}=\frac{1}{1.2} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} P1 = 2.4 atm

questions.explanation:

Reversible reactions always attain equilibrium and never go for completion. Reversible reactions, however a static equilibrium is not being established. In these most of the reactions when carried out in closed vessels do not go to completion, under a given set of conditions of temperature and pressure. questions.topicName: Equilibrium in Chemical Processes - Dynamic Equilibrium

options.option: the velocity of the forward reaction is decreasing and that of backward reaction is increasing
the velocity of both forward and backward reactions is decreasing
the velocity of both forward reaction and backward reaction is also increasing

questions.explanation:
Before attaining the equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction decreases due to decrease in concentration of reactants with time and the rate of backward reaction increases due to increase in concentration of products with time.

Attainment of equilibrium in a reversible reaction.

topics.topicName: Law of Chemical Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant

questions.question: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, {tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}{/tex} Where Kc is called as ________.
questions.topicName: Law of Chemical Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a general reversible reaction:
A + B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} C + D
where A and B are the reactants, C and D are the products in the balanced chemical equation.
On the basis of experimental studies of many reversible reactions, opposed in 1864 that the concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, Kc = [C] [D] /[A] [B]
Where Kc is the equilibrium constant and the expression on the right side is called the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: Equilibrium constant for the reaction, 4NH3(g) + 5O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO(g) + 6H2O (g) is written as:
questions.topicName: Law of Chemical Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant
questions.explanation:
Kc is equilibrium constant {tex} = \frac{{{{\left[ {products} \right]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}{{{{[reactant]}^{stoichiimetry}}}}{/tex}    
{tex}{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = {\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{NO}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right]^{\text{6}}}/{\text{ }}{\left[ {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right]^{\text{4}}}{\left[ {{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]^{\text{5}}}{/tex}

questions.question: For reactions involving gases, however, it is usually more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of
questions.topicName: Law of Chemical Equilibrium and Equilibrium Constant
questions.explanation: Kp = {tex}\frac{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { products }}\right)^{\text { sttoichimery }}}{\left(\mathbf{P}_{\text { reactant }}\right)^{\text { stoichimetry }}}{/tex}

In reaction, N2 (g) + 3H2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3 (g)

Therefore, Kp = Kc (RT)-2

questions.question:
Find Kc for the following reaction, if CO2 pressure is in atmospheres:
CaCO3(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 (at 1000K).

{tex}2.1 \times {10^{ - 4}} = {K_c}{(0.0821 \times 1000)^1}{/tex}
{tex}{K_c} = \frac{{2.1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{82.1}} = 2.57 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 2.6 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}

questions.question: PCl5, PCl3 and Cl2 are at equilibrium at 500 K in a closed container and their concentrations are 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1, 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 and 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 respectively. The value of Kc for the reaction PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) will be:
