questions.question:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium:
2ICl(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} I2(aq) + Cl2(aq)
Initially, [ICl] = 0.050 M, [I2] = 0.035 M and [CI2] = 0.030 M
What is the equilibrium concentration of ICl, if Kc = 0.145?
questions.explanation:
2ICl(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} I2(aq) + Cl2(aq)
(M) ICl I3 Cl3
I 0.05 0.035 0.030
C +2x -x -x
E (0.05 + 2x) (0.035 - x) 0.03 - x

Q = {tex}\frac{(0.03)(0.035)}{(0.05)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.42
Since Q > Kc, the reaction will proceed towards reactants.
Now, Kc = {tex}\frac{(0.035-x)(0.03-x)}{(0.05+2 x)^{2}}{/tex}
(0.05 + 2x)2 0.145 = 0.00105 - 0.065x + x2
On solving, we get 0.42x2 - 0.094x + 6.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 = 0
The roots of above quadratic equation is determined using formula,
x = {tex} {-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac} \over 2a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.216 or 0.007
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0.007
[ICl]eq = 0.05 + 2x
[ICl]eq = 0.064 M

questions.question: For a reaction of the type, AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A + B, the total pressure at which AB gets 50% dissociated will be numerically equal to:
questions.explanation:
AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A + B
Initial (mol) 1 0 0
Equilibrium (mol) {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{50}{100}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Total no. of moles = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Let P be the total pressure of the gaseous mixture.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Partial pressure of a component in a mixture = {tex}\frac{\text { no. of mol of component }}{\text { Total no. of mol at equilibrium }} \times \mathrm{P}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pA = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}} \times {P}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times {P}=\frac{1}{3} {P}{/tex}
Since pA = pB, so pB = {tex}\frac{1}{3}P{/tex};
pAB = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}} \times {P}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times {P}=\frac{1}{3} {P}{/tex}
But KP = {tex}\frac{[{A}][{B}]}{[{AB}]}=\frac{\frac{{P}}{3} \times \frac{{P}}{3}}{\frac{{P}}{3}}=\frac{{P}}{3}{/tex}
P = 3Kp

questions.question: Consider a chemical reaction A(g) + B(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C(g), for which K is 100. If the above equation is multiplied by 2, the value of K becomes ________.
If the value of the equilibrium constant for a particular reaction is 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012, then at equilibrium the system will contain ________.

options.option: similar amounts of reactants and products

questions.explanation: If the equilibrium constant (Kc) is very large (generally if Kc > 103), then the reaction is in favor of products. Hence, for the given reaction, at equilibrium, the system will contain the majority of products.

questions.question: Which of the following lines CORRECTLY show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant, K, for an exothermic reaction?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = -RT In K
Now, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}So
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho - T{tex}\triangle{/tex}So = -RT In K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In K = {tex}\frac{\Delta {H}^{\circ}}{{RT}}+\frac{\Delta {S}^{\circ}}{{R}}{/tex}
When the temperature range is small, {tex}\triangle{/tex}Ho and {tex}\triangle{/tex}So can be considered constant, hence the equation gives In K as a linear function of {tex}\frac 1T{/tex}.
In K = {tex}\underbrace{\left(\frac{-\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\right)}_{\text {Slope }} \times \frac{1}{T}+\underbrace{\frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}}_{\text {Intercept }}{/tex}
For an exothermic reaction, the enthalpy change is negative. Therefore, the plot of In K versus 1/T will have a positive slope.
Hence, lines A and B correctly show the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant (K) for an exothermic reaction.

questions.question: For which of the following values of equilibrium constant does the reaction mixture contain mostly reactants?
questions.explanation: The smaller the value of K, the greater will be the concentration of reactants.

questions.question: Identify the reaction in which appreciable concentrations of both reactants and products are present at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: N2O(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g) (Kc = 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)

questions.question:
The reaction A2(g) + 3B2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2AB3(g) has an equilibrium constant K = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10. Which reaction mixture below represents this reaction at equilibrium?
Here,
questions.explanation:
For each mixture, determine Q. In case of reaction mixture (iii), Q = K
Q = {tex}\frac{\left[{AB}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[{A}_{2}\right]\left[{B}_{2}\right]^{3}}=\frac{(1)^{2}}{(3)(3)^{3}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = K

questions.question:
The following diagrams show equilibrium mixtures of A2, B2 and AB at two different temperatures, (T2 > T1). Which of the following is TRUE about the reaction:
A2(g) + B2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2AB(g)?

options.option: Value of K at T_2 is larger than its value at T_1.
The value of K remains constant.

questions.explanation: Fewer AB molecules are produced at T2. This indicates the equilibrium has shifted to the left upon increasing the temperature. Thus, the reaction is exothermic.

questions.question:
A weak acid, HA, has a Ka, of 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. If 0.100 mole of this acid is dissolved in one litre of water, the
percentage of acid dissociated at equilibrium is closest to
questions.explanation:
Ka = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}=\frac{0.1 x \times 0.1 x}{0.1-x}{/tex}
Assume x < < 0.1 
1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}=\frac{x^2 \times 0.01}{0.1} \Rightarrow x^2{/tex}  = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
Percentage of acid dissociated
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.0%

questions.question:
The value of Kp for the reaction
2SO2 (g) + O2 (g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2SO3 (g) is 5.
What will be the partial pressure of O2 at equilibrium when equal moles of SO2 and SO3 are present at equilibrium?
questions.explanation:
2SO2 + O2 {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2SO3
Kp = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}^2}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}^2 \times \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{/tex} = 5
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{SO}_2}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{SO}_2}{/tex} = {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_2}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{SO}_3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_2}{/tex} = 0.2

questions.question:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction.
{tex}A _2 \rightleftharpoons 2 A{/tex}
at 500 K and 700 K are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The given reaction is:
questions.explanation:
Since the equilibrium constant increases with temperature (from {tex}10^{-10}{/tex} at 500 K to {tex}10^{-5}{/tex} at 700 K), the forward reaction is favored at higher temperature.
By Le Chatelier's Principle, this means the reaction absorbs heat, so it is endothermic.

questions.question: The rate constant for forward and backward reactions of hydrolysis of ester are 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 and 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 per minute. Equilibrium constant for the reaction, CH3COOC2H5 + H+ {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + C2H5OH is:
questions.explanation:
Equilibrium constant
{tex}=\frac{\text { Rate constant of forward reaction }}{\text { Rate constant of backward reaction }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}=7.33{/tex}

topics.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question: The value of Kc for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
A mathematical expression of this thermodynamic view of equilibrium can be described by the following equation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 + RT ln Q
where G0 is standard Gibbs energy.
At equilibrium, when {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 and Q = Kc,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 + RT ln K = 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 = – RT lnK (7.22)
lnK = – {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 / RT
Taking antilog of both sides, we get,
K = e– {tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 / RT

questions.question: Which of the following options will be correct for the stage of half completion of the reaction A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B.
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
we know that  {tex}\Delta {G^0} = - RT\ln K{/tex}
for the stage of half completion of the reaction A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B.
Therefore K = 1
[A] = [B]
{tex}\Delta {G^0} = - RT\ln 1{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G^0} = 0{/tex}

questions.question: If in a mixture where Q = k is combined, then what happens?

options.option: nothing appears to happen, but forward and reverse are continuing at the same rate

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Q=K
The reaction is already at equilibrium. The concentrations won't change since the rates of the forward and backward reactions are equal.

questions.question: The incorrect match in the following is
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
The incorrect match is {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° < 0, K < 1
For an ideal gas {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = - RT ln K.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In {tex}K=-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{R T}{/tex} and {tex}K=e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex}
The above equation is helpful in predicting the spontaneity of the reaction. e.g.

  1. If {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}<0,-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T{/tex} = + ve and {tex}e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex} > 1 and hence, K > 1. It means that the reaction occurs spontaneously in the forward direction or products predominate over reactants.
  2. If {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}>0 ;-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T{/tex} = - ve and {tex}e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / R T}{/tex} <1 and hence, K < 1. It means that the reaction is non-spontaneous in the forward direction (i.e. product side) but spontaneous in reverse direction (i.e. reactants predominate over products or the reaction occurs rarely).
  3. When K = 1, then {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° - 0. This situation generally occurs at equilibrium

questions.question: In which one of the following equilibria, Kp {tex}\ne{/tex} KC?
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Consider the following equilibria reactions

  1. {tex}2 \mathrm{C}(s)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-(1)=1{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g} \neq 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{So}, K_{p} \neq K_{c}{/tex}
  2. {tex}2 \mathrm{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{I}_{2}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \text { So, } K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{SO}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}(g)+\mathrm{SO}_{3}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \text { So, } K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}
  4. {tex}2 \mathrm{NO}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=n_{\text {product }}-n_{\text {reactant }}=2-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta n_{g}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{So}, K_{p}=K_{c}{/tex}

questions.question: The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction, {tex}2 A \rightleftharpoons B+C{/tex} is 2494. 2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is {tex}[A]=\frac{1}{2},[B]=2 \text { and }[C]=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. The reaction proceeds in the (R = 8.314 JK/mol, e = 2.718)

options.option: forward direction because Q > K_ c

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=2494.2 \mathrm{J}{/tex}
{tex}Q=\frac{[B][C]}{[A]^{2}}=\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} we know, {tex}\Delta G=\Delta G^{\circ}+R T{/tex} In Q
= 2494.2 + 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex}300 In 4
= 28747.27 J(+ve value)
Also, we have {tex}\Delta G=R T \ln \frac{Q}{K}{/tex}
If {tex}\Delta G{/tex} is positive, Q > K
Therefore, reaction shifts in reverse direction.

questions.question: When two reactants, A and B are mixed to give products, C and D, the reaction quotient, (Q) at the initial stages of the reaction:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left.A+B \rightleftharpoons C+D, Q=\frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]}\right]{/tex}
As time passes, amount of products ‘C’ and 'D’ increases, hence Q increases.

questions.question: For the equilibrium, {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go at 298 K is approximately
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
We know that,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -2.303 RT log K
Also, given equilibrium is
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
[H+][OH-] = 10-14 or K = 10-14 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = {tex}-2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{K} \times \log 10^{-14}{/tex}
= {tex}79881.8 \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}=79.8 \mathrm{kJ} \approx 80 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question:
For the gas phase reaction
{tex}{C_2}{H_4} + {H_2} \rightleftharpoons {C_2}{H_6};[\Delta H =  - 327kcal]{/tex}
Carried out in a vessel, the equilibrium concentration of {tex}{C_2}{H_4}{/tex} can be increased by
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}C_2H_4+H_2⇔C_2H_6 , ΔH=−32.7 kcal{/tex}
{tex}Δn=P_{mole}−R_{mole}=1−2=−1 {/tex}
{tex}ΔH=−ve \ exothermic \ reaction ↓ x∝\frac{1}{T}↑{/tex}
The temperature increase reaction goes toward the reactant. Thus, the concentration of C2H4 increases.

questions.question:
Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-; Ka1 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{HS}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-; Ka2 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HS}]^{-}}{/tex}
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-; Ka3 = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka3 = Ka1 {tex}\times{/tex} Ka2

questions.question: For the reaction H2(g) + I2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI(g), the standard Gibbs energy is {tex}\Delta G^{\ominus}>0{/tex}. The equilibrium constant (K) would be:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T{/tex} ln Kp
if {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=+v e{/tex}, then Kp < 1

questions.question: For the gaseous phase reaction, 2NO (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} N2 (g) + O2 (g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 43.5 kcal mol-1, which statement is correct for, N2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO (g)?

options.option: K varies with addition of NO

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}2.303 \log \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R} \frac{\left[T_{2}-T_{1}\right]}{T_{1} T_{2}}{/tex};
{tex}\Delta H{/tex} = +ve for the reaction, N2(g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO(g)

questions.question: At equilibrium, if Kp = 1, then:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.303RT log Kp,
Thus if {tex}\Delta G^{\circ} = 0{/tex}, log Kp = 0 or Kp = 1

questions.question: van‘t Hoff equation giving the effect of temperature on chemical equilibrium is represented as:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
The variation of equilibrium constant with temperature is given by van’t Hoff isochore as:
{tex}\frac{d}{d T} \ln K_{p}=\frac{\Delta H}{R T^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: Densities of diamond and graphite are 3.5 and 2.3 g/mL respectively. Increase of pressure on the equilibrium C(diamond) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} C(graphite):
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle is not applicable to solid-solid heterogeneous equilibrium.

questions.question: The equilibrium constants for the reaction, Br2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2Br at 500K and 700 K are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively. The reaction is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}2.303 \log \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}=\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} K increases with temperature and thus {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = +ve

questions.question: For a system in equilibrium, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0 under conditions of constant:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: {tex}(\Delta G)_{\mathrm{T.P.}}=0{/tex}, at equilibrium state.

questions.question:
If {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go for the reaction given below is 1.7 kJ; the equilibrium constant of the reaction.
2HI(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H2(g) + I2(g) + I2(g) at 25oC is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = 2.303 RT log Kp;
{tex}1.7=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 298 \log K_{p}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kp = 0.5

questions.question:
The following reaction is performed at 298 K.
2NO (g) + O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G_{f}^{\circ}=2 \times G^{\circ} \mathrm{NO}_{2}-\left[2 \times G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}+G_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=2 G_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\circ}-2 G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\left[G_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\circ}=0\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad G_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} \Delta G_{f}^{\circ}+G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[-R T \ln K_{p}\right]+G_{\mathrm{NO}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[-R \times 298 \ln \left(1.6 \times 10^{12}\right)\right]+86600{/tex}
{tex}=0.5\left[-R \times 298 \ln 1.6 \times 10^{12}+2 \times 86600\right]{/tex}

questions.question:
The standard Gibbs energy change at 300 K for the reaction 2A {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} B+ C is 2494.2 J. At a given time, the composition of the reaction mixture is [A] {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, [B] = 2 and [C] {tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. The reaction proceeds in the:
[R = 8.314 J/K/mol]
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}Q=\frac{[B][C]}{[A]^{2}}=\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.303 RT log KC
2494.2 {tex}=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \log K_{c}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{c}=0.36{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Q > Kc thus backward reaction

questions.question: The reaction quotient (Q) for the reaction N2(g) + 3H2(g); {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g) is given by {tex}Q=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}. The reaction will proceed from right to left if:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: Q should decrease to became {tex}\leq{/tex} Kc

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct for a reversible process in a state of equilibrium?
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G = \Delta G^o{/tex} + 2.30 RT log Q
At equilibrium {tex}\Delta G = 0{/tex} and Q = K
{tex}\Delta G^o{/tex} = -2.30 RT log K

questions.question: When two reactants A and B are mixed to give products C and D, the reaction quotient, Q at the initial stages of reaction:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: At the initial stage if only reactants are taken Q = 0, but it goes increasing with time to become equal to Kc.

questions.question:
For the heterogenous equilibrium at 1 atm pressure:
CaCO3(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g), Kp = 1 atm then temperature is given by:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Kp = 1, {tex}\therefore \Delta G^{\circ}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ T=\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}}{/tex}

questions.question: The graph relates In Keq {tex}\text { vs. } \frac{1}{T}{/tex} for a reaction. The reaction must be:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\ln \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
K increase with {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} or decreases with T it is decided by {tex}\Delta H{/tex}. Here, K decrease with T
Thus, {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = -ve

questions.question:
Hydrolysis of sucrose is given by the following reaction. Sucrose + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Glucose + Fructose
If the equilibrium constant (Kc) is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013 at 300 K, the value of {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}{/tex} at the same temperature will be:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
The relation between Gibbs energy and equilibrium constant is given by,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G0 = -RT InKc
= -8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 {tex}\times{/tex} In(2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1013)

questions.question:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture, it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
{tex}\underset{Pink}{[Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}}(aq)+4Cl^-(aq) \rightleftharpoons \underset{Blue}{[CoCl_4]^{2-}}(aq)+6H_2O(l){/tex}

options.option: The sign of $\Delta$H cannot be predicted on the basis of this information

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H > 0 for the reaction
For the given reaction -
{tex}[Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}\ _{(aq)}+4Cl^-\ _{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons [CoCl_4]^{2-}\ _{(aq)}+6H_2O_{(l)}{/tex}
Since the reaction shifts to backward direction on cooling, this means that the backward reaction is exothermic reaction. Therefore, the forward reaction is endothermic reaction and hence {tex}\Delta{/tex}H > 0.

questions.question: For an endothermic reaction where, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H represents the enthalpy of the reaction in kJ/mol, the minimum value for energy of activation will be:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Activation energy: Ea​ is the energy that must be possessed by the molecules in excess to the average energy at a given temperature to enter a chemical reaction.
Relation between activation energy and enthalpy of a reversible reaction:
If the reaction is endothermic in forward direction, then Ea(backward)​ = Ea(forward)​ + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
If the reaction is exothermic in forward direction then Ea(backward​ = Ea(forward) ​+ {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
For an endothermic reaction, where {tex}\Delta{/tex}H represents the enthalpy of the reaction in kJ mol−1, the minimum value for the energy of activation will be slightly more than {tex}\Delta{/tex}H.

questions.question:
For the equilibrium, PCl5 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3 + Cl2,
Kc = {tex}\frac {\alpha^2}{(1 - \alpha) F}{/tex}, temperature remaining constant:

options.option: K_c will not change with the change in volume
K_c may increase or decrease with the change in volume depending upon its numerical value
K_c will increase with the decrease in volume
K_c will increase with the increase in volume

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: The magnitude of the equilibrium constant is not affected by the changes in concentrations of reactants and products, pressure and volume. Thus, when volume is changed, the value of the equilibrium constant Kc​ is unaffected.

questions.question: The equilibrium constant for a reaction is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 at 300 K. The standard Gibbs energy change for this reaction is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question: The equilibrium constant of a reaction is 20.0. At equilibrium, the rate constant of forward reaction is 10.0. The rate constant for backward reaction is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{c}=\frac{K_{f}}{K_{b}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ 20=\frac{10}{K_{b}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ K_{b}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5

questions.question:
Choose the incorrect statement.

  1. Kc = {tex}e^{\frac {-\Delta G^\circ}{RT}}{/tex}
  2. More negative is {tex}\Delta{/tex}G°, larger is equilibrium constant
  3. In complex formation equilibrium constant refers for formation constant of complex
  4. Any equilibrium is influenced by increase in pressure
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: Any equilibrium is influenced by increase in pressure.

questions.question: The reaction quotient (Q) predicts:

options.option: the ratio of activities at equilibrium, i.e., K_c
the ratio of activities at any time
the direction of equilibrium to be attained

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: all of these

questions.question: The value of {tex}\Delta U^\circ{/tex} for the reaction 2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A2B(g) for which Kp = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 atm-2 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}S° = 5 JK-1 and T = 300 K, is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question:
The following reaction is performed at 298 K.
2NO(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g)
The standard free energy of formation of NO(g) is 86.6 kJ/mol at 298 K. What is the standard free energy of formation of NO2(g) at 298 K? (Kp = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012)
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
The relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change for reaction and the equilibrium constant is as shown below.
{tex}\Delta G_{rxn}^0{/tex} ​= −RT ln Kp ...(i)
The standard Gibbs free energy change for the reaction is given by the expression
{tex}\Delta G_{rxn}^0{/tex} ​= {tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}​(NO2​) − {tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}​(NO) ...(ii)
From equations (i) and (ii)
{tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}​(NO2​) − ​{tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}(NO) = −RT ln Kp
{tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}(NO2​) = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} [{tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}​(NO) − RT ln Kp​]
Substitute values in the above expression.
{tex}2 \Delta G_{f}^{0}{/tex}​(NO2​) = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 86600 − R(298) ln1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012] J/mol

questions.question: For the reaction: N2 + O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO, standard Gibb’s energy change is given by {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° = 22000 - 2.5 T, (in cal) what is the value of Kp at 2000 K.
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question: The equilibrium constant Kp for the reaction: N2O4(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g) at 27°C and 1 atm is 0.141 atm. What would be the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}G (in cal) for the production of NO2 gas when partial pressures of NO2 and N2O4 are 1 and 10 atm respectively at 27°C:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question: The activation energy for the forward and backward reactions are 10.303 and 8.0 k cal respectively at 500 K. Assuming pre-exponential factor same for both forward and reverse reaction are same and R = 2 cal, the equilibrium constant for the reaction A {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} B is:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G

questions.question:
The variation of equilibrium constant with temperature is given below:

Temperature Equilibrium Constant
T1 = 25 oC K1 = 10
T2 = 100 oC K2 = 100

The values of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex}, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} at T1 and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} at T2 (in kj mol-1) respectively, are close to [use R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1]
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} = -RT In K, T1 = 25°C, K1 = 10
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} at T1 = −8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} log10 = −5.71 kJ/mol
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} at T2 = -8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}\times{/tex} 373 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} log(100)
= -14.29 kJ/mol
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ}{/tex}  
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -5.71 = {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-298\left(\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -14.29 = {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-373\left(\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}{/tex} = 28.4 kJ/mol

questions.question: For the equilibrium 2H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H3O+ + OH-; the value of {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° at 298 K is approximately:
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G=\Delta G^{\circ}+R T \ln Q{/tex}
At equilibrium; {tex}\Delta G{/tex} = 0 and {tex}Q=K_{\mathrm{eq}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = 2.303 RT log Kw
= -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}\times{/tex} log10-14
= 79.9 kJ/mol {tex}\approx{/tex} 80 kJ/mol

questions.question: For the reaction A(g) + B(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C(g) + D(g), Kp = 1.61 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 and Kc = 49. At what temperature is the reaction being carried out?
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
Kp = 1.61 {tex}\times{/tex} 103; Kc = 49; {tex}\triangle{/tex}n = 1
Kp = Kc(RT){tex}\triangle n{/tex}
1.61 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 = 49(0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} T)1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} T = 400 K
T = 127oC

questions.question: For the following reactions at equilibrium, at 298 K, identify the reaction with the largest {tex}\frac{K_p}{K_c}{/tex} ratio.
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
1. {tex}\frac{{K}_{{p}}}{{K}_{{c}}}{/tex} = (RT)-1
  2. {tex}\frac{{K}_{{p}}}{{K}_{{c}}}{/tex} = (RT)-1
  3. {tex}\frac{{K}_{{p}}}{{K}_{{c}}}{/tex} = (RT)0
  4. {tex}\frac{{K}_{{p}}}{{K}_{{c}}}{/tex} = (RT)1

questions.question: For the reaction 2HI {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2 + I2, if the standard free energy {tex}\triangle{/tex}Go < 0, the equilibrium constant Kc would be ________.
questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Go and are related as
{tex}\triangle{/tex}Go = -RT lnKc
Given: {tex}\triangle{/tex}Go < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kc should be positive, i.e., Kc > 1

questions.question:
Match the following:

Column I Column II
i. Reaction favours formation of more products a. Q < K
ii. Reaction is at equilibrium b. Q > K
iii. Reaction favours formation of more reactants c. Q = K

options.option: i - b, ii - c, iii - a
i - b, ii- a, iii - c
i - c, ii - a, iii - b
i - a, ii - c, iii - b

questions.topicName: Relationship Btw Equilibrium Constant K, Reaction Quotient Q and Gibbs Energy G
questions.explanation: i - a, ii - c, iii - b

topics.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle

questions.question: In order to decide what course the reaction adopts and make a qualitative prediction about the effect of a change in conditions on equilibrium we use:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle- It states that a change in any of the factors that determine the equilibrium conditions of a system will cause the system to change in such a manner so as to reduce or to counteract the effect of the change.

questions.question: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:

options.option: lowering the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture
increasing the activation energy for the forward reaction
making available a new low energy pathway

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction by making available a new low energy pathway for the conversion of reactants to products. It increases the rate of forward and reverse reactions that pass through the same transition state and does not affect equilibrium. Catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by exactly the same amount. Catalyst does not affect the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture. It does not appear in the balanced chemical equation or in the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
{tex}\mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{Co }}\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right){\text{6}}} \right]}^{{\text{3}} + }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }(aq)}\limits_{pink}{/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]}^{{\text{2}} - }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}(l)}\limits_{Blue} {/tex}

options.option: The sign of $\Delta $H cannot be predicted on the basis of this information.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
For an endothermic reaction - If temperature is decreased reaction will shift to backward direction
{tex}\mathop {{{[Co{{({H_2}O)}_6}]}^{3 + }}(aq)}\limits_{pink} + \mathop {4C{l^ - }(aq)}\limits_{colourless} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex} \mathop {{{\left[ {{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]}^{{\text{2}} - }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}(l)}\limits_{Blue} {/tex}
At room temperature, the equilibrium mixture is blue due to [CoCl 4 ]2–. When cooled in a freezing mixture, the colour of the mixture turns pink due to [Co(H2O)6 ]3+.

questions.question: On increasing the pressure, in which direction will the gas phase reaction proceed to re-establish equilibrium is predicted by applying the Le Chatelier’s principle. Consider the reaction. {tex}{N_2}(g) + 3{H_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3}{/tex} (g) Which of the following is correct, if the total pressure at which the equilibrium is established, is increased without changing the temperature?

options.option: K will increase initially and decrease when pressure is very high

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
In the reaction {tex}Na(g) + 3{H_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3}(g){/tex}

According to the the Le Chatelier’s principle, at constant temperture, the equilibrium constant will change but K will remain same.

questions.question: In which of the following reactions, the equilibrium remains unaffected on addition of small amount of argon at constant volume?

options.option: The equilibrium will remain unaffected in all the three cases.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
For addition of inert gas at constant pressure, the reaction having same number of moles in reactant and product side will not go any change in the position of equilibrium.
H2 (g) + I2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI (g)
The equilibrium constant will be affected on addition of inert gas in all the three cases.

questions.question:
Following data is given for the reaction: {tex}{\text{CaC}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{ CaO }}\left( {\text{s}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV|CaO(s)] = - - 635.1\;kJ1mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[C{O_2}(g)] = - - 393.5\;kJ\;mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{\Delta _f}HV[CaC{O_3}(s)] = - - 1206.9\;kJmo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Predict the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant of the above
Reaction (Hint: Apply Le-Chatelier’s principle)

options.option: increase in temperature reaction will stop.
increase in temperature reaction will proceed in forward direction
increase in temperature reaction will proceed in reverse direction
increase in temperature reaction will attain equilibrium

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}{\Delta _{rxn}}H = (\Delta {H_{product}} - \Delta {H_{reac\tan t}}){/tex}
= -393.5 - 635.1 + 1206.9 = +ve
This is endothermic reaction, which is favoured at high temperature.

questions.question: Does the number of moles of reaction products increase, decrease or remain same when each of the following equilibria is subjected to a decrease in pressure by increasing the volume? {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}{\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){/tex}
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
1. Pressure will increase in the forwarded reaction and the number of moles of the products increase.
  2. Pressure will increase in the backward reaction and the number of moles of the products decrease.
  3. The change in pressure will have no effect on the equilibrium constant and there will be no change in the no. of moles.

questions.question: Hydrogen molecule (H2) can be dissociated into hydrogen atoms (H). Which one of the following changes will not increase the number of atoms present at equilibrium?

options.option: increasing the volume of the container

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for dissociation of H2 into H atoms is as follows:
H2 {tex}⇌{/tex}H+H.
Since a number of atoms on the reactant side and product side are the same, therefore, change in pressure has no effect on the position of equilibrium.

questions.question: A catalyst will increase the rate of a chemical reaction by:

options.option: shifting the equilibrium to the right
shifting the equilibrium to the left

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Catalysts increase the rate of the reaction without being used up. They do this by lowering the activation energy needed. With a catalyst, more collisions result in a reaction, so the rate of reaction increases.
"A catalyst provides an alternative route for the reaction with lower activation energy."

questions.question: H2S+H2+S energy In this reversible reaction, select the factor which will shift the equilibrium to the right.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
adding the heat will shift the equilibrium to the right.
H2S {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2+S

questions.question: What effect does a catalyst have on the equilibrium position of a reaction?

options.option: a catalyst does not change the equilibrium position of a reaction
a catalyst may favour reactants or product formation, depending upon the direction in which the reaction is written
a catalyst favours the formation of reactants
a catalyst favours the formation of products

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Catalyst provides a low activation energy path to complete the reaction. This affects reactions in forward and backward direction equally. Thus, Catalysts have no effect on the position of equilibrium.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2SO2 (g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}2SO3 (g), {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 57.2 kJ mol-1 and Kc = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016. Which of the following statement is incorrect?

options.option: The equilibrium will shift in a forward direction as the pressure increases.
The addition of inert gas at constant volume will not affect the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant is large suggestive of reaction going to completion and so no catalyst is required.
The equilibrium constant decreases as the temperature increases.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
The explanation of the given statements are as follows:

  1. For the given equilibrium, AH is negative, so the equilibrium constant will decrease with increase in temperature and the equilibrium will shift in the backward direction.
  2. When inert gas is added at constant volume and constant temperature, an equilibrium remains undisturbed. 
  3. For the equilibrium,
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 2 - (2 + 1) = -1, i.e. (-ve)
    So, increase in pressure will shift the equilibrium in the forward direction.
  4. The reaction takes place in the presence of a catalyst which is V2O5(s) in the contact process or NO(g) in the chamber process.

questions.question:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} .Which is a correct statement if N2 is added at equilibrium condition?

  1. The equilibrium will shift to forward direction because according to Ilnd law of thermodynamics, the entropy must increase in the direction of spontaneous reaction.
  2. The condition for equilibrium is G(N2) + 3G(H2) = 2G(NH3) where, G is Gibbs free energy per mole of the gaseous species measured at that partial pressure. The condition of equilibrium is unaffected by the use of catalyst, which increases the rate of both the forward and backward reactions to the same extent.
  3. The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by {tex}\alpha{/tex} and that of backward reaction by {tex}\beta{/tex}.
  4. Catalyst will not alter the rate of either of the reaction.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = 0
G(reactants) = G(products)
G(N2) + 3G(H2) = 2G(NH3)
A catalyst does not affect either equilibrium composition or equilibrium constant, it just increases rate of both forward and backward reaction but by the same factor.

questions.question:
For the chemical reaction,
{tex}3 X(g)+Y(g) \rightleftharpoons X_{3} Y(g){/tex}
the amount of X3Y at equilibrium is affected by
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The given reaction will be exothermic in nature due to the formation of three X - Y bonds from the gaseous atoms. The reaction is also accompanied with the decrease in the gaseous species (i.e. {tex}\Delta n{/tex} is negative). Hence, the reaction will be affected by both temperature and pressure. The use of catalyst does not affect the equilibrium concentrations of the species in the chemical reaction.

questions.question:
For the reaction,
 {tex}\mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2}(g){/tex} at a given temperature, the equilibrium amount of CO2 (g) can be increased by

options.option: Decreasing the volume of the container

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
CO(g) + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CO2(g) + H2(g)
KC = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]}{[\mathrm{CO}]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
A catalyst simply helps in attaining the equilibrium earlier. Addition of inert gas has no effect on a reaction because in it, {tex}\Delta n{/tex} = 0.
This equilibrium is not based upon volume because in it,  {tex}\Delta n{/tex} = 0.
On increasing the amount of CO, KC should decrease but it is constant at constant temperature, so for maintaining the constant value of KC, the amount of CO2 increases.

questions.question: For the reaction, H2 (g) + I2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2HI(g) the equilibrium constant Kp changes with

options.option: The amount of H_2 and I_2 present

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Equilibrium constant of a given reversible reaction depends only on temperature.

questions.question: In {tex}{N_2} + 3{H_2} \to 2N{H_3}{/tex} reversible reaction, increase in pressure will favour
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: According to Le-Chatelier's principle, an increase in pressure will favor the direction where there is lesser number of species.

questions.question: Which of the following information can be obtained on the basis of Le Chatelier’s principle?

options.option: Shift in equilibrium position on changing value of a variable
Equilibrium constant of a chemical reaction
Dissociation constant of a weak acid

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: According to Le Chatelier's principle, if an equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, pressure or temperature, etc. equilibrium shifts in such a way so as to undo the effect of a change imposed.

questions.question:
Of the following which change will shift the reaction towards the product?
{tex}{I_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2I\left( g \right),{\text{ }}\Delta {H_r}^\circ \left( {298{\text{ }}K} \right) =  + 150{\text{ }}kJ{/tex}
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
The reaction is {tex}I_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 I^{-}(g), \Delta H_{r}^{\circ}(298 K)=+150 k J{/tex}
According to Le-Chatelier's principle, the conditions favouring the formation of I are given below:

  1. The high concentration of I2​(g).
  2. High temperature (reaction is endothermic so, on increasing the temperature equilibrium it will shift in the direction where heat is absorbed).
  3. Low pressure (moles of products are more than moles of reactant. Therefore, if pressure is increased, equilibrium will shift in the direction where moles are decreasing).

questions.question:
For the chemical equilibrium,
{tex}CaC{O_3}(g) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + C{O_2}(g),{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {H_r}^\circ {/tex} can be determined from which one of the following plots?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
For the reaction, {tex}\operatorname{CacO} 3(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO} 2(\mathrm{g}){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{Kp}=P_{C O_{2}} \text { and } \mathrm{KC}=\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]{/tex} ([CaCO3] = 1 and [CaO] = 1 for solids]
According to Arrhenius equation we have  
{tex}K=A e^{-\Delta H^{\circ} r / R T}{/tex}
Taking logarithm, we have   
{tex}\log K_{p}=\log A-\frac{\Delta H_{r}^{o}}{R T(2.303)}{/tex}
 This is an equation of a straight line. When log Kp is plotted against 1 / T. we get a straight line.

The intercept of this line {tex}=\log A, \text { slope }=? \Delta H^{\circ} \mathrm{r} / 2.303 \mathrm{R}{/tex}
Knowing the value of slope from the plot and universal gas constant R, ∆Hr° can be calculated.
(Equation of straight line : Y = m.x + C). Here, 
{tex}\underset Y{\log K_p}=-\underset m{\frac{\Delta H_r^o}{2.303R}\;}\underset X{\left(\frac1T\right)}+\underset C{\log A}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is incorrect?

  1. In equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask mass of ice and water does not change with time.
  2. The intensity of red colour increases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron(III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
  3. On the addition of catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is not affected.
  4. The equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative {tex}\Delta{/tex}H value decreases as the temperature increases
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}F{e^{ + 3}} + SCN^- \rightleftharpoons {[Fe\mathop {(CNS)}\limits_{red} ]^2}^ + {/tex}
Addition of oxalic acid shows redox change with Fe3+ to convert them into Fe2+ and thus backward reaction predominates and thus the intensity of red colour decreases.

questions.question:
When hydrochloric acid is added to cobalt nitrate solution at room temperature, the following reaction takes place and the reaction mixture becomes blue. On cooling the mixture it becomes pink. On the basis of this information mark the correct answer.
{tex}{[Co{(\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(pink)} )_6}]^{2 + }}(aq) + 4C{l^ - }(aq){/tex}{tex} \rightleftharpoons {[\mathop {CoC{l_4}}\limits_{(blue)} ]^{2 - }}(aq) + 6{H_2}O(l){/tex}

options.option: The sign of $\Delta$H cannot be predicted on the basis of this information

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}{[Co{(\mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{(pink)} )_6}]^{3 + }}(aq.) + 4C{l^ - }(aq.){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {[\mathop {CoC{l_4}}\limits_{(blue)} ]^{2 - }}(aq.) + 6{H_2}O{/tex}
On cooling mixture becomes pink means backward reaction predominates i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is -ve for backward reaction. Thus {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is +ve for the forward reaction.

questions.question:
On increasing the pressure, in which direction will the gas-phase reaction proceed to re-establish equilibrium, is predicted by applying Le Chatelier’s principle. Consider the reaction
N2(g) + 3H2( g ) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g)
Which of the following is correct, if the total pressure at which the equilibrium is established, is increased without changing the temperature?

options.option: K will increase initially and decrease when pressure is very high

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: K remains constant at a constant temperature.

questions.question:
Favourable conditions for manufacture of ammonia by the reaction,
N2 + 3H2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -21.9 kcal are:

options.option: low temperature, low pressure and catalyst
low temperature, high pressure and catalyst
high temperature, low pressure and catalyst
high temperature, high pressure and catalyst

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: low temperature, high pressure and catalyst

questions.question: Raising the temperature of a reversible chemical reaction:

options.option: favours neither the forward nor backward rates
favours both the forward and backward rates whatever the case may be

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The rate of reaction always increases with temperature K = Ae-Ea/RT and rate = K {tex}\times{/tex} C.

questions.question:

From this, it may be deduced that:

options.option: The forward reaction gives out heat

There are more molecules on the right hand side of the chemical equation than on the left
The reaction proceeds ten times faster at 450 K than 400 K

questions.explanation:
{tex}2.303 \log \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: For the reaction, A+B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 3C at 25°C, a 3 litre vessel contains 1, 2, 4 moles of A, B and C respectively. If Kc for the reaction is 10, the reaction will proceed in:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Quotient Q {tex}=\frac{[C]^{3}}{[A][B]}=\frac{4^{3} \times 3 \times 3}{3^{3} \times 1 \times 2}{/tex} = 10.66
{tex}\left[[C]=\frac{4}{3} ;[A]=\frac{1}{3},[B]=\frac{2}{3}\right]{/tex}
Since Kc = 10, Thus Q must decrease to attain value of Kc and therefore [C] must decrease or [A] or [B] must increase i.e., backward direction.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_{Mole} }\limits_{At.\;eq.\;(a - x)} ightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_3}(g)}\limits_{} }\limits_x + \mathop {\mathop {C{l_2}(g)}\limits_{} }\limits_x {/tex}
Now, {tex}K_{c}= rac{x^{2}}{V(a-x)}{/tex}
Addition of inert gas at constant V has no effect on reactions having {tex}\Delta n=0{/tex} or {tex}\Delta n eq 0{/tex}. But addition of inert gas at constant P has effect on reactions having {tex}\Delta n eq 0{/tex}; and no effect, if {tex}\Delta n=0{/tex}. The given reaction has {tex}\Delta n eq 0{/tex} and thus, only choice (c) is correct. Also the effect may be drawn as: On addition of inert gas at constant P, volume increases. To have Kc constant, X must increase.

Which of the following is correct?

options.option: The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by 2 times and that of backward reaction by 1.5 times
he catalyst will decrease the rate of forward reaction by 5 times and that of backward reaction by 3.5 times
The condition for equilibrium is $G_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+3 G_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=2 G_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$, where G is Gibbs energy per mole of gaseous species measured at that partial pressure
On adding N_2, the equilibrium will shift to forward direction because according to II law of thermodynamics the entropy must decrease in the direction of spontaneous reaction

questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta G = 0{/tex}; at equilibrium

questions.question: The activation energies of forward and backward reaction: A2 + B2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2AB are 180 kJ mol-1 and 200 kJ mol-1 respectively. The presence of a catalyst lowers the activation energy of both (forward and backward) reactions by 100 kJ mol-1. The enthalpy change of the reaction in the presence of catalyst will be (in kJ mol-1)
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta H=E_{a_{(f)}}-E_{a_{(b)}}=180-200{/tex}
= -20 kJ mol-1 (in absence of catalyst)
{tex}\therefore \Delta H=E_{a_{(f)}}-E_{a_{(b)}}{/tex}
= [180 - 100] - [200 - 100]
= -20 kJ mol-1 (in presence of catalyst)
Catalyst's have no influence on {tex}\Delta{/tex}H

will increase the mole of Cl2 or SO2 to maintain Kc values constant.

questions.question: Reaction BaO2 (s) BaO(s) + O2(g); {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = +ve. In equilibrium condition, pressure of O2 depends on:

options.option: increase mass of BaO_2 and BaO both

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}BaO _2( s ) \rightleftharpoons BaO ( s )+ O _2( g ){/tex}
At equilibrium, {tex}K =\frac{[ BaO ]\left[ O _2\right]}{\left[ BaO _2\right]}{/tex}
BaO2 and BaO are in solid state. Thus, their concentration is constant and does not change. Hence, equilibrium constant is independent of these two concentration terms.
K = [O2]
Thus, increased mass of BaO2 or BaO does not have any effect on equilibrium state of the reaction. Now, as the reaction is endothermic (as {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0) then increase in temperature will favour the forward direction of equilibrium and hence more O2 is formed and higher will be the pressure of O2,

questions.question:
For the reaction,
CH4(g) + 2O2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O(l); {tex}\Delta_{r} H{/tex} = -170.8 KJ mol-1
Which of the following statements is not true?

options.option: At equilibrium, the concentrations of CO_2(g) and H_2O_(l) are not equal
Addition of CH_4(g) or O_2(g) at equilibrium will cause a shift to the right
The equilibrium constant for the reaction is given by: $K_{p}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{4}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}$

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
The products CO2 and H2O have different stoichiometric coefficients and they are at different physical states thus, they have different concentration at equilibrium.
For the given reaction, equilibrium constant
{tex}K _{ C }=\frac{\left[ CO _2\right]}{\left[ CH _4\right]\left[ O _2\right]^2}{/tex}
Now, as we know, Kp = Kc {tex}\times{/tex} (RT)
{tex}\Delta n_g =n_{\text {products }}-n_{\text {reactants }}{/tex}
{n = number of gaseous moles}
= 1 - (1 + 2)
= -2
{tex}K _{ P } = K _{ C }( RT )^{-2} \frac{\left[ CO _2\right]}{\left[ CH _4\right]\left[ O _2\right]} \times \frac{1}{( RT )^2}{/tex}
Thus, statement (b) is not true.
On addition of CH4 or O2, i.e., reactants will increase the concentration of reactants and according to Le-Chatelier's principle the equilibrium shifts to the right.
As change in enthalpy of the reactions is negative thus, the reaction is exothermic. Hence, the statements (a), (c) and (d) are true while (b) is not true.

questions.question: If the concentration of OH- ions in the reaction, {tex}Fe ( OH )_{3(s)} \rightleftharpoons Fe ^{3+}{ }_{(a q)}+3 OH _{(a q)}^{-}{/tex} is decreased by {tex}\frac 14{/tex} times, then equilibrium concentration of Fe3+ will increase by:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Kc = [Fe3+][OH-]3
if {tex}\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\frac{1}{4} \times\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]_{\text {initial }}{/tex}, then [Fe3+] = 64[Fe3+]initiaI to have constant.

questions.question: The value of equilibrium constant of the reaction, {tex}\mathrm{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}(g){/tex} is 8.0. The equilibrium constant of the reaction H2(g) + I2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI(g) will be:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
For {tex}\mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2} ; K_{c_{1}}=8{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For H2 + I2 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2HI; {tex}K_{c_{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{K_{c_{1}}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{64}{/tex}

questions.question:
The value of {tex}\Delta H{/tex} for the reaction
X2(g) + 4Y2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2XY4(g) is less than zero
Formation of XY4(g) will be favoured at:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: As {tex}\Delta{/tex}H < 0 i.e., the given reaction is exothermic. According to Le-Chatelier principle, for exothermic reaction, forward reaction is favoured when temperature becomes low. Also, there are 5 gaseous moles on reactant side and 2 gaseous moles on products side. So, forward reaction is favoured when pressure of the reaction mixture becomes high. The reason is that at high pressure reaction tends to more in direction where there is lessee number of gaseous moles.

questions.question:
KMnO4 can be prepared from K2MnO4 as per the reaction,
{tex}3 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{OH}^-{/tex}.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:

CO2 + 4OH- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2CO3 + H2O

questions.question:
For the reversible reaction:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g) + heat
The equilibrium shifts in forward direction:

options.option: by increasing the concentration of NH_3(g)
by decreasing the concentrations of N_2 (g) and H_2(g)
by increasing pressure and decreasing temperature

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
According to Le-Chatelier's principle, the equilibrium shifts in that direction so as to oppose the applied change.
If concentration of NH3 increases then equilibrium shifts in backward direction to oppose the increased concentration of NH3.
If pressure is decreased then equilibrium shifts to the direction in which there are more number of gaseous moles are present. Thus, it also shifts in backward direction as there are 4 gaseous moles in reactant side and 2 moles in products side. If concentration of N2(g) and H2(g) decreases then equilibrium shifts to backward direction to increase the concentration of reactants. Ifpressure isincreased, equilibrium shifts to the side where lower number of gaseous moles are present i.e., forward direction. Also, when temperature is decreased i.e., the heat evolved in the reaction is consumed then reaction proceeds towards forward direction.

{tex}A_{2}(g)+B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons X_{2}(g);{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} H=-X \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}

On decreasing temperature equilibrium shifts in exothermic direction i.e., forward direction.
Thus, high pressure and low temperature favours maximum formation of product.

{tex}K_{c}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right]}{/tex}
In (iii) {tex}Q=\frac{(0.1)^{2}}{0.1}=0.1{/tex}; Q < KC
