An aqueous solution of hydrogen sulphide shows the equilibrium,
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS-
If dilute hydrochloric acid is added to an aqueous solution of hydrogen sulphide without any change in temperature, then:

options.option: the concentration of HS^- will decrease
the concentration of HS^- will increase
the concentration of undissociated hydrogen sulphide will decrease

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Reaction: H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS-
As we know, for this reaction
Ka for H2S = {tex}\frac{\left[{H}^{+}\right]\left[{H} {S}^{-}\right]}{\left[{H}_{2} {S}\right]}{/tex}
An increase in [H+] will show a decrease in [HS-] to maintain constant Ka Value.

questions.question: Which equilibrium in gaseous phase would be unaffected by an increase in pressure?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
N2 + O2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO
would be unaffected by an increase in pressure as pressure has no effect on equilibrium if {tex}\Delta{/tex}n = 0

questions.question:
Consider the reversible reaction,
HCN(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+(aq) + CN-(aq)
At equilibrium, the addition of CN-(aq) would:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: decrease the H+(aq) ion concentration

questions.question: Which can be explained as applications of Le Chatelier’s principle?

options.option: Transport of oxygen by haemoglobin in blood
Removal of CO_2 from tissues by blood
Tooth decay due to use of sweet substances

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: All of these

questions.question: Indicate the correct answer out of the following for the reaction, NH4Cl + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + HCl

options.option: the reaction is favoured by the addition of NH_4OH
the reaction is retarded by the addition of KOH
the reaction is retarded by the addition of hydrogen ion
the reaction is favoured by the addition of hydrogen ion

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: the reaction is retarded by the addition of hydrogen ion

questions.question: When NaNO3 is heated in a closed vessel, oxygen is liberated and NaNO2 is left behind. At equilibrium:

options.option: addition of NaNO_2 favours forward reaction
addition of NaNO_2 favours reverse reaction

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
The reaction proceeds as
2NaNO3 ​{tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NaNO2​ + O2
Changing the quantities of NaNO3​ and NaNO2​ does not affect the equilibrium because they are in a solid-state. only the pressure of oxygen gas can change the equilibrium. Since the reaction is endothermic hence increasing the temperature favours forward direction reaction.

questions.question: In lime kiln, the reversible reaction, CaCO3(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2(g) proceeds to completion because:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: In lime kilns, the following reversible reaction, CaCO3​(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CaO(s) + CO2​(g) proceeds to completion because of CO2​ escapes so that it is no longer available for the reaction with CaO.

questions.question: According to Le Chatelier’s principle adding heat to a solid and liquid in equilibrium with endothermic nature will cause the:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
According to Le Chatelier's principle adding heat to a solid and liquid in equilibrium with endothermic nature will cause the mass of solid to decrease.
Solid {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Liquid; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve;
Increase in temperature favours forward reaction. In endothermic reactions, ({tex}\Delta{/tex}H = +ve) heat is absorbed with the reactants. It means more energy is needed to overcome the forces of attraction between molecules and to separate them from one another than the energy gained when new bonds are formed.

questions.question: In which of the following equilibrium, change in volume of the system does not alter the number of mole?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: In the reaction N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g), the total number of moles of reactants is equal to the total number of moles of products. For such reactions the change in volume will not affect the number of moles.

questions.question: In a vessel containing SO3, SO2 and O2 at equilibrium, some helium gas is introduced so that the total pressure increases while temperature and volume remain constant. According to Le Chatelier’s principle the dissociation of SO3:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The volume is kept constant. Hence, the concentration of the gases does not change. The partial pressure of the gases in the reaction does not change, even though we have increased the total pressure by adding helium. Therefore, there is no change in equilibrium.

questions.question:
For the reaction, PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
The forward reaction at constant temperature is favoured by:

options.option: introducing an inert gas at increases pressure
introducing an inert gas at constant pressure
introducing an inert gas at constant volume
introducing chlorine gas at constant volume

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: If inert gas is introduced at constant pressure, volume of container will have to be increased and this will favour the forward reaction. Also adding PCI5 (g) at constant volume will favour forward reaction because PCI5 (g) is a reactant.

questions.question: A cylinder fitted with a movable piston contains liquid water in equilibrium with water vapour at 25°C. Which operation result in a decrease in the equilibrium vapour pressure?

options.option: Removing a small mass of vapour
Moving the piston downward a short distance
Removing a small mass of the liquid water

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Addition of salt always lowers the vapour pressure. Also, vapour pressure of a liquid is its characteristic pressure and depends only on temperature. Adding a solute lowers vapor pressure because the additional solute particles will fill the gaps between the solvent particles and take up space. This means less of the solvent will be on the surface and less will be able to break free to enter the gas phase, resulting in a lower vapor pressure.

questions.question:
The volume of the reaction vessel containing an equilibrium mixture in the reaction,
SO2Cl2(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex}SO2(g) + Cl2(g)
is increased. When equilibrium is restablished:

options.option: the mass of SO_2(g) will decrease
the mass of Cl_2(g) will increase
the mass of Cl_2(g) will remain unchanged
the mass of SO_2Cl_2(g) will increase

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: the mass of Cl2(g) will increase

questions.question: Densities of diamond and graphite are 3.5 and 2.3 g/mL respectively. Increase of pressure on the equilibrium C(diamond) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex}C(graphite):
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: have no effect

questions.question:
The reaction that proceeds in the forward direction is:

  1. SnCl4 + Hg2Cl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} SnCl2 + 2HgCl2
  2. NH4Cl + NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O + NH3 + NaCl
  3. Mn2+ + 2H2O + Cl2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} MnO2 + 4H+ + 2Cl-
  4. S4{tex}O_6^{2-}{/tex} + 2I- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2S2{tex}O_3^{2-}{/tex} + I2
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: NH4Cl + NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O + NH3 + NaCl

questions.question: Which information can be obtained from Le Chatelier’s principle?

options.option: Shift in equilibrium position on changing P, T and concentration
Dissociation constant of a weak acid
Equilibrium constant of a chemical reaction

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Le Chtelier's principle states that if a dynamic equilibrium is disturbed by changing the conditions, the position of equilibrium shifts to counteract the change to re-establish equilibrium. If a chemical reaction is at equilibrium and experiences a change in pressure, temperature, or concentration of products or reactants, the equilibrium shifts in the opposite direction to offset the change.

questions.question:
For, Cl2(g) + 3F2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2ClF3(g); {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = -329 kJ
Which of the following will increase the quantity of ClF3 in an equilibrium mixture of CI2, F2 and ClF3?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Addition of F2

questions.question:
The brown gas prepared by the action of concentrated nitric acid on copper is an equilibrium mixture of dinitrogen tetraoxide and nitrogen dioxide:
N2O4(g) {tex}\overset{endothermic}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = + ve
Which one of the following changes would result in a darkening of the colour?

options.option: Removal of dinitrogen tetraoxide by liquefaction

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
N2O4(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g); {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = + ve
Brown colour is formed due to NO2​ gas. Darkening of colour is possible when more NO2​ is formed. When temperature increases the equilibrium shifted to the right side and more NO2​ is formed since the reaction is endothermic.

questions.question: For the reaction, A + B {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 3C at 25°C, a 3 litre vessel contains 1, 2, 4 moles of A, B and C respectively. If Kc for the reaction is 10, the reaction will proceed in:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Quotient Q = {tex}\frac{[C]^{3}}{[A][B]}=\frac{4^{3} \ \times \ 3 \ \times \ 3}{3^{3} \ \times \ 1 \ \times \ 2}{/tex} = 10.66
{tex}\left[[C]=\frac{4}{3} ;[A]=\frac{1}{3},[B]=\frac{2}{3}\right]{/tex}
Since Kc = 10, Thus Q must decrease to attain value of Kc and therefore [C] must decrease or [A] or [B] must increase i.e., backward direction.

questions.question: In a system: A(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2B(g) + 3C(g). If the concentration of C at equilibrium is increased by a factor 2, it will cause the equilibrium concentration of B to change to:

options.option: one half of its original value

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
For A(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2B(g) + 3C(g)
Kc = [C]3[B]2; if [C] becomes 2C, cone, of will become then
Kc = [2C]3[B1]2
or [C]3[B]2 = 8[C]3[B1]2
or {tex}\frac{\left[B_{1}\right]}{[B]}=\frac{1}{8}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The colour of given reaction mixture is blue at room temperature. On {tex}\underset{\text{Pink}}{[Co(H_2O)_6]^{2+}(aq.)} + 4Cl^- \rightleftharpoons\underset{\text{Blue}}{ [CoCl_4^{2-}](aq.) + 6H_2O}{/tex} cooling it becames pink, this indicates that:

options.option: reaction shifts in forward direction on cooling
reaction shifts in Backward direction on cooling

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: reaction is endothermic

questions.question: Ca3(PO4)2 is insoluble in water. On adding a few drops of HCl to solid Ca3 (PO4)2 in contact with water, the solid dissolves. The reason is:

options.option: Ca_3(PO_4)_2 combines with HCl to form soluble CaCl_2
H_3PO_4, a weak acid is formed and the solubility product of Ca_3(PO_4)_2 decreases
the solvent becomes more polar on adding HCl

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Ca3 (PO4)2 combines with HCl to form soluble CaCl2

questions.question:
For the given reaction step I is endothermic and step II is exothermic such that overall reaction is exothermic.
A2(g) + B2(g) {tex}\overset {K_1}{\underset {\underset {Step \ I}{K_2}} \rightleftharpoons} 3C(g) \overset {K_3}{\underset {\underset {Step \ II}{K_4}} \rightleftharpoons}{/tex} D(g)
Which of the following statement is not true?

  1. Step I is favoured by low P, high T whereas step II is favoured by high P and low T.
  2. Equilibrium constant for step I increases with temperature whereas equilibrium constant for step II decreases with temperature
  3. Rate constants for both the steps increase with temperature
  4. The ratio of two equilibrium constants for step I and step II respectively is {tex}\frac{[D]}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The ratio of two equilibrium constants for step I and step II respectively is {tex}\frac{[D]}{\left[A_{2}\right]\left[B_{2}\right]}{/tex}

questions.question:
The % yield of ammonia as a function of time in the reaction
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g), {tex}\Delta H{/tex} < 0
at (P, T1) is given below:

If this reaction is conducted at (P, T2), with T2 > T1, the % yield of ammonia as a function of time is represented by:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Initially as the temperature increase and with the passes of time the % yield will increase at faster rate. Since the reaction is exothermic as {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is negative so when we increase the temperature the equilibrium will shift left. So, at equilibrium % yield will be less in T2 then T1.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
N2O4(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g); DH0 = + 58 kJ
For each of the following cases ((i), (ii)), the direction in which the equilibrium shifts is:

  1. Temperature is decreases
  2. Pressure is increased by adding N2 at constant T.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
1. As the reaction is endothermic ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Hz = +ve) so on a decrease in temperature, equilibrium will shift towards the reactant side.
  2. On an increase in pressure by adding inert gas (N2) at the same temperature, no shifting will take place. The equilibrium changes only if the added gas is a reactant or product involved in the reaction.

questions.question: In which of the following reactions, an increase in the volume of the container will favour the formation of products?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Volume {tex}\uparrow {P} \downarrow{/tex} reaction proceeds in which direction where the number of moles of gases increases.
2NO2 (g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO (g) + O2(g) {tex}\Delta n_{\mathrm{g}}{/tex} = (2 + 1) - 2 = 1

questions.question:
The following reaction occurs in the Blast Furnace where iron ore is reduced to iron metal:
Fe2O3(s) + 3CO(g) {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2 Fe(l) + 3 CO2(g)
Using Le Chatelier’s principle, predict which one of the following will not disturb the equilibrium?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Perturbation Shifts reaction towards
Removal of CO Left
Removal of CO2 Right
Addition of CO2 Left
Addition of Fe2O3 No change
(This is a solid compound. Its concentration has no effect on the equilibrium.)

questions.question: What happens when an inert gas is added to an equilibrium keeping volume unchanged?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: On adding inert gas at constant volume the total pressure of the system is increased, but the partial pressure of each reactant and product remains the same. Hence no effect on the state of equilibrium.

questions.question:
The Haber’s process for the formation of NH3 at 298K is N2 + 3H2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H= -46.0 kJ; Which of the following is the correct statement?

  1. The condition for equilibrium is {tex}G_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+3 G_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=2 G_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}{/tex} 
    where G is Gibb's free energy per mole of the gaseous species measured at that partial pressure.
  2. On adding N2, the equilibrium will shift to forward direction because according to IInd law of thermodynamics, the entropy must increase in the direction of spontaneous reaction
  3. The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by 2 times and that of backward reaction by 1.5 times
  4. None of these
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
In a reversible reaction, catalyst speeds up both the forward and backward reactions to the same extent, so The catalyst will increase the rate of forward reaction by 2 times and that of backward reaction by 1.5 times is wrong. At equilibrium,
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}=\mathrm{G}_{\text {products }}-\mathrm{G}_{\text {reactants }}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}-\left(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+3 \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}\right)=0{/tex}
or {tex}2 \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+3 \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}
On adding N2, reaction will move to forward direction because of Le-Chatelier's principle.

questions.question: The oxidation of SO2 by O, to SO3 is an exothermic reaction. The yield of SO3 will be maximum if

options.option: both temperature and pressure are increased
both temperature and pressure are reduced
temperature is reduced and pressure is increased
temperature is increased and pressure is kept constant

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
1. According is Le-Chatelier's principle, exothermic, reactions are favoured at Low temperature.
  2. According to Le-Chatelier's principle, the reaction in which n < 0, are favoured at high pressure.

Given 2SO2(g) + O2( g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2SO3(g) + Heat 
{tex}\because{/tex} It is an exothermic reaction
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The yield of SO3 is maximum at low temperature
n = 2 - 3 = -1 or n < 0 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The yield of SO3 is maximum at high pressure.

questions.question:
A certain endothermic reaction A {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2B is carried out at three different temperatures. Measured Kp values are given below. Arrange the reactions in increasing order of their reaction temperatures.

Reaction Kn
i 1700
ii 0.98
iii 47.9
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: For an endothermic reaction, the value of equilibrium constant increases with increase in temperature.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
2HI(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2(g) + I2(g) (Kc = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2)
If you start the reaction with 1 mol of HI, 0.371 mol of H2 and 0.750 mol of I2 in a 1L flask, the reaction will ________.

options.option: produce more reactants, since Q < K_c
produce more reactants, since Q > K_c
produce more products, since Q > K_c
produce more products, since Q < K_c

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
Reaction: 2HI {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2 + I2
Q = {tex}\frac{\left[{H}_{2}\right]\left[{I}_{2}\right]}{[{HI}]^{2}}=\frac{0.371 \times 0.750}{(1)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.278
Kc = 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q > Kc
The reaction will proceed in backward direction to form more reactants.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction at equilibrium: Fe3O4(S) + CO(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 3FeO(s) + CO2(g)
What effect will reducing the pressure have on the system?

options.option: Equilibrium will not shift in any direction.
Equilibrium will shift to the right.
Equilibrium will shift to the left.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: On reducing the pressure, equilibrium will shift in the direction that produces more gaseous moles. In this case, both reactant and product sides contain one gaseous mole each. Thus, the equilibrium will not shift in any direction.

questions.question: Sulphur dioxide combines with oxygen to produce sulphur trioxide gas. When the reaction reaches a point of equilibrium if the pressure of the equilibrium mixture is halved, what is the effect on Kp at constant temperature?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Change in pressure does not change the value of equilibrium constant.

questions.question:
Which one of the following conditions will favour maximum formation of the product in the reaction?
A2(g) + B2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} X2(g), {tex}\Delta_rH{/tex} = -x kJ
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex}H is negative, so the decrease in temperature will favour the formation of the product. The number of gaseous species decreases, so the increase in pressure will favour the formation of the product. Hence, low temperature and high pressure will favour the maximum formation of the product.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
{tex}\underset{Pink}{\lbrack Co(H_2O)_6\rbrack^{2+}} + 4Cl^- \rightleftharpoons \underset{Blue}{\lbrack CoCl_4\rbrack^{2-}} + 6H_2O{/tex}
What change will be observed on the addition of more water to the system at equilibrium?

options.option: More chloride ions will be formed.
The equilibrium will shift to the right.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: More chloride ions will be formed.

questions.question: To the following reaction mixture equilibrium, H2(g) + I2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2HI(g), ________.

options.option: when some HI is removed, Q > K
when I_2 is added, Q < K
when more H_2 is added, Q > K
when H_2 is removed, Q < K

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: when I2 is added, Q < K

questions.question: Hydrogen and oxygen do not combine under ordinary conditions to produce water. In the presence of a catalyst such as platinised asbestos, the reaction proceeds and attains a state of equilibrium. Which of the following is TRUE about the addition of platinised asbestos?

options.option: It increases the rate of the forward reaction only.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is attained faster.

questions.question: What happens if we drop some radioactive sugar into a saturated sugar solution of nonradioactive sugar?

options.option: Only the solid sugar will show traces of radioactive sugar.
The solution will show no traces of radioactive sugar.
Only the solution will show traces of radioactive sugar.
Both solution and solid sugar will show traces of radioactive sugar.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Due to dynamic equilibrium, rate of dissolution = rate of crystallization. When radioactive sugar is dissolved in a saturated sugar solution, traces of it will appear in both, the solution and solid sugar that remains undissolved in the solution.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction: 2H2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H2O(g)
Which of the following is TRUE about this reaction?

options.option: Addition of an inert gas at constant pressure shifts the equilibrium towards reactants.
The addition of inert gas at constant volume shifts the equilibrium towards products.
Addition of an inert gas at constant pressure has no effect on the equilibrium position.
The addition of inert gas at constant volume shifts the equilibrium towards reactants.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Addition of inert gas at constant pressure shifts the equilibrium towards reactants.

questions.question:
Which of the following change favors the forward direction?

  1. N2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO(g) {tex}\triangle{/tex}H = -180.7 kJ (Pressure increased and temperature increased)
  2. Dissolution of NaOH in water (Temperature increased)
  3. Water vapour {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Liquid water (Pressure increased)
  4. 2C(s) + O2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2CO(g) (volume reduced)
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
1. Pressure has no effect. An increase of temperature will shift the equilibrium in the backward direction.
  2. Solubility will decrease because it is an exothermic process.
  3. At higher temperatures, vapour will be converted to liquid as it decreases volume. Equilibrium will shift in the forward direction i.e., more liquid water is formed.
  4. On reducing the volume, equilibrium will shift to the side accompanied by a decrease in the number of gaseous moles, i.e., backward direction.

questions.question:
Hydrogen and iodine react to give hydrogen iodide in a closed vessel. Which of the following statement regarding chemical equilibrium for this reaction is FALSE?

  1. Relative concentrations of all species at equilibrium will be the same regardless of whether the starting mixture contained 1 mol H2 and 1 mol I2 or 2 mol HI.
  2. The reaction is reversible.
  3. Equilibrium is static in nature.
  4. Concentration of reactants and products can be changed by altering the temperature and pressure conditions.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is static in nature.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements regarding a reaction that is at equilibrium must be FALSE?

  1. The rate constant for the forward reaction is equal to the rate constant for the reverse reaction.
  2. The concentration of reactants and products are equal.
  3. The forward and reverse reactions are occurring at the same speed.
  4. The ratio of [Products]/[Reactants] < 1.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
1. The rate constant for the forward reaction is equal to the rate constant for the reverse reaction.
  2. The concentration of reactants and products are equal.
  3. The ratio of [Products]/[Reactants] < 1.

questions.question: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be ________.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
HO - NO2 + 2H2SO4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}2 {HSO}_{4}+{NO}_{2}^{+}+{H}_{3} {O}^{+}{/tex}
Nitric acid
KHSO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K+ + {tex}HSO_4^-{/tex}
On adding KHSO4, the equilibrium will shift towards the reactant side. This will decrease the {tex}NO_2^+{/tex}concentration. Hence, the rate of nitration will be slower.

questions.question:
Consider the following reaction:
A2B4(g) + C2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A2(g) + 2B2C(l)
Enthalpy of formation of A2B4 is 50.6 kJ/mol and B2C is -285.83 kJ/mol. A2 and C2 are in their elemental forms.
Which of the following changes will shift the equilibrium to the right?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta_{{f}} {H}\left({A}_{2} {B}_{4}\right){/tex} = 50.6 kJ/mol ... {tex}\triangle _f H(A_2){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Delta_{{f}} {H}\left({B}_{2} C\right){/tex} = -285.83 kJ/mol ... {tex}\triangle _f H(C_2){/tex} = 0
{tex}\triangle H_{reaction}{/tex} = 2(-285.83) - (50.6)
{tex}\triangle H_{reaction}{/tex} = -622 kJ/mol
The reaction is exothermic.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} All of the mentioned changes will shift the equilibrium to right.

questions.question:
The binding of oxygen by haemoglobin in blood involves the following equilibrium reaction:
{tex}{HbH}_{({aq})}^{+}+{O}_{2({aq})} \rightleftharpoons {HbO}_{2({aq})}+{H}_{({aq})}^{+}{/tex}
The pH of blood is normally in the range of 7.35 to 7.45. What will happen to the above equilibrium if pH of the blood is increased?

options.option: Fewer oxygen molecules will bind to HbH^+.
More oxygenated haemoglobin will be produced.

questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: As pH of the blood increases, the concentration of H+ decreases. This shifts the equilibrium towards the product side, producing more HbO2.

questions.question:
Considering the combustion of propane as an equilibrium process, which of the following is TRUE?

  1. Increasing the temperature produces more water molecules.
  2. Addition of a catalyst to the system increases the equilibrium constant value.
  3. Removing heat from the system increases the value of the equilibrium constant.
  4. Increasing the pressure increases the value of equilibrium constant.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Combustion of propane is an exothermic process. Assuming that the reaction is at equilibrium, removing heat from this reaction mixture will shift the equilibrium to the right. This will produce more products and thus the value of the equilibrium constant will increase.

questions.question: In the process of decomposition of ammonia, what change should be made to the system in order to increase the value of Kp for this reaction?
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Decompostition of ammonia is an endothermic process. For an endothermic reaction, increasing the temperature will cause the equilibrium to move towards the side that consumes heat (i.e. towards products). The value of Kp will thus increase.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is CORRECT?

  1. In an equilibrium mixture of ice and water kept in perfectly insulated flask, mass of ice and water changes with time.
  2. The intensity of red colour decreases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.
  3. On addition of catalyst, the equilibrium constant value is affected.
  4. Equilibrium constant for a reaction with negative {tex}\triangle{/tex}H value increases as the temperature increases.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The intensity of red colour decreases when oxalic acid is added to a solution containing iron (III) nitrate and potassium thiocyanate.

questions.question: In Haber’s process, if the reaction mixture is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a piston and the mixture is compressed to half the volume, then Qc = ________.
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NH3(g)
When volume is compressed to one-half of the initial volume, the concentration is doubled.
Kc = {tex}\frac{\left[{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[{N}_{2}\right]\left[{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
Now, Qc = {tex}\frac{\left[2 {NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[2 {N}_{2}\right]\left[2 \mathrm{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}=\frac{4\left[{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{2\left[{N}_{2}\right] \times 8\left[{H}_{2}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Qc = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}Kc

I2(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2I(g); {tex}\triangle H^{\circ}{/tex} (298 K) = +150 kJ
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: The given reaction is endothermic, so on increasing the temperature, it will shift in forward direction.

It is subjected to high pressure
It is subjected to high temperature

When argon is added at constant pressure, volume increase {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} active mass decreases i.e.. Decrease in active mass is more for products since there are more number of gaseous products.

questions.question: Consider the nitration of benzene using mixed cone. H2SO4 and HNO3. If a large amount of KHSO4 is added to the mixture, the rate of nitration will be:
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: When KHSO4 is added to this mixture then it dissociates to K+ and {tex}HSO _4^{-}{/tex} ions which increases {tex}HSO _4^{-}{/tex} concentration in the mixture thus, equilibrium shifts to backward reaction and hence rate of nitration becomes slower.

questions.question: For a reversible reaction, if the concentrations of the reactants are doubled, the equilibrium constant will be
questions.topicName: Factors Affecting Equilibria - Le Chatelier's Principle
questions.explanation: Equilibrium constant of a reaction is independent of the concentration of species involved in the reaction but dependent only on the temperature if the concentration of any species increases then concentration of other species changes accordingly to keep the value of equilibrium constant same.

questions.question: The species present in solution when CO2 is dissolved in water are
questions.explanation:
When CO2 is dissolved in water, following equilibria are established:
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
Therefore, in solution, all of the above mentioned species exist.

questions.question: Which of the following salts is the most basic in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation:
Among the given salts FeCl3 is acidic in nature i.e., have an acidic solution as it is the salt of a weak base and strong acid.
Al(CN)3 and Pb(CH3COO)2 are the salts of a weak acid and weak base.
CH3COOK is the salt of a strong base and weak acid.
Hence, the solution of CH3COOK will be most basic because of the following reaction.
{tex}CH_3COOK\;+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{(Weak\;acid)}{CH_3COOH\;}+\underset{(Strong\;base)}{KOH}{/tex}

questions.question: A certain weak acid has a dissociation constant of 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 104. The equilibrium constant for its reaction with a strong base is
questions.explanation:
The reaction of HA with strong base is
{tex}\mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+A^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K=\frac{\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\frac{K_{a}}{K_{w}}=\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-14}}=10^{10}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following compounds will undergo hydrolysis?
questions.explanation:
The salt of strong acid and strong bases like NaCl, KCl, and NaNO3 does not undergo hydrolysis.
In this case, neither the cations nor the anion undergoes hydrolysis. Therefore, the solution remains neutral.
In the aqueous solution of NaCl, KCl, and NaNO3  its ions Na+, K+, NO3, and Cl- ions have no tendency to react with H+ or OH- ions of water.
This is because the possible products of such reaction are NaOH, KOH, and HCl, which are they completely dissociated. As a result, there is no change in the concentration of H+ and OH- ions and hence the solution continues to remain neutral.
Only NH4Cl undergoes hydrolysis.

questions.question: Which of the following will not undergo hydrolysis in water?
questions.explanation: The salt of strong acid and the strong base does not undergo hydrolysis. So sodium sulphate will not undergo hydrolysis in water.

questions.question: A weak acid HX (Ka = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) on reaction with NaOH gives NaX. For 0.1 M aqueous solution of NaX, the % hydrolysis is:
questions.explanation:
For NaX
{tex}\underset{1-h}{X^-\;}+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\;H\underset hX+O\underset h{H^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore h=\sqrt{\frac{K_{H}}{c}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a} \cdot c}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5} \times 0.1}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10^{-8}}{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} %h = 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10-2 = 0.01

questions.question: A certain ion B- has an Arrhenius constant for basic character (eq. constant 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7). The equilibrium constant for Lowry-Bronsted basic character is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Bronsted\;base}{B^{-\;}\;}+\;H^+\;\rightleftharpoons\;HB;{/tex} Kb = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\underset{Arrhenius\;base}{B^{-\;}\;}+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\;HB\;+\;OH^-;{/tex} Ka = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 ...(ii)
By (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{b}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HB}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]} \times \frac{\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{[\mathrm{HB}]}{/tex}
= [H+] [OH-] = Kw ...(iii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kb = {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{w}}=\frac{2.8 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-14}}{/tex} =  2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 107

So, CH3COONa formed at equivalence point, which will undergoes hydrolysis to show pH >7 and solution will be alkaline in range.

questions.question: The two equilibria AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A+ + B- and AB + B- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}AB_2^ - {/tex} are simultaneously maintained in a solution with equilibrium constant K1 and K2 respectively. The ratio of A+ to {tex}AB_2^ - {/tex} in the solution is:

options.option: directly proportional to the square of the concentration of B^-
inversely proportional to the square of the concentration of B^-
inversely proportional to the concentration of B^-
directly proportional to the concentration of B^-

questions.explanation:
The two equilibria AB {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} A+ + B- and AB + B- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}AB_2^ - {/tex} are simultaneously maintained in a solution with equilibrium constant K1 and K2 respectively.
K1 = {tex}\frac{\left[{A}^{+}\right]\left[{B}^{-}\right]}{[{A}{B}]}{/tex}; K2 = {tex}\frac{\left[{A B}_{2}^{-}\right]}{[{A B}]\left[{B}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
Dividing K1 by K2, we get
{tex}\therefore \frac{{K}_{1}}{{K}_{2}}=\frac{\left[{A}^{+}\right]\left[{B}^{-}\right]^{2}}{\left[{A B}_{2}\right]^{-}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\left[{A}^{+}\right]}{\left[{A B}_{2}\right]} \propto \frac{1}{\left[{B}^{-}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Hence, the ratio of A+ and {tex}AB_2^ - {/tex} in the solution is inversely proportional to the square of the concentration of B-.

questions.question:
If equal volumes of {tex}{AB}_{2}{/tex} and XY (both are salts) aqueous solutions are mixed, which of the following combination will give a precipitate of {tex}{AY}_{2}{/tex} at 300 K?
[Given {tex}{K}_{{sp}}{/tex} (at 300 K) for {tex}{AY}_{2}=5.2 \times 10^{-7}{/tex}]
questions.explanation:
{tex}{AY}_{2} \rightleftharpoons {~A}^{2+}+2 {Y}^{-}{/tex}
Here, if {tex}K_{i p} \gt K_{s p}{/tex}, then the precipitation of {tex}{AY}_{2}{/tex} takes place.

  1. {tex}{A}^{2+}=3.6 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}K_{i p} =\left[3.6 \times 10^{-3}\right]\left[5 \times 10^{-4}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =5 \times 10^{-4} {M} \text { shift }{/tex}
    {tex}K_{i p} =9 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}K_{i p} \lt K_{s p}{/tex}, no precipitate is formed.
  2. {tex}{\left[{A}^{2+}\right] } =2 \times 10^{-4} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[2 \times 10^{-4}\right]\left[0.8 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =0.8 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =1.28 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p^{\prime}}{/tex} no precipitate is formed.
  3. {tex}{A}^{2+} =2 \times 10^{-2} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[2 \times 10^{-2}\right]\left[2 \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{\left[{Y}^{-}\right] } =2 \times 10^{-2} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =8 \times 10^{-6} {M}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p}{/tex}, precipitate is formed.
  4. {tex}{A}^{2+} =1.5 \times 10^{-4} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}{Y}^{-} =1.5 \times 10^{-3} {M}{/tex}
    {tex}k_{i p} =\left[1.5 \times 10^{-4}\right]\left[1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=3.375 \times 10^{-10} {M}{/tex}
    So, when {tex}k_{i p} \lt k_{s p}{/tex}, no precipitate is formed.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.explanation:
1. Higher the electronegatively of central atom, higher will be the acidic strength.
  2. In case of same atom, higher the value of oxidation state of the metal, higher will be its acidic strength.
    The electronegativity of Cl > S.
    Oxidation no. of Cl in ClO3(OH) = + 7
    Oxidation no. of Cl in ClO2(OH) = + 5
    Oxidation no. of S in SO(OH)2 = + 4
    Oxidation no. of S in SO2(OH)2 = + 6
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} ClO3(OH) is the strongest acid.

questions.question: The compound that is not a Lewis acid is

questions.question: The best indicator for detection of end point in titration of a weak acid and a strong base is
questions.explanation: When a weak acid (FLY) is titrated against a strong base NaOH, basic salt (N&Y) is present at the end point which makes end point slightly basic with pH around 8. Hence, phenolphthalein, that changes its colour in this pH range, would be the best choice of indicator to detect the end point.

questions.question: Of the given anions, the strongest base is
questions.explanation:
The order of acidic strength of conjugate acids is
HOCI < HCIO2 < HCIO3 < HCIO4
Reverse is the order of basic strength of their conjugate base, i.e.
CIO- is the strongest base.

questions.question: Strongest conjugate base is
questions.explanation: The strong acid has a weak conjugate base and we know in halogen acidity increases down the group so {tex}{F^ - }{/tex} is the strongest conjugate base.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
questions.explanation: A Lewis acid is any substance, such as the H+ ion, that can accept a pair of nonbonding electrons. In other words, a Lewis acid is an electron-pair acceptor.  It means that Ag+ is acting as lewis acid by accepting a pair of electron.

questions.question: The strongest Bronsted base among the following anions is
questions.explanation: Bronsted base is the substance that can accept H+ ions. With the increase in the number of oxygen atoms in the given conjugate bases, the delocalization of the {tex}\pi{/tex} bond becomes more extended. This results in a decrease in electron density. Consequently, proton attraction and basicity also decrease in the order ClO−>ClO2−​>ClO3−​>ClO4−​. Hence, the strongest Bronsted base is ClO−.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest base?
questions.explanation: As hydride ion is a stronger base, so it will react with water which acts as an acid.

questions.question: A base, as defined by Bronsted theory, is a substance which can
questions.explanation: A Bronsted-Lowry base is a substance that accepts a proton in the form of a hydrogen (H) atom. This is why when we look at the Bronsted-Lowry acid-base reaction, the Bronsted-Lowry base is identified as the substance that gains a hydrogen atom.

questions.question: In the reaction : {tex}{I_2} + {I^ - } \to {I_3}^ - ,{/tex} the Lewis base is
questions.explanation: A Lewis Base is a species that can donate a pair of electrons to an electron acceptor. Here I− is giving electron pair to I2​ molecule. Therefore I2​ is a lewis acid and I− is a lewis base.

questions.question: Which of the following doesn’t act as Lewis acid?
questions.explanation: Lewis acids are those molecules which can accept electron pair and those who can donate electron pairs are called Lewis base. CCI4 molecule does not have vacant d-orbital where it can accept pair of electrons so CCI4 cannot act as Lewis acid.

questions.question: Acidic nature of BF3 can be explained on the basis of which of the following concepts?

questions.question: Aprotic solvent is:

Acid {tex}\stackrel{-\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} Conjugate base; Base {tex}\stackrel{+\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} Conjugate acid;
[Al(H2​O)2​(OH)3​]−H+→[Al(H2​O)2​(OH)4−​].

questions.question: Mg(OH)Cl is an example of:
questions.explanation: A basic salt has one or more replaceable OH in it.

H3BO3 is monobasic Lewis acid and accepts low pair from OH-

questions.question: Which statement is/are correct?

options.option: All Bronsted bases are also Lewis bases
All Bronsted acids are not Lewis acids
All cations are acids and all anions are bases

questions.explanation: Bronsted bases are proton acceptor and each Lewis base contain one or more electron pair and thus accept a proton. On the other hand, Bronsted acids are proton donors and may or may not be capable of accepting lone pairs of electrons, e.g., H2SO4 is Bronsted acid but not Lewis acid; BF3 is Lewis acid but not Bronsted acid. Also, each cation is acid and each anion is base.

questions.question: NH3 acts as an acid in:
questions.explanation: N-H bond behaves as acidic in nature.

questions.explanation: H3PO2 is monobasic acid and forms only one salt NaH2PO3. Na3BO3 is however not obtained by the action of NaOH over H3BO3 but can be formed by alternate methods.

questions.question: Which one is Lewis acid?
questions.explanation: Rest all are bases; each anion is base.

questions.question: pH of 0.01 HS- will be:
questions.explanation: pH of salt of amphoteric nature is given by this formula.

questions.question: Glycine (NH2CH2COOH) is:

questions.question: Which is not a Lewis base?
questions.explanation: Ag+ cannot donate the electron pair.

questions.question: Decreasing acid strengths of HI, HBr, HCl and HF is:

  2. ZnO + CO2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnCO3

In (a), Na2O is basic oxide and thus ZnO acts as acidic oxide.
In (b), CO2 is acidic oxide and thus ZnO acts as basic oxide.

questions.question:

ClOH (I), BrOH (II), IOH (III)
questions.explanation: HOCl > HOBr > HOI due to more electronegative nature of the halogen.

questions.question: The concept that an acid is a proton donor and a base is a proton acceptor was introduced by:
questions.explanation: This is the Bronsted-Lowry concept of acid-base.

questions.question:
An alkali is titrated against acid with methyl orange as indicator, which of the following is a correct combination?

  Base Acid Endpoint
(a) Weak Strong Yellow to pinkish-red
(b) Strong Strong Pink to colourless
(c) Weak Strong Colourless to pink
(d) Strong Strong Pinkish red to yellow
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Weak\;base}{NH_4OH}\;+\underset{Strong\;acid}{\;HCl\;}\rightarrow NH_4Cl\;+\;H_2O{/tex}
At the end point, pH will be less than 7 due to hydrolysis of NH4Cl
{tex}NH_4^+{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + H+
Working range of methyl orange (MeOH):
In acid medium : Pink red
In alkaline medium : Yellow
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Methyl orange will change colour from yellow to pinkish red at the end point.

questions.question: 0.1 M solution of which one of these substances will act basic?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + H+ (acidic)
Na2SO4 and Ca(NO3)2 will not hydrolyse. Na3BO3 will give alkaline solution.

questions.question: Which statement is correct?

options.option: The conjugate base of a strong acid is strong base
An acid and its conjugate base react to form salt and water

The acid H_2O is its own conjugate base

questions.explanation: Stronger is acid, weaker is its conjugate base and vice-versa.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the correct sequence for decreasing Lewis acid nature?
questions.explanation: Due to back bonding (BF3 shows maximum tendency due to the small size of F).

questions.explanation: BF3 has an incomplete octet and will act as Lewis acid. NH3 has lone pair of electrons available for donation and thus Lewis base.

CO_2 < SiO_2 < SnO_2 < PbO_2 : increasing oxidising power

questions.explanation: The acidic character of hydride increases down the group, so, pKa decreases.

questions.explanation: Bond length increases from H2S to H2Te. Larger is bond length more easily it breaks up.

questions.question:
Four species are listed below:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
  2. H3O+
  3. {tex}\mathrm{HSO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
  4. HSO3F

Which one of the following is the correct sequence of their acid strength?
questions.explanation: HSO3F is strong acid. Also, H2O is neutral, its conjugate base is stronger and thus, H3O+ is stronger acid than {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}. Alos {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} is a stronger base of conjugate weak acid H2CO3 whereas {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} is weak base of strong conjugate acid. Thus, {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} is a stronger acid than {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}.

questions.question: Among the following, the correct order of acidity is:

Greater is the oxidation number of a central atom in oxoacids more is the acidic nature of oxoacid.
{tex}\underset{+1}{HClO}<\underset{+3}{HClO_2}<\underset{+5}{HClO_3}<\underset{+7}{HClO_4}{/tex}
Note: In question, acidic strength should have been written in place of acidity.

questions.question: Which of the following oxides is most acidic in nature?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{BeO}<\mathrm{MgO}<\mathrm{CaO}<\mathrm{BaO}}{\text { Basic character increases }}{/tex}
BeO is amphoteric oxide while other given oxides are basic.

questions.question:
Which is the strongest acid in the following?

  2. HClO4

questions.explanation: Acidic order: {tex}H\overset{+7}{Cl}O_4>H\overset{+5}{Cl}O_3>H\overset{+3}{Cl}O_2>H\overset{+1}{Cl}O{/tex}; Higher is the oxidation number of central non-metal in oxo-acids, more stronger is acid. Also, HClO4 is stronger acid than H2SO4.

  2. BrOH
  3. IOH

{tex}\mathop {HClO}\limits_{ + 1} > {\text{ }}\mathop {HCI{O_2}}\limits_{ + 3} > {\text{ }}\mathop {HCl{O_3}}\limits_{ + 5} > {\text{ }}\mathop {HOC{l_4}}\limits_{ + 7} {/tex}

  2. All Bronsted-Lowry bases are Arrhenius bases.
  3. A Lewis acid is an electron pair acceptor.
  4. The pH of a solution of the salt of a weak acid and a strong base is usually less than 7.
questions.explanation: (i) and (iii)

  2. All alkalis are bases.

questions.question: Acidic nature of BF3 can be explained on the basis of:

options.option: Bronsted - Lowry as well as Lewis concept

questions.explanation: BF3 is electron deficient compound and hence is a Lawis acid.

questions.question: The correct order of acidic strength is:
questions.explanation: The species Cl2O7, SO2 and P4O10 are the anhydrides of HClO4, H2SO3 and H3PO4 respectively. The acid strength of these acids follows the order HClO4 > H2SO3 > H3PO4. The corresponding anhydrides also follow the same order.

questions.question: The conjugate base of {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex} is:
questions.explanation: {tex}HPO_4^ {2- }{/tex}

questions.question: In the reaction, AICI3 + Cl- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} [AICI4]-, AlCl3 acts as:
questions.explanation:
According to Lewis theory of Acids and Bases, acids are electron pair (lone pair) acceptors and bases are electron pair (lone pair) donors.
For example, in the following reaction:
NH3 + BF3 {tex}\to{/tex} NH3 {tex}\to{/tex} BF3
NH3 donates its electron pair (lone pair) to BF3.
Hence, NH3​ is a Lewis Base whereas BF3​ is a Lewis Acid.
In the given example, AlCl3 ​accepts an electron pair (lone pair) from Cl-.
Therefore, AlCl3 is a Lewis Acid.

questions.question: Which does not act as Bronsted acid?
questions.explanation: Bronsted acid is an specie which can donate a proton. CH3COO− cannot donate a proton and others can donate a proton so they acts as a bronsted acid.

questions.question: Arrange {tex}NH_4^ + {/tex}, H2O, H3O+, HF and OH- in increasing order of acidic nature:
questions.explanation: OH- is base and H2O is neutral.

questions.question: The strongest Bronsted base is:
questions.explanation: HClO is the weakest acid. Its conjugate base ClO- is the strongest base.

questions.question:
The following equilibrium are attained in forward direction:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{HBr} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{HBr} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}^{+}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}{/tex}
The correct order for acidic strength is:
questions.explanation: HBr > {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}^{+}>\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}

questions.question: The conjugate base of H3BO3 is:
questions.explanation: H3BO3 + OH- {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}H_4BO_4^ - {/tex} or {tex}B(OH)_4^ - {/tex}

questions.question: Which species acts as an acid and also a conjugate base of another acid?
questions.explanation: {tex}HSO_4^ - {/tex}

questions.question: The weakest base among the following is:
questions.explanation: Cl-

questions.question: Decreasing acid strengths of HI, HBr, HCl and HF is:
questions.explanation: HI > HBr > HCl > HF

questions.question: The decreasing trend of acidic nature of trihalides of boron is:
questions.explanation:
The decreasing trend of acidic nature of trihalides of boron is as follows:
BI3 > BBr3 > BCl3 > BF3
This is reverse of the order expected on the basis of relative electronegativities of the halogens.
This anomalous behaviour is due to the relative tendency of the halogen atom to back donate its unutilized electrons to the vacant p orbitals of boron atom.
The tendency for back donation increases in the order BI3 > BBr3 > BCl3 > BF3
The back bonding is maximum in boron trifluoride due to which the electron deficiency is minimum and the acidic character is minimum.

questions.question: The increasing order of acid strength HCIO4, HClO3, HClO2, HCIO is:
questions.explanation:
With increase in oxidation number of a particular halogen atom, the acidic character of corresponding oxoacid increases.
The charge stabilization increases in the order: ClO- < ClO2- < ClO3- < ClO4-
The increasing order of acid strength is:
HCIO < HCIO2 < HClO3 < HCIO4

questions.question: Which one is more acidic in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation:
AlCl3​ is more acidic in aqueous solution as on hydrolysis, it gives weak base and strong acid.
AlCl3 + 3H2O {tex}\to{/tex} Al(OH)3 + 3HCl

questions.question: Water acts as an acid in presence of:
questions.explanation:
NH3 is a proton acceptor and thus, H2​O behaves as a proton donor. So, it acts as an acid.
NH3 + H2O {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}NH_4^ + {/tex} + OH-

questions.question: Which one of the following substances has the highest proton affinity?
questions.explanation: Among the given compounds, the NH3 is the most basic. Hence, has highest proton affinity.

questions.question: Identify the correct order of acidic strengths of CO2, CuO, CaO and H2O.
questions.explanation:
Non-metal oxides are acidic in character. They show acidic nature on reaction with water.
The non-metallic character and the same will be the order for acidity.
Non-metallic nature decreases down the group and increases down the period.
CO2​ - Acidic, CuO - Weak base
CaO - Basic, H2O - Acidic
Hence the non-metallic nature order: CaO < CuO < H2O < CO2

questions.question: H3BO3 is:
questions.explanation:
Central boron atom in H3BO3 is electron deficient, therefore it accepts a pair of electron, hence it is weak Lewis acid. There is no d-orbital of suitable energy in boron atom. So, it can accommodate only one additional electron pair in its outermost shell. Thus, H3BO3 is a monobasic weak Lewis acid.
H2O(base) + B(OH)3(acid) {tex}\to{/tex} [B(OH)4]- + H+

questions.question: In HS-, I-, R-NH2 and NH3 order of proton accepting tendency will be:
questions.explanation:
Proton accepting tendency is known as the strength of basicity.
In {tex}R - \ddot N{H_2}{/tex}, N has lone pair of electron which intensify due to electron releasing R-group and increase the tendency to donate lone pair of electrons to H+.
Secondly as the size of the ion increases there is less attraction for H+ to form weaker bonds with H-atom and are less basic. The order of the given series: R - NH2 > NH3 > HS- > I-.

questions.question: Which one is hard base?
questions.explanation:
As we know, Hard bases (ligands) are also small, not very polarisable and having high electronegativity.
So F- is a hard base.

questions.question: The conjugate base of OH- is:
questions.explanation:
When acid gives H+ then the remaining of its part is called conjugate base.
So if we remove H+ from OH-, we get O2-
OH- {tex}\to{/tex} O2- + H+
Hence O2- is the conjugate base of OH-.

questions.question: According to Bronsted Lowry concept, the correct order of strength of bases follows the order:
questions.explanation: OH- > CH3COO- >Cl-

questions.question: An acid with molecular formula C7H6O3 forms three types of sodium salts, i.e., C7H5O3 Na, C7H4O3 Na2 and C7H3O3 Na3. The basicity of the acid:
questions.explanation:
Basicity of an acid is the number of replaceable hydrogen atoms present in the molecule of an acid.
C7H6O3 {tex}\to{/tex} Acid
C7H3O3Na3 {tex}\to{/tex} Three replaceable hydrogens
Basicity = 3

questions.question: The mixed salt among the following is:
questions.explanation: A mixed salt is one which furnishes two types of cations or anions. (other than H+ and OH−).

questions.question: From separate solutions of four sodium salts NaW, NaX, NaY and NaZ had pH 7.0, 9.0, 10.0 and 11.0 respectively. When each solution was 0.1 M, the strongest acid is:
questions.explanation: The salt hydrolysis in each case occurs except NaW because its pH = 7. Thus HW is strongest acid.

questions.question: The conjugate base of hydrazoic acid is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {{{\text{N}}_3}{\text{H}}}\limits_{Hydrazoic \ acid} \rightleftharpoons {\text{N}}_3^ - + {{\text{H}}^ + }{/tex} 
i.e, conjugate base of hydrazoic acid is N3.

questions.question:
The set with correct order of acidity is

  1. HClO < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO4
  2. HClO4 < HClO3 < HClO2 < HClO
  3. HClO < HClO4 < HClO3 < HClO2
  4. HClO4 < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO
questions.explanation:
TIPS/Formulae: For oxyacids containing similar central atom, the acid strength increases with the increase in the number of an oxygen atom attached to the central atom and not attached to any other atom.
TIPS/Formulae: Higher the oxidation number of the central atom, the higher is the acidity of the species. Thus, acidity follows the order
Oxi. No. of Cl: {tex}\underset{+1}{\mathrm{HClO}_{}}{/tex} < {tex}\underset{+3}{\mathrm{HClO}_{2}}{/tex} < {tex}\underset{+5}{\mathrm{HClO}_{3}}{/tex} < {tex}\underset{+7}{\mathrm{HClO}_{4}}{/tex}

HOCl (I) > HOBr (II) > HOI (III)

questions.question: Which of the following statements about pH and H+ ion concentration is incorrect?

options.option: Addition of one drop of concentrated HCI in NH_4OH solution decreases pH of the solution.
A solution of the maximum of one equivalent of each of CH_3COOH and NaOH has a pH of 7.
pH of pure neutral water is not zero.
A cold and concentrated H_2SO_4 has lower H^+ ion concentration than a dilute solution of H_2SO_4

questions.explanation: HCI is a strong acid and NH4OH is a weak base thus, on addition of HCI to NH4OH solution, concentration of H+ increases and pH decreases according to the equation, pH = - log[H+]. CH3COOH is a weak acid and NaOH is a strong base thus, in a solution containing 1 equivalent of each there are more OH- ions than H+ ions due to incomplete ionization of CH3COOH hence, pH is more than 7. Thus, the statement is incorrect. pH value of pure neutral water is not zero but it has a value of 7. A cold and concentrated H2SO4 has lower H+ ion concentration because in this condition it exists mostly in unionizable form while when diluted it completely ionizes to give H+ ions.

questions.question: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. options.option: acetic acid > hypochlorous acid > formic acid
formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid
hypochlorous acid > acetic acid > formic acid

questions.explanation:
Here, Ka for Formic Acid (HCOOH) =  1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4  , Acetic Acid (CH3COOH) = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 and  Hypochlorous Acid (HCIO) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–8
At a given temperature T, Ka is a measure of the strength of the acid HX i.e., larger the value of Ka , the stronger is the acid. Ka is a dimensionless quantity with the understanding that the standard state concentration of all species is 1M.
Ka = [H+][X-] / [HX]
{tex}{[{H_3}{O^ + }]\; = \;\sqrt {{K_a}.C} }{/tex}
{tex}{pH = \; - \log \;[{H_3}{O^ + }]}{/tex}
larger the value of Ka larger will be H3O+,hence lower will be pH

questions.question: What will be the value of pH of 00.1 mol dm-3 CH3COOH (Ka = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)?

that is the equation to use is: [H+] = (Ka.Cweakacid)1/2   = (1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01)1/2 = 4.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
pH = -log [H+] = - log (4.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) = - [log 4.3 + (-4)log 10] = -[0.633-4] = 3.367

questions.question: Ka for CH3COOH is {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} and Kb for NH4OH is {tex}1.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}. The pH of ammonium acetate will be

questions.question:
The ionisation of hydrochloric in water is given below:
{tex}{\text{HCl }}\left( {{\text{aq}}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right){/tex} {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}H_3O^+\:+\;Cl^-\\\end{array}{/tex}

questions.explanation:

questions.question: 0.3g of Ca(OH)2 is dissolved in water to give 500 mL of solution. The pH of the solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
Molarity={tex}\;\frac{mass\;of\;Ca(OH)_2\times1000}{Molar\;mass\;\times Volume}=\frac{0.3\times1000}{56\times500}={/tex} 0.0107M
The chemical reaction involved are, CaO + H2O = Ca(OH)2
Here, Ca(OH)2 = Ca2+ + 2OH-
Concentration of OH- = 2[OH-] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0107 = 0.0214M
Now,
Kw = [H3O+][OH-] {tex}{ \Rightarrow [{H_3}{O^ + }] = \frac{{{K_w}}}{{[O{H^ - }]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.0214}}}{/tex} = 46.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
Therefore,  pH = -log[H3O+] = - log [46.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14] = 12.33

7.38 = -log[H+]

Taking antilog on both sides, we get [H+] = 4.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M

questions.question: The ionization constant of acetic acid is {tex}1.74 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}. Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in its 0.05 M solution. Calculate the concentration of acetate ion in the solution and its pH.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_a}C}{/tex}

Here, C=0.05M,

 {tex}K_a=1.74\times10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = {\left\{ {\frac{{1.74 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.05}}} \right\}^{1/2}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha = {\{ 1.74 \times {10^{ - 5}}/0.05\} ^{1/2}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = [34.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}]{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = (3.48)1/2 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha = 1.86 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
CH3COOH = CH3COO- + H+
Concentration of {tex}C{H_3}CO{O^ - } = C\alpha {/tex}
{tex} = 0.05 \times 1.86 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 0.00093M{/tex}
Since [CH3COO-] = [H+]
[H+] = 0.00093 = {tex}0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
{tex}pH = - \log [{H^ + }] = - \log [0.093 \times {10^{ - 2}}]{/tex}
pH = -[log(0.093) + (-2)log10]
{tex}pH = 3.03{/tex}

questions.question: It has been found that the pH of a 0.01M solution of an organic acid is 4.15. Calculate the ionization constant of the acid,  the concentration of the anion, and its pKa.

[H+] = 7.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = [A-]
Ka = [H+][A-][HA]
Ka = 5.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
pKa = - log [5.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7] = 6.3001

questions.question: 0.023 g of sodium metal is reacted with 100 cm3 of water. The pH of the resulting solution is ______.
questions.explanation:
Moles of NaOH formed  = 0.023/23 =0.001
Concentration of NaOH = {tex}\frac{\left(0.001\right)\times\left(1000\right)}{\left(100\right)}=0.01{/tex}M
pOH=-log[OH-] = - log [ 0.01] = -log [10-2 ] = - (-2) log 10 = 2
We know that, pH + pOH =14
pH =14-2 = 12

questions.question: pH value of which one of the following is NOT equal to one?
questions.explanation:
{tex}CH_3COOH\;+H_2O\rightleftharpoons\;CH_3COO^-+H_3O^+{/tex}
Since the dissociation constant of acetic acid is so small, we can assume that the concentration of acetic acid is still approximately 0.1 M even after dissociating.
Also [CH3COO-] = [H3O+]
Therefore,
{tex}K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}K_a=\frac{\left[CH_3COO^-\right]\left[H_3O^+\right]}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{\left[CH_3COOH\right]}\\\Rightarrow1.7\times10^{-5}\;=\frac{\left[H_3O^+\right]^2}{0.1}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=\sqrt{1.7\times10^{-6}}\\\Rightarrow\left[H_3O^+\right]=1.32\times10^{-3}\\now,\;pH=\;-\log\left[H_3O^+\right]=\;-\log1.32\times10^{-3}\;=2.88\end{array}{/tex}

questions.question: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.explanation:
pH=5 means the solution is acidic. On dilution, it will remain acidic. However, acidic character decreases so much that it is almost neutral (pH=5 means [H+] = 10-5M). After dilution, [H+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-5}}{100}=10^{-7}M{/tex} ; [H+] from H2O cannot be neglected.
Hence total [H+] = 10-7 + 10-7 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH=-log(2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 ) = 7-0.3010=6.699

questions.question: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H3O+,F- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.explanation:
HF {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + F-
Initial Concentration 1 0 0
Equilibrium concentration C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) C {tex}\alpha{/tex} C {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Now, {tex}{{K_a}\; = \frac{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]\left[ {{F^ - }} \right]}}{{\left[ {HF} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]}}{{\left[ {C(1 - \alpha )\;} \right]}} = \frac{{\;C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )\;}} \approx C{\alpha ^2}}{/tex}  (Since {tex}\alpha{/tex} is less (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) can be written as 1
{tex}{ \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}\;}}{C}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{0.02}}} = 12.65 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}{/tex}
[H3O+] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} =0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1265 = 2.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
[F-]  = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1265 = 2.53 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3
[HF]= C(1-{tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} (1-0.1265) = 17.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
Now,
pH= - log [H3O+] = - log (2.53 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3 - 0.4031 = 2.5969

questions.question: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.explanation:
KOH {tex}\to{/tex} K+ + OH- (As KOH undergoes complete ionization)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [OH-] = [KOH] = 0.02
Now, Kw = [H+] [OH-]{tex}{ \Rightarrow \left[ {{H^ + }} \right] = \frac{{\;{K_w}\;}}{{\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.02}} = \;5 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH of KOH = - log [5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12] = 12 - log 5 = 12 - 0.699 {tex}\approx{/tex} 11.31

questions.question: The pH of a 0.02 M NH4Cl solution will be [Given Kb(NH4OH) = 10-5 and log2 = 0.301]
questions.explanation:
Given, Kb(NH4OH) = 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pKb = - logKb = - log(10-5) = 5
C = concentration of salt solution = 0.02 M
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M
Now, pH = {tex}7-\frac{1}{2}\left(p K_{b}+\log C\right){/tex}
On substituting the given values in above equation, we get
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}\left[5+\log \left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}[5+\log 2-2]{/tex}
{tex}=7-\frac{1}{2}[5+0.301-2]{/tex} = 7 - 1.65 = 5.35

questions.question:
Consider the following statements.

  1. The pH of a mixture containing 400 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 and 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH will be approximately 1.3.
  2. Ionic product of water is temperature-dependent.
  3. A monobasic acid with Ka = 10-5 has a pH = 5. The degree of dissociation of this acid is 50%.
  4. The Le-Chatelier’s principle is not applicable to the common ion effect.
    The correct statements are
questions.explanation:
The explanation of given statements are as follows:
In statement (I), millimoles of H+ = 400 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 80
Millimoles of OH- = 400 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 40 (Limiting reagent)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Millimoles of H+ left = 80 - 40 = 40
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{40}{400+400}=\frac{40}{800} M=\frac{1}{20} M{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(\frac{1}{20}\right){/tex}
= -logl + log2 + log10
= -0 + 0.301 + 1
= 1.30
In a statement (II), the ionic product of H2O is temperature-dependent.
Kw = [H+][OH-] {tex}\approx{/tex} 10-14(mol/L)2 at 25°C
With the increase in temperature, dissociation of H2O units into H+ and OH- ions will also increase. As a result, the value of the ionic product, [H+] {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-] will be increased, e.g.

Temperature Kw(mol/L2)
5°C 0.186 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
25°C 1.008 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14
45°C 4.074 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14

Hence, the option (b) is correct.
In the statement (III), for a weak monobasic acid HA
{tex}\mathbf{H} A \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{H}^{\oplus}+A^{\ominus}{/tex}
(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) CM {tex}\alpha{/tex}CM {tex}\alpha{/tex}CM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH of the solution is 5, i.e.
[H+] = 10-5 M = {tex}\alpha{/tex}C
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K_{a}=\frac{\alpha C \times \alpha C}{(1-\alpha) C}=\frac{10^{-5} \times \alpha}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 10^{-5}=\frac{10^{-5} \times \alpha}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha{/tex}% = 50
In the statement (IV), Le-Chatelier’s principle is applicable to the common ion effect. Because, in the presence of common ion (given) by a strong electrolyte (say, Na+ {tex}\overline A{/tex}), the product of the concentration terms in RHS increases. For the weaker electrolyte, HA (say) the equilibrium shifts to the LHS,
{tex}\mathbf{H} A \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{H}^{\oplus}+A^{\ominus}{/tex}.
As a result dissociation of IL4 gets suppressed. Hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.question: 20 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 solution is added to 30 mL of 0.2 M NH4OH solution. The pH of the resultant mixture is [pK b of NH4OH = 4.7]
questions.explanation:
The reaction takes place when H2SO4 is added to NH4OH is as follows:


So, the resulting solution is a basic buffer
[NH4OH+ (NH4)2S04]
According to the Henderson’s equation,
{tex}\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_{b}+\log \frac{\left[\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=4.7+\log \frac{2}{2}{/tex}
= 4.7 + 0.3010
= 5.001 {tex}\approx{/tex} 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 5 = 9

questions.question: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is
questions.explanation:
For a salt of weak acid and weak base,
pH = {tex}7+\frac{1}{2} p K_{a}-\frac{1}{2} p K_{b}{/tex}
Given, pKa (HA) = 3.2, pKa (BOH) = 3.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = {tex}7+\frac{1}{2}(3.2)-\frac{1}{2}(3.4){/tex}
= 7 + 1.6 - 1.7 = 6.9

questions.question: How many litres of water must be added to 1 L of an aqueous solution of HCl with a pH of 1 to create an aqueous solution with pH of 2?
questions.explanation:
pH = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-1 = 0.1 M
pH = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-2 = 0.01 M
For dilution of HCl, M1V1 = M2V2
0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} V2
V2 = 10 L
Volume of water to be added = 10 -1 = 9 L

questions.question: 2.5 mL of {tex}\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{M}{/tex} weak monoacidic base (Kb = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 25°C) is titrated with {tex}\frac{2}{15} \mathrm{M}{/tex} HCl in water at 25°C. The concentration of H+ at equivalence point is (Kw = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 at 25°C)
questions.explanation:
Let the weak manoacidic base be BOH, then the reaction that occurs during titration is BOH + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BCl + H2O
Equilibrium: {tex}\underset{C(1-h)}{B^{+}}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{C . h}{B O H}+\underset{C . h}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{/tex}
Using the normality equation, {tex}\begin{aligned} & N_1 V_1= \\ & \text { (acid) } \end{aligned}{/tex}{tex}\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2 V_2 \\ & \text { (base) } \end{aligned}{/tex}
Substituting various given values, we get
{tex}\frac{2}{15} \times V_1=2.5 \times \frac{2}{5}{/tex} r V1 = {tex}2.5 \times \frac{2}{5} \times \frac{15}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 705 mL
Then the concentration of BCl in resulting solution is given by
[BCl] = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{15} \times 2.5}{10}=\frac{2}{10}{/tex} or 0.1 M
Since Kh = {tex}\frac{K_w}{K_b}{/tex} {tex}\therefore K_h=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 10-2
Thus Kh = {tex}\frac{0.1 h^2}{(1-h)}{/tex} or 10-2 = {tex}\frac{0.1 h^2}{(1-h)}{/tex}
or 10-2 - 10-2 h = 0.1 h2 or 0.1 h2 + 10-2 h - 10-2 = 0
(Solving this quadratic equation for h, we get)
h = {tex}\frac{-10^{-2} \pm \sqrt{\left(10^{-2}\right)^2+4 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}}}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex}
{tex}\left[x=\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4 a c}}{2 a}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-10^{-2} \pm \sqrt{10^{-4}+4 \times 10^{-3}}}{2 \times 0.1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm \sqrt{.0001+0.004}}{0.2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm \sqrt{0.0041}}{0.2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-0.01 \pm 0.64}{0.2}=\frac{0.54}{0.2}{/tex} [Neglecting the negative term]
= 0.27
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = C.h = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.27 = 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M

questions.question: CH3NH2 (0.1 mole, Kb = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) is added to 0.08 mole of HCl and the solution is diluted to one litre, resulting hydrogen ion concentration is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_{b}+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}\right]}{/tex}
= - log (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) + log {tex}\frac{0.08}{0.02}{/tex} = 3.9
pH = 14 - pOH = 10.1
[H+] = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11

questions.question: HX is a weak acid (Ka =10-5). It forms as reacting with caustic soda. The degree of hydrolysis of NaX is
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{h}\left(X^{-}\right)=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5}}=10^{-9} \Rightarrow \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{h}}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-9}}{0.10}}=10^{-4}{/tex}
% hydrolysis = 100 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.01

questions.question: The pH of 0.1 M solution of the following salts increases in the order
questions.explanation:
The characteristics of the given solutions are:
NaCl - neutral solution
NH4Cl - slightly acidic due to the following reaction
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex}
NaCN - slightly alkaline due to the following reaction
CN- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HCN + OH-
HCl - highly acidic
The pH of the solution will follow the order highly acidic < slightly acidic < neutral < slightly alkaline i.e.
HCl < NH4Cl < NaCl < NaCN

questions.question: Which of the following solutions will have pH close to 1.0?

options.option: 10 mL of (M/10) HCl + 90 mL of (M/10) NaOH
55 mL of (M/10) HCl + 45 mL of (M/10) NaOH
100 mL of (M/10) HCI + 100 mL of (M/10) NaOH
75 mL of (M/5) HCl + 25 mL of (M/5) NaOH

questions.explanation:
75 mL {tex}\frac{M}{5} {/tex}HCl = 15 mmol HCl
25 mL {tex}\frac{M}{5} {/tex} NaOH = 5 mmol NaOH
After neutralisation, 10 mmol HCl will be remaining in 100 mL of solution
Molarity of HCl in the final solution = {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 0.10
pH = -log [H+] = -log (0.10) = 1

questions.question: The pKa of acetyl salicylic acid (aspirin) is 3.5. The pH of gastric juice in human stomach is about 2-3 and the pH in the small intestine is about 8. Aspirin will be

options.option: Completely ionised in the small intestine and in the stomach
Unionised in the small intestine and in the stomach
Ionised in the stomach and almost unionised in the small intestine
Ionised in the small intestine and almost unionised in the stomach

questions.explanation: In stomach, pH is 2-3, i.e. strongly acidic and aspirin will be almost unionised here due to common ion effect. However, pH in small intestine is 8, basic, aspirin will be neutralised here.

questions.question: At 90°C, pure water has [H3O+] as 10-6 mol L-1. What is the value of Kw at 90°C ?
questions.explanation: K w = [H3O+ ] [OH-] = 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 10-12

questions.question: The pH of 10-8 M solution of HCl in water is
questions.explanation: No matter, what is the concentration of HCI, its pH will always be less than 7 at 25°C. In the present case, the solution is very dilute, pH will be between 6 and 7.

questions.question: In a basic buffer 0.0025 mole of {tex}N{H_4}Cl{/tex} and 0.15 mole of {tex}N{H_4}OH{/tex} are present. The pH of the solution will be {tex}\left( {p{K_a}} \right){/tex} = 4.74
questions.explanation:
From Henderson's equation,
pOH=pKb​ + {tex}log\frac{[base]}{[salt]}​{/tex}
=4.74 + {tex}log\frac {0.0025​}{0.15}{/tex}
=4.74+ {tex}log\frac{1}{ 60}​{/tex}  
=2.96
pH=14−2.96
=11.04

questions.question: Ionic product of {tex}Ni{(OH)_2}{/tex} is {tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 15}}{/tex}. Molar solubility of {tex}Ni{(OH)_2}{/tex} in 0.10 M NaOH will be
questions.explanation:
Let the solubility of Ni(OH)2​= S
{tex}\underset S{{Ni(OH)}_2}\;+\underset S{H_2O}\;\rightleftharpoons\;Ni^{2+}(aq)+\underset{2S}{2OH^-(aq)}{/tex}
Ksp​ = 2.0×10−16 = [Ni2+][OH−]2
=[S][0.10+2S]2
As Ksp​ is small  2S<<0.10
therefore (0.10+2S) = 0.10
Hence, 2.0×10−15= S(0.10)2
S = 2.0×10−13M = [Ni2+] =[Ni(OH)2​]

questions.question: If pH value of a solution is 3 and by adding water, it becomes 6, then the dilution is increased by
questions.explanation: pH value of Solution is 3 and by adding water, it became 6. Then the dilution is increased by 103 times = 1000 times.

questions.question: The pH of 0.001 (N) acetic acid solution, which is 10% dissociated, is
questions.explanation:
Concentration of acetic acid = 0.001 N for 10% dissociation, the effective concentration of H+ is one-tenth of total concentration.
Therefore, effective concentration = 1/10 x 0.001 N = 0.0001 N
⇒ pH = -log[H+] = 4

questions.question: The pH value of human blood is about
questions.explanation: The pH scale ranges from 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly basic or alkaline). A pH of 7.0, in the middle of this scale, is neutral. Blood is normally slightly basic, with a normal pH range of about 7.35 to 7.45. Usually, the body maintains the pH of blood close to 7.40. to 7.5.

questions.question: The aqueous solution of which of the following salt will have the lowest pH ?
questions.explanation:
{tex}NaCl{O_4} \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO_4^-{/tex}
{tex}NaCl{O_3} \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO_3^-{/tex}
{tex}NaCl{O_2} \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO_2^-{/tex}
{tex}NaClO \rightarrow Na^+ \ + ClO^-{/tex}
In aqueous solution, these compounds decompose into ions. ClO-4 will most easily abstract protons from water molecules. So it will be most acidic.
