questions.question: A solution with pH = 2 is more acidic than one with a pH = 6, by a factor
questions.explanation:
pH = -log [H+]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = -log  [H+], and 6 = -log  [H+]2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{log[H^+]_1}{log[H^+]_2} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}} = 10^{-4} = 10000{/tex}
Therefore, acidity is more at pH = 2 than pH =6 by an order of 10,000.

questions.question: The ionic product of water at {tex}25^\circ C{/tex} is {tex}{10^{ - 14}}{/tex}. Its ionic product at {tex}90^\circ C{/tex} will be
questions.explanation: At high temperatures, the value of the ionic product increases.

questions.question: The pH value of 0.1 M NaOH solution is (when there is a given reaction {tex}\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right] = {10^{ - 14}}){/tex}
questions.explanation:
0.1M NaOH gives 0.1M OH−
pOH=1 pH = 13

questions.question: The concentration of H+ ions in a solution of 1 M NaOH is
questions.explanation:
Since NaOH is a strong base, .1 mol of it will dissociate into 1 mol Na+ and OH-.
By definition [H+]× [OH-] =Kw where Kw, the ionization constant of water, is 10-14. This is why the pH scale is 0–14 and also why looking at the exponent H+ concentration will generally tell you approximately the pH.
So if the concentration of the base (OH-) is 1M, then we can say
Ka= {tex}{10^{ - 14}}\,M{/tex}

questions.question: When the temperature of water will increase then the pH value will
questions.explanation: In water, Hydrogen ions come from cleaving H2O into H+ and OH-. The pH at room temperature is ~7 meaning there are about 10-7 moles of H+ per liter of water at room temperature. As the temperature increases, the ability of water to ionize in this way increases and so the concentration of H+ in solution will increase (and hence the pH will drop).

questions.question: The pH of aqueous solution of ammonium formate is {tex}(p{K_a}\,of{\text{ }}HCOOH = 3.8{\text{ }}and{\text{ }}p{K_b}\,of{\text{ }}N{H_3} = 4.8){/tex}
questions.explanation:
Ammonium formate is a salt of a weak acid and weak base.
{tex}So \ pH=7+ \frac{1}{2}(pK_a​−pK_b​){/tex}
{tex}pH=7+\frac{1}{2}(3.8−4.8){/tex}
pH = 6.5

questions.question: The pH value of decinormal solution of {tex}N{H_4}OH{/tex} which is {tex}20\% {/tex} ionised is
questions.explanation:
Concentration of NH4OH solution = 0.1 N Concentration of OH- ions in solution,
{tex}[OH^-] = 20\% \ of \ 0.1N = \frac {20}{100} \times 0.1= 0.02N{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow pOH = -log[OH^-] = -log(0.02) = 2-0.301 =1.699{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow pH = 14 - 1.699 = 12.301{/tex}

questions.question: At {tex}80^\circ C,{/tex} distilled water has {tex}{H^ \oplus }{/tex} concentration equal to {tex}1 \times {10^{ - 6}}mole/litre.{/tex} The value of {tex}{K_W}{/tex} at this temperature will be
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{mole} / \mathrm{litre}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=\left[1 \times 10^{-6}\right] \times\left[1 \times 10^{-6}\right]=1 \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
The value of Kw is 1 x 10-12.

questions.question: The pH of solution containing 0.10 M sodium acetate and 0.03 M acetic acid is {tex}\left( {p{K_a}\,for{\text{ }}C{H_3}COOH = 4.57} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation:
According to Henderson's equation, 
{tex}pH = pK_a + log \frac{[salt]}{[acid]}{/tex}
{tex}CH_3COOH + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3COONa + H_2O{/tex}
{tex}pH = 4.57 +log \frac{0.10}{0.03} \Rightarrow pH =5.09{/tex}

questions.question: In which of the following acid-base titration, pH is greater than 8 at equivalence point?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{N}^{+} \mathrm{H}_{4}{/tex}
{tex}C H_{3} C O O H+N a O H \rightarrow N a^{+} C H_{3} C O O{/tex}
{tex}H C l+N H_{3} \rightarrow N H_{4} C l{/tex}
{tex}H C l+N a O H \rightarrow N a C l{/tex}
pH greater than 8 at the equivalence point implies that salt formed during the titration is undergoing anionic hydrolysis. Only the salt of a strong base (NaOH) and weak acid (CH3COOH) undergoes anionic hydrolysis to give the basic solution.

questions.question: Which of the following is only acidic in nature?
questions.explanation:
The solution of B(OH)3 in water acts as a weak acid. Thus, it reacts with NaOH solution to produce the sodium salt.
{tex}\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}\left[\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]\Leftrightarrow\underset{Sodium\;metaborate}{Na^+BO_2^-}\;+\;2H_2O{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}

questions.question: Dimethyl glyoxime gives a red precipitate with {tex}N{i^{2 + }},{/tex} which is used for its detection. To get this precipitate readily the best pH range is
questions.explanation: Nickel (Il) forms a precipitate with the organic compound dimethylglyoxime, C4H6(NOH)2. The formation of the red chelate occurs quantitatively in a solution in which the pH is buffered in the range of 5 to 9.

questions.question: Among the following the dissociation constant is highest for
questions.explanation: Dissociation of a proton from CH3−NH3+Cl− is very difficult due to the −I effect of Cl− and N+ while in C6H5CH due easily, similarly due to H-bonding in C6H5CH2OH it can be eliminated and CH3≡CH shows acidic character by triple bond by which proton can be dissociated.

questions.question:
What is the pH of 0.01 M glycine solution? For glycine,
{tex}{K_{{a_1}}} = 4.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,and\,{K_{{a_2}}} = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 10}}{/tex} at 298 K?
questions.explanation:
{tex}K=K_{a_{1}} \times K_{a_{2}}=4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.7 \times 10^{10}{/tex}
{tex}H^{+}=\sqrt{K_{c}}=\sqrt{1.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.01 a}{/tex}
{tex}=0.87 \times 10^{-7}{/tex}
{tex}p H=-\log 0.87 \times 10^{-7}=7-0.93=6.07 = 6.1{/tex}

questions.question: When 10 ml of 0.1 M acetic acid {tex}\left( {p{K_a} = 5.0} \right){/tex} is titrated against 10 ml of 0.1 M ammonia solution{tex}\left( {p{K_b} = 5.0} \right){/tex}, the equivalence point occurs at pH
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{p} K_{a}=-\log K_{a}, \mathrm{p} K_{b}=-\log K_{b}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{pH}=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log K_{a}+\log K_{w}-\log K_{b}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2}\left[-5+\log \left(1 \times 10^{-14}\right)-(-5)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{2}[-5-14+5]=-\frac{1}{2}(-14)=7{/tex}

questions.question: 40 ml of 0.1 M ammonia solution is mixed with 20 ml of 0.1 M HCl. What is the pH of the mixture? ({tex}p{K_b}{/tex} of ammonia solution is 4.74)
questions.explanation:
40mL of 0.1 M ammonia solution = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1
= 4 milli-equivalent ammonia solution
20mL of 0.1 M HCl = 20 {tex}\times{/tex}0.1
= 2 milli-equivalent HCl
{tex}\underset{\underset{Milli-eq.\;after\;reaction}{Initial\;milli-eq}.}\;\;\underset{4-2=2}{\underset4{{\mathrm{NH}}_4\mathrm{OH}}}+\underset0{\underset2{\mathrm{HCl}}}\rightarrow\underset2{\underset0{{\mathrm{NH}}_4\mathrm{Cl}}}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{b}+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}=4.74+\log \frac{2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=4.74+\log 1=4.74{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-4.74=9.26{/tex}

questions.question: A buffer solution contains 0.1 M of acetic acid and 0.1 M of sodium acetate. What will be its pH? (({tex}p{K_b}{/tex} of acetic acid is 4.75)
questions.explanation:
Applying the equation:
{tex}\mathrm{pH}=\log \frac{[\mathrm{Salt}]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}+\mathbf{p} K{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}+\log 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=4.75{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. As the temperature increases, ionisation of water increases, however, the concentration of H+ ions and OH- ions are equal. What will be the pH of pure water at 60oC?
questions.explanation:
At 25oC Kw = 10-14. It increases on heating i.e., Kw > 10-14 at 60oC
But [H+] = [OH-]
Thus [H+]2 > 10-14
or [H+]2 > 10-7 or pH < 7

questions.question: The ionisation constant of an acid, Ka, is the measure of strength of an acid. The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?

options.option: Formic acid > hypochlorous acid > acetic acid
Acetic acid > hypochlorous acid > formic acid
Hypochlorous acid > formic acid > acetic acid
Formic acid > acetic acid > hypochlorous acid

questions.explanation:
Higher is Ka, more will be [H+] for weak acid
[H+] = c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}c \sqrt{\frac{K a}{c}}=\sqrt{K a \cdot c}{/tex} lower will be pH.

questions.question: What will be the value of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 CH3COOH (Ka = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)?
questions.explanation:
[H+] = {tex}c \alpha=c \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{c}}=\sqrt{K_{a} \cdot c}=\sqrt{1.74 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.1}{/tex}
= 1.319 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
pH = 2.88

questions.question: The percentage error in hydronium ion concentration made by neglecting the ionisation of water in 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 M NaOH is:
questions.explanation:
Neglecting ionisation of water
[H+] = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
Also if ionisation of H2O is not neglected then
H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = {tex}\overset{a}{10^{-14}}=\overset{(10^{-6}+a)}a\times(a + 10^{-6}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 9.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % error = {tex}\frac{10 \times 10^{-9}-99 \times 10^{-9}}{9.9 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 1%

questions.question: The use of methyl orange as an indicator in the volumetric determination of the equivalent mass of a weak acid would lead to:

options.option: a high value for the equivalent mass
a low value for the equivalent mass

questions.explanation: Methyl orange will give colour only when more of weak acid is used, i.e., more milli-equivalents are added. Thus, {tex}\frac{w}{E} \times{/tex} 1000 = meq. Thus, E will be lower if meq. are more.

questions.question: The common ion effect is shown by which of the following sets of solutions?
questions.explanation: Common ion effect includes two necessary points; one presence of common ion and the other a weak electrolyte.

questions.question: The pH of a solution is 5.0. To this solution, sufficient acid is added to decrease the pH to 2.0. The increase in hydrogen ion concentration is:
questions.explanation:
[H+]1 = 10-5
[H+]11 = 10-2
Thus, increase in [H+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 1000 times

questions.question: An acid type indicator, HIn differs in colour from its conjugate base (In-). The human, eye is sensitive to colour differences only when the ratio [In-]/[HIn] is greater than 10 or smaller than 0.1. What should be the minimum change in the pH of the solution to observe a complete colour change (Ka = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5):
questions.explanation:
The two conditions when colour of indicator will be visible are derived by
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]}{/tex}

  1. pH = 5 + log 10 = 6
  2. pH = 5 + log 0.1 = 4

Thus, minimum change in pH i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex}pH = (6 - 5) or (5 - 4) = 1

questions.question: The pH at which an acid indicator with Ka = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 changes colour, when the indicator concentrations is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M is:
questions.explanation:
For indicator dissociation equilibrium; being an acid
{tex}\underset{Colour A}HIn \rightleftharpoons H^{+}+\underset{ColourB}In^{-}{/tex}
KIn = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{In}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{Hln}]}{/tex}
The indicator HIn will show colour change at mid-point that is the point at which [In-] = [HIn]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} KIn = [H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
or pH = 5

questions.question: Which aqueous solution will have pH less than 7?
questions.explanation: Fe3+ ions are hydrolysed to develop acidic nature in solution.

questions.question: The pH of the solution, whose hydrogen ion concentration is one mol per litre, will be:
questions.explanation:
[H+] = 1 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log 1 = 0

questions.question: The pH of 0.1 M HCl is approximately 1. The approximate pH of 0.05 M H2SO4 is:
questions.explanation:
[H+] in 0.05 M H2SO4 = 0.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 0.1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 0.1 and pH = 1

questions.question: The pH of a saturated solution of Mg(OH)2 (Ksp of Mg (OH)2 = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12) is:
questions.explanation:
Let solubility of Mg(OH)2 be S mol litre-1
{tex}Mg(OH)_2\rightleftharpoons \underset{s}mg^{2+} = \underset{2s}2OH^-{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Mg2+] [OH-]2 = Ksp
4S3 = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 1.305 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.305 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 3.5832
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 10.4168

questions.question: The pH of water is 7 at 25oC. If water is heated to 50oC, which of the following should be true?
questions.explanation: pH will decrease as Kw will increase and [H+] will increase but [H+] = [OH-] and thus, solution will be neutral or we can say the scale of pH will change.

questions.question: The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 0.1 M HCl and 40 mL of 0.2 M H2SO4 is:
questions.explanation:
Milli equivalent of H+ from HCl = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 1
Milli equivalent of H+ from H2SO4 = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 16
Total meq. of H+ in solution = 1 + 16 =17
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{17}{50}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 {tex}\left(\because\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{Meq} .}{V_{\mathrm{in} \mathrm{mL}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log [H+] = -log 0.34
pH = 0.4685

questions.question: What is the pH of solution after mixing 100 mL 0.1 M CH3COOH and 100 mL 0.04 M CH3COONa Ka for CH3COOH = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
For acidic buffer mixture
pH = -log Ka + {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
[CH3COOH] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.1}{200}{/tex} = 0.05, [CH3COO-] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.4}{200}{/tex} = 0.2
pH = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{02}{0.05}{/tex}
pH = 4.7447 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.05}{/tex}
= 4.7447 + 0.6020 = 5.3467

questions.question: The number of H+ ions present in 10 mL of a solution of pH = 3 are:
questions.explanation:
meq of H+ = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of H+ = {tex}\frac{10^{-2} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}}{1000}{/tex} = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018

questions.question: The resultant pH of a solution on mixing 200 mL of an aqueous solution of HCl (pH = 2.0) with 300 rnL of an aqueous solution of NaOH (pH = 12.0) is:
questions.explanation:
pH of HCl = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCl] = 10-2 M

{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] left from NaOH = {tex}\frac{1}{500}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log OH- = -log 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 2.6989
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 11.3010

questions.question: What is the hydrolysis constant of a salt BCl where B+ is derived from BOH, a weak base? Kb for BOH = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
questions.explanation:
B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+
KH = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-6}}{/tex} = 10-8

questions.question: The pH of pure water at 25oC and 35oC are 7 and 6 respectively. The heat of formation of water from H+ and OH- are:
questions.explanation:
At 250C; [H+] = 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = 10-14
At 35oC; [H+] = 10-6
Kw = 10-12
Now using
2.303 log {tex}\frac{K_{w_{2}}}{K_{w_{1}}}=\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} \times T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
2.303 log {tex}\frac{10^{-12}}{10^{-14}}=\frac{\Delta H}{2}\left[\frac{10}{298 \times 308}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 84551.4 cal/mol
= 84.551 kcal/mol
Thus H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + OH-; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 84.551 kcal/mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} H+ + OH- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2O; {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -84.551 kcal/mol

questions.question: At infinite dilution, the percentage ionisation for both strong and weak electrolytes is:
The pH of solution A, B, C, D are 9.5, 2.5, 3.5 and 5.5 respectively. The most acidic solution is:
questions.explanation: Lesser is the pH, more is acidic nature.

questions.question: The concentration of fluoroacetic acid (Ka of acid = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) which is required to get [H+] = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M is:
questions.explanation:
Given, [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol litre-1
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Ka = {tex}\frac{(C \alpha)(C \alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)}=\frac{C \alpha \cdot \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.634
Now, {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1.50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.634}{/tex} = 2.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
Note: Since Ka is of the order of 10-3 M and thus it is not advisable to use Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}{/tex}. Because (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) is not equal to 1 since {tex}\alpha{/tex} is not small.

questions.question: In water, the acid HClO4, HCl, H2SO4, and HNO3 exhibit the same strength as they are completely ionised in water (a base). This is called ________ of the solvent water.
questions.explanation: It is the definition of Levelling effect.

questions.question: What is the pH of the solution on mixing 100 mL of 0.1 M NH4OH and 100 mL of 0.1 M (NH4)3 PO4? Kb for NH4OH = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
For basic buffer mixture
pOH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid }]}{[\text { Base }]}{/tex}
[base] = [NH4OH] = {tex}\frac{100 \times 0.1}{200}{/tex} = 0.05
[conjugate acid] = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]=\frac{100 \times 0.1 \times 3}{200}{/tex} = 0.15
(NH4)3 PO4 gives three ions of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} (conjugate acid)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.15}{0.05} {/tex}
= 4.7447 + 0.4771 = 5.2218
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 5.2218 = 8.7782

questions.question: pH of K2S solution is:
questions.explanation:
K2S shows alkaline nature due to hydrolysis of S2- ions,
S2- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HS- + OH-

questions.question:
Given, HF + H2O {tex}\stackrel{K_{a}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} H3O+ + F-
F- + H2O {tex}\stackrel{K_{b}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} HF + OH-
Which relation is correct?
questions.explanation:
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{F}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HF}]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
and Kb = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HF}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{F}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka {tex}\times{/tex} Kb = [H3O+] [OH-] = Kw

questions.question: What happens to pH of a solution when NH4Cl crystal is added to a dilute solution of NH4OH?
questions.explanation: Due to the common ion effect, dissociation of NH4OH decreases and thus pH decreases.

questions.question: The relation for calculating pH of a weak base is:
questions.explanation:
For weak base, [OH-] = {tex}\sqrt{K_{b} C}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{\sqrt{K_{b} C}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = pKw + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} pKb - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} log C

questions.question: A weak acid HA after treatment with 12 mL of 0.1 M strong base BOH has a pH of 5. At the end point, the volume of same base required is 26.6 mL. Ka of acid is:
questions.explanation:
For neutralisation:
Total meq. of acid = meq. of base
= 26.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.66
Now for partial neutralisation of acid

The resultant mixture acts as a buffer and [HA] and [BA] or [conjugate base B+] may be placed in terms of Meq. since volume of mixture is constant.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
or 5 = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[1.2]}{[1.46]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka = 8.219 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

questions.question: Approximate pH of 0.10 M aqueous H2S solution when K1 and K2 for H2S at 25oC are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 respectively:
questions.explanation:
pH of 0.1 M H2S solution can be derived by:
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS-; K1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}C \alpha=C \sqrt{\frac{K_{1}}{C}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{K_{1} \times C}=\sqrt{1 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.1}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10^{-8}}{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4

questions.question: pH for the solution of salt undergoing anionic hydrolysis (say CH3COONa) is given by:
questions.explanation:
CH3COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = C {tex}\cdot{/tex} h = {tex}C \sqrt{\frac{K_{H}}{C}}=\sqrt{K_{H} \cdot C}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a}}} C{/tex}
or - log OH = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log Kw + log C - log Ka]
or pOH = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [pKw - log C - pKa]
Now pH + pOH = pKw
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = {tex}\therefore{/tex} [pKw + log C + pKa]

questions.question: The pH of the solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 10-1 N HCl and 10 mL of 10-1 N NaOH is:
questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = 1
meq. of NaOH = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = 1
Thus both are neutralised and 1 meq. of NaCl (a salt of strong acid and strong base) which does not hydrolyse and thus pH = 7

questions.question: 50 mL of 2 N acetic acid mixed with 10 mL of 1 N sodium acetate solution will have an approximate pH of (Ka = 10-5):
questions.explanation:
meq. of acetic acid = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 100
meq. of CH3COONa = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 10
pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
or pH = -log 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 4

questions.question: One litre of water contains 10-7 mole H+ ions. The degree of ionisation of water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\text { number of mole dissociated }}{\text { total mole present }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{10^{-7}}{1000 / 18}{/tex} = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 % (Total mole of H2O in 1 litre = {tex}\frac{1000}{18}{/tex})

questions.question: Ka for the acid HA is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6. The value of K for the reaction A- + H3O+ {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HA + H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Ka = 10-6 for HA + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H3O+ + A-
Thus K for reverse reaction is {tex}\frac{1}{10^{-6}}{/tex} = 106

questions.question: From separate solutions of four sodium salts NaW, NaX, NaY and NaZ had pH 7.0, 9.0, 10.0 and 11.0 respectively, When each solution was 0.1 M, the strongest acid is:
questions.explanation: The salt hydrolysis in each case occurs except NaW because its pH = 7. Thus HW is the strongest acid. More is the pH of salt solution, weaker is its acid part.

questions.question: To separate and identify the ions in a mixture that may contain Pb2+, Cu2+ and Mg2+ use the reagents H2S, NaOH. They should be added in the order:
questions.explanation: HCl precipitates Pb2+ only, H2S precipitates Pb2+and Cu2+ and NaOH precipitates all.

questions.question: pH of a mixture containing 0.10 M X- (base) and 0.20 M HX (salt of X-) is: [pKb (X-) = 4].
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid }]}{[\text { Base }]}{/tex} = 4 + log {tex}\frac{02}{0.1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4 + log 2 and pH = 10 - log 2

questions.question:
Slaked lime, Ca(OH)2 is used extensively in sewage treatment. What is the maximum pH that can be established in Ca(OH)2(aq):
Ca(OH)2 (s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ca(aq) + 2OH- (aq), Ksp = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
questions.explanation:
Ksp of Ca(OH)2 = 4S3 = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{5.5 \times 10^{-6}}{4}}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 2S = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 1.6528
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 12.3471

questions.question: 10 mL of a strong acid solution of pH = 2.000 are mixed with 990 mL of another strong acid solution of pH = 4.000. The pH of the resulting solution will be:
questions.explanation:
[H+] after mixing = {tex}\frac{10^{-2} \times 10+10^{-4} \times 990}{1000}=\frac{0.1+0.0990}{1000}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.1990}{1000}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4 - 0.28 = 3.72

questions.question: For a given solution pH = 6.9 at 60oC where Kw = 10-12. The solution is:
questions.explanation: If Kw = 10-12, then [H+]2 = 10-12 for pure water or [H+] = 10-6 or pH = 6 will show neutral nature at 60oC. Thus, pH = 6.9 is alkaline.

questions.question: Ka for formic acid and acetic acid are 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 and 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively. The relative strength of acids is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} Relative strength of weak acids = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_{a_{1}}}{K_{a_{2}}} \times \frac{C_{1}}{C_{2}}}{/tex}
Assume C1 and C2 are same (Although not given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Relative strength = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_{a_{1}}}{K_{a_{2}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2.1 \times 10^{-4}}{1.1 \times 10^{-5}}}{/tex}
Relative strength for HCOOH to CH3COOH = 4.36 : 1

questions.question: Strong acids are generally used as standard solution in acid-base titrations because:

options.option: the salts of strong acids do not hydrolyse
they form more stable solutions than weak acids
the pH at equivalence point will be 7
they titrate both strong and weak base

questions.explanation: They titrate both strong and weak base

questions.question: At 90oC, pure water has [H3O+] = 10-6 mol/litre. The value of Kw at 90oC is:
questions.explanation:
For pure water, [H+] = [OH-]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = [H+] [OH-] = [H+]2 = (10-6)2 = 10-12

questions.question: Pure ammonia is placed in a vessel at a temperature where its degree of dissociation (a) is appreciable. At equilibrium:

options.option: K_p does not change with pressure

questions.explanation: Kc or Kp is a characteristic value for a given reaction and is independent of P. It however changes with temperature.

questions.question: pH of a 10-8 M solution of HCl in water is:
questions.explanation:
10-10 M HCl furnishes H+ =10-8 M;
Also, 10-7 M H+ are given by H2O and thus, total [H+] = 10-8 + 10-7
= 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH {tex}\approx{/tex} 7

questions.question: A solution of pH 9.0 is one thousand times as basic as a solution of pH:
questions.explanation:
pH = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-9
pH = 6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-6

questions.question: The pH of {tex}\frac 1{1000}{/tex} N KOH solution is:
questions.explanation:
10-3 N KOH will give [OH-] = 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 3
Also, pH + pOH = 14
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 11

questions.question: 0.04 g of pure NaOH is dissolved in 10 litre of distilled water. The pH of the solution is:
questions.explanation:
NNaOH = {tex}\frac{0.04}{40 \times 10}{/tex} = 10-4 N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 10

questions.question: Blood is:
questions.explanation: Blood is alkaline having pH in between 7.0 to 7.4

questions.question: The polyprotic acid is:
questions.explanation: Polyprotic acids furnishes more than one H+ ions in solution.

questions.question: Which solution will have pH closer to 1.0?

options.option: 75 mL of ($\frac M {5}$) HCl + 25 mL of ($\frac M {5}$) NaOH
55 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) HCl + 45 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) NaOH
10 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) HCl + 90 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) NaOH
100 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) HCl + 100 mL of ($\frac M {10}$) NaOH

questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 75 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} = 15
meq. of NaOH = 25 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} meq. of HCl left = 15 - 5 = 10
Now [HCl] left = {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex} = 10-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 1

questions.question: Which of the following will occur if a 0.1 M solution of a weak acid is diluted to 0.01 M at constant temperature?
questions.explanation: On dilution, the degree of dissociation of weak electrolyte increases.

questions.question: A certain buffer solution contains equal concentration of X- and HX. The pKa for HX is 10. The pH of the buffer is:
questions.explanation: pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} = 10 + 0 = 10

questions.question: The pH of a 10-10 M NaOH solution is nearest to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-10}+10^{-7}\\ &\text { (from NaOH) } \quad\left(\text { from } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right) \end{aligned}{/tex}
= 1.001 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH {tex}\approx{/tex} 7 and pH {tex}\approx{/tex} 7

questions.question: In 100 mL of an aqueous HCl of pH 1.0, 900 mL of distilled water is added, the pH of the resultant solution becomes:
questions.explanation:
100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} NHCl = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 2

questions.question: The addition of HCl does not suppresses the ionisation of:
questions.explanation: Common ion effect is noticed only for weak electrolyte dissociation. H2SO4 is strong electrolyte.

questions.question: To a mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate, a further amount of sodium acetate is added. The pH of the mixture:
questions.explanation: pH = - log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of blood is:
questions.explanation: Blood is alkaline having pH between 7.0 to 7.4.

questions.question: The pH of gastric juice is normally:

options.option: less than 1 and greater than zero

questions.explanation: pH of gastric juice is in between 1 and 3.

questions.question:
At 298 K, the equilibrium between N2O4 and NO2 is represented as:
N2O4(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2(g). If the total pressure of the equilibrium mixture is P and the degree of dissociation of N2O4 (g) at 298K is x, the partial pressure of NO2 (g) under these conditions is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{N_2}{O_4}}\limits_1 }\limits_{(1 - x)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2N{O_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{2x} {/tex}
{tex}P_{\mathrm{NO}_{2}}^{\prime}=P_{\text {total }} \times{/tex} mole fraction of NO2 {tex}=\frac{P \cdot 2 x}{(1+x)}{/tex}

questions.question: A 0.01 M ammonia solution is 5% ionized. The concentration of [OH-] ion is:
questions.explanation: [OH-] = {tex}c \cdot \alpha=0.01 \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question: An acid-base indicator has Ka = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The acid form of the indicator is red and the basic form is blue. The [H+] required to change the indicator from 75% red to 75% blue is:
questions.explanation:
{tex} {\left[H^{+}\right]=K_{I n} \times \frac{[{Hin}]}{\left[{In}^{-}\right]}} {/tex}
{tex} \text {Case } I\left[H^{+}\right]_1=3 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{0.75}{0.25}=9 \times 10^{-5} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Case } I I\left[H^{+}\right]_2=3 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{0.25}{0.75}=1 \times 10^{-5} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Change in }\left[H^{+}\right]=8 \times 10^{-5} {/tex}

questions.question: The dissociation constant of NH4OH is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The hydrolysis constant of NH4Cl would be:
questions.explanation:
For hydrolysis of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex};
KH = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{p}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question: The pH of 1.0 M aqueous solution of a weak acid HA is 6.0. Its dissociation constant is:
questions.explanation:
[H+] = 10-6 or {tex}c \alpha{/tex} = 10-6; also, c = 1.0 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-6
Ka = {tex}c \alpha^{2}{/tex} = 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 10-12

questions.question: A certain weak acid has a dissociation constant 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. The equilibrium constant for its reaction with a strong base is:
questions.explanation:
HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, HA + B+ + OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B+ + A- + H2O
Keq. = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{/tex} ...(ii)
By (i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{K_{e q}}{K_{a}}=\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}=\frac{1}{K_{w}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Keq. = {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{K_{w}}=\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-14}}{/tex} = 1010

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of PCI5 ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) obeying the equilibrium: PCl5 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} PCl3 + Cl2 is related to the pressure at equilibrium by:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_1 }\limits_{1 - \alpha } \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {PC{l_3}}\limits_0 }\limits_\alpha + \mathop {\mathop {C{l_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_\alpha {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{p}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}\left(\frac{P}{1+\alpha}\right)^{1}{/tex}
or {tex}K_{p}=\frac{\alpha^{2} P}{1-\alpha^{2}} \text { or } \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{p}}{P}}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\alpha^{2}=1\right){/tex}

questions.question: If pKb for fluoride ion at 25oC is 10.83, the ionization constant of hydrofluoric acid in water at this temperature is:
questions.explanation:
Ka {tex}\times{/tex} Kb {tex}\times{/tex} Kw
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ka = Kw/Kb = {tex}\frac{10^{-14}}{1.48 \times 10^{-11}}{/tex} = 6.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question: Kb for the hydrolysis reaction, B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+ is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6, the hydrolysis constant of the salt is:
questions.explanation: For hydrolysis of B+; KH = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-6}}{/tex} = 10-8

questions.question: If the dissociation constant of an acid HA is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, the pH of a 0.1 M solution of the acid HA will be approximately:
questions.explanation:
[H+] from weak acid,
{tex}=c \cdot \alpha=c \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{c}}=\sqrt{K_{a} c}=\sqrt{10^{-5} \times 0.1}{/tex}
= 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 3

questions.question: The pH of 0.1 M solution of the following salts increases in the order:
questions.explanation: Higher is pH, lesser is acidic nature. Also NH4Cl (aq.) is acidic and NaCN (aq.) is alkaline. NaCl (aq.) is neutral.

questions.question: If in the reaction: N2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NO2, {tex}\alpha{/tex} is degree of dissociation of N2O4, then the number of molecules at equilibrium will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_1 {O_4}}\limits_{1 - \alpha} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2N}\limits_0 {O_2}}\limits_{2 \alpha} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total mole at equilibrium
{tex}=1-\alpha+2 \alpha=1+\alpha{/tex}

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is not true?

options.option: pH + pOH = 14 for all aqueous solution
The conjugate base of H_2$\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{-}$ is HP$\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$
96500 coulomb of electricity is passed through a CuSO_4 solution to deposit 1 g equivalent of Cu at the cathode

questions.explanation: HCl is acid. Its pH will be < 7.

questions.question: The pH of a 0.1 molar solution of the acid HQ is 3. The value of the ionization constant, Ka of this acid is:
questions.explanation:
0.1 M weak acid (HQ) has pH = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} = 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2 ({tex}\because{/tex} C = 10-1)
Now for weak acid,
Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}=C \alpha \cdot \alpha{/tex} = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 10-5

questions.question: The initial rate of hydrolysis of methyl acetate (1 M) by a weak acid (HA, 1M) is 1/100th of that of a strong acid (HX, 1M), at 25oC. The Ka of HA is:
questions.explanation:
Rate = K[Ester] [H+]
For strong acid [H+] = 1 M
For weak acid [H+] = {tex}\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{a}} \cdot C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\text { Rate }_{1}}{\text { Rate }_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{1} \text { for strong acid }}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]_{2} \text { for weak acid }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{1 / 100}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{K_{a} \times 1}}{/tex} (C = 1 M for weak acid)
Ka = 10-4 M

questions.question: How many litres of water must be added to 1 litre of an aqueous solution of HCl with a pH of 1 to create an aqueous solution with pH of 2?
questions.explanation:
meq. of I HCl = meq of II HCl
1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = V {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 10 litre
{tex}\therefore{/tex} volume of water added = (10 -1) = 9L

questions.question: pKa of a weak acid (HA) and pKb of a weak base (BOH) are 3.2 and 3.4, respectively. The pH of their salt (AB) solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (pKa - pKb)
= 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (3.2 - 3.4)
= 6.9

questions.question: The pKa of acetylsalicylic (aspirin) is 3.5. The pH of gastric juice in human stomach is about 2-3 and the pH in the small intestine is about 8. Aspirin will be :

options.option: completely ionised in the small intestine and in the stomach
ionised in the stomach and almost unionised in the small intestine
unionised in the small intestine and in the stomach
ionised in the small intestine and almost unionised in the stomach

questions.explanation: Aspirin an acid will ionise completely in alkaline medium.

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of water at 25oC is 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 % and density is 1.0 g cm-3. The equilibrium constant for water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{e q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{/tex}
for {tex}\underset{1-\alpha}{H_2O}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset\alpha{H^+}\;+\;\underset\alpha{OH^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}C \alpha{/tex} and {tex}C_{\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{2}} \mathrm{O}}=\frac{1000}{18}{/tex} = 55.6
{tex}K_{e q}=\frac{C \alpha \cdot C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}{/tex} (1 - {tex}\alpha \simeq{/tex} 1)
{tex}\therefore K_{e q .}=\frac{\left(55.6 \times 1.9 \times 10^{-9}\right)^{2}}{55.6}{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16

questions.question: The dissociation constant of benzoic acid at 25oC is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. The pH of a 0.01M solution of its sodium salt is:
questions.explanation:
C6H5COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C6H5COOH + OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = CH = {tex}\mathrm{C} \sqrt{\frac{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{\mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \mathrm{C}}{K_{a}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14} \times 0.01}{1 \times 10^{-4}}}{/tex} = 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-8 or pH = 8

questions.question: The dissociation constant of HCN is 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9. The value of hydrolysis constant of KCN will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}K_{h}=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{1.3 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 7.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

questions.question: The compound whose water solution has the highest pH is:
questions.explanation: Na2CO3 is a salt of the weak acid and strong base. Hence its aqueous solution will be strongly basic i.e. pH>7.

questions.question: pH of 0.05 M Mg(OH)2 is:
questions.explanation:
[OH-] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 = 0.1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 1 and pH = 13

questions.question: Aqueous solution of CH3COOH contains:
questions.explanation:
CH3COOH is weak acid and thus, partially ionised,
CH3COOH + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H3O+ + CH3COO-

questions.question: At 80oC, distilled water has [H3O+] concentration equal to 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 mole/litre. The value of Kw at this temperature will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} For water [H+] = [OH-]
Kw = [H3O+] [OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: If {tex}\alpha{/tex} is dissociation constant, then the total number of moles for the reaction 2HI {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2 + I2 will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons & \mathrm{H}_{2} & +\mathrm{I}_{2} \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1-2 \alpha & \alpha & \alpha \end{array}{/tex}
Total mole at equilibrium {tex}=1-2 \alpha+\alpha+\alpha{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The pH value of blood does not appreciably change by a small addition of an acid or a base, because the blood:

options.option: Contains serum protein that acts as buffer.
Contains iron as apart of molecule.

questions.explanation: Serum protein in blood acts as buffer solution that restricts the change in pH value of blood after the addition of small amount of acid and base.

questions.question: The pH of 10M HCl aqueous solution is
questions.explanation:
The pH of a solution is calculated using the formula:
{tex} {pH}=-\log \left[{H}^{+}\right]{/tex}
For a strong acid like HCl, it dissociates completely in water, meaning:
{tex} \left[{H}^{+}\right]=10 {M}{/tex}
Now, applying the formula:
{tex} {pH}=-\log (10)=-1{/tex}

questions.question: The pH value of {tex}\frac N{10}{/tex} NaOH solution is:
The hydride ion H- is a stronger base than its hydroxide ion OH-. Which of the following reaction will occur if sodium hydride (NaH) is dissolved in water?
questions.explanation: {tex}\underset{Base}{H^-\;}+\;\underset{Acid}{H_2O}\;\rightarrow H_2\;+\;OH^-{/tex}

questions.question: The concentration of [H+] and concentration of [OH-] of a 0.1 M aqueous solution of 2% ionised weak acid is: [ionic product of water = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14]
questions.explanation:
[H+] = {tex}c \alpha{/tex} = 0.1 {tex}\times \frac{2}{100}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
Also, [H+] {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-] = 10-14

questions.question: Which statement is wrong about pH and H+?

options.option: Mixing solution of CH_3COOH and HCl, pH will be less than 7
Adding 1N solution of CH_3COOH and 1N solution of NaOH, pH will be seven
pH of neutral water is not zero
[H^+] of dilute and hot H_2SO_4 is more than concentrated and cold H_2SO_4

questions.explanation:
Mixing will produce 1N CH3COO Na CH3COO- will hydrolyze to give alkaline solution [pH > 7]
CH3COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + H+

questions.question: Correct relation between dissociation constants of a dibasic acid is:
questions.explanation: Ka1 of a polyprotic acid is always greater than Ka2

questions.question: Ionisation constant of CH3OOH is 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and concentration of H+ ions is 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. Then find out the initial concentration of CH3COOH molecules.
questions.explanation:
{tex}C\underset{c(1-\alpha)}{H_3COO}H\;\rightleftharpoons\;C\underset{c\alpha}{H_3CO}O^-\underset{3.4\times10^{-4}}{\;+\underset{c\alpha}{\;H^+}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\text { c. } \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{c}}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or {tex}\sqrt{K_{a} \cdot c}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or c = {tex}\frac{\left(3.4 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{2}}{1.7 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 6.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question: Solution of 0.1 N NH4OH and 0.1 N NH4Cl has pH 9.25, then find out pKb of NH4OH.
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}{/tex}
(14 - 9.25) = pKb + log {tex}\frac{0.1}{0.1}{/tex}
pKb = 4.75

questions.question: Which has the highest pH?
questions.explanation:
Due to hydrolysis of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} ions.
{tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} NH4OH + H+ acidic, less pH
{tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 2H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2CO3 + 2OH- alkaline, more OH-
CH3COO- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COOH + OH- alkaline, less OH-

questions.question: The rapid change of pH near the stoichiometric point of an acid-base titration is the basic of indicator detection. pH of the solution is related to the ratio of the concentration of conjugate acid [HIn] and base [In-]:

options.option: $\stackrel{4}{ C } H \equiv \stackrel{3}{ C }-\stackrel{2}{ C } H =\stackrel{1}{ C } H _2$

questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for acid indicators is as follows:
{tex}H _{ In } \rightleftharpoons H ^{+}+ In ^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K _{ In }=\frac{\left[ H ^{+}\right]\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{\left[ H _{\text {In }}\right]}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow\left[ H ^{+}\right]=\frac{ K _{\text {In }}[ HIn ]}{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{/tex}
Take -log on both sides
{tex}-\log \left[ H ^{+}\right]=-\log \left(\frac{ K _{\text {In }}[ HIn ]}{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}\right) {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow pH =-\log K _{\text {In }}+\log \frac{\left[ In ^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]}{/tex}
{tex}pH =p K _{\text {In }}+\log \frac{\left[\text { In }^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{\left[\text { In }^{-}\right]}{[ HIn ]}=p H -p K _{\text {In }}{/tex}

questions.question:
The values of Kp1 and Kp2 for the reactions
{tex}X \rightleftharpoons Y+Z{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}A \rightleftharpoons 2 B{/tex} ...(ii)
are in the ratio 9 : 1. If degree of dissociation of X and A be equal, then total pressure at equilibrium (i) and (ii) are in the ratio:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {\mathop X\limits_{1 - \alpha } }\limits_{} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop Y\limits_\alpha }\limits_{} + \mathop {\mathop Z\limits_\alpha ;}\limits_{} {/tex} {tex}K_{c_{1}}=\frac{[Y][Z]}{[X]}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)} \cdot\left[\frac{P_{1}}{1+\alpha}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\mathop A\limits_{1 - \alpha } }\limits_{} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {2B}\limits_\alpha ;}\limits_{} {/tex} {tex}K_{c_{2}}=\frac{[B]^{2}}{[A]}=\frac{4 \alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)} \cdot\left[\frac{P_{2}}{1+\alpha}\right]^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{c_{1}}}{K_{c_{2}}}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\alpha^{2} \times(1-\alpha)}{(1-\alpha) \times 4 \alpha^{2}} \times \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{36}{1}{/tex}

questions.question: At 25oC, the dissociation constant of a base, BOH is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12. The concentration of hydroxyl ions in 0.01 M aqueous solution of the base would be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{(1-\alpha)}{BOH}\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{c\alpha}{B^+\;}+\;\underset{c\alpha}{OH^-}{/tex}
Kb = {tex}\frac{\left[B^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{B \mathrm{OH}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{BOH}]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}}{0.01}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question: The correct relationship between the pH of isomolar solutions of sodium oxide (pH1), sodium sulphide (pH2), sodium selenide (pH3) and sodium telluride (pH4) is:
questions.explanation: Acidic character of non-metals ions increases down the group.

questions.question: The hydrogen ion concentration of a 10-8 M HCl aqueous solution at 298 K (Kw = 10-14) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}HCl\;\rightarrow\underset{10^{-8}}{H^+}\;+\;Cl^-{/tex}
{tex}H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons\underset{10^{-8}+a}{H^+\;}+\underset a{\;OH^-\;}{/tex}The dissociation of H2O is suppressed due to common ion effect
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw = [H+] [OH-]
or 10-14 = (10-8 + a) {tex}\times{/tex} a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H-] = 10-8 + 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question: A monoprotic acid in 0.1 M solution has Ka = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The degree of dissociation of acid is:
questions.explanation:
Ka = {tex}c \alpha^{2}{/tex} or 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = 0.1 {tex}\times \alpha^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \alpha{/tex} = 10-2 or 1%

questions.question: Calculate the pOH of a solution at 25oC that contains 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M of hydronium ions?
questions.explanation:
pH = -log [H+] = -log (10-10) = 10
At 25oC, pH + pOH = 14
10 + pOH = 14
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pOH = 14 - 10 = 4

questions.question: Equal volumes of three acid solutions of pH 3, 4 and 5 are mixed in a vessel. What will be the H+ ion concentration in the mixture?
questions.explanation:
The problem is valid only if strong acids are mixed. After mixing equal volumes of three acids, total volume = 3 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] after mixing = {tex}\frac{10^{-3} \times V}{3 V}+\frac{10^{-4} \times V}{3 V}+\frac{10^{-5} \times V}{3 V}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.11 \times 10^{-3}}{3}{/tex} = 3.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: Equimolar solutions of the following were prepared in water separately. Which one of the solutions will record the highest pH?
questions.explanation: All of the given salts have same anion i.e., Cl which on hydrolysis gives HCl which is a strong acid. Now, among the salts which have cation that gives a strongest base on hydrolysis of salt have the highest pH value. As Ba form Ba(OH)2 which is a stronger base thus, it results in the highest pH value.

questions.question: The ionization constant of ammonium hydroxide is 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 at 298 K. Hydrolysis constant of ammonium chloride is:
questions.explanation: KH = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{1.77 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 5.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question: The equivalent conductance of {tex}\frac{M}{32}{/tex} solution of a weak monobasic acid is 8.0 mhos cm2 eq-1 and at infinite dilution is 400 mhos cm2 eq-1. The dissociation constant of this acid is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{v}}{\Lambda_{\infty}}=\frac{8}{400}{/tex} = 0.02
Now Ka = {tex}c \alpha^{2}=\frac{1}{32} \times{/tex} (0.02)2
= 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: What is [H+] in mol/L of a solution that is 0.20 M in CH3COONa and 0.10 M in CH3COOH? (Ka for CH3COOH is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
= -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.1}{/tex}
= 5.045
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6

questions.question: A buffer solution is prepared in which the concentration of NH3 is 0.30 M and the concentration of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} is 0.20 M. If the equilibrium constant, Kb for NH3 equals 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 what is the pH of this solution? (log 2.7 = 0.43)
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid] }]}{[\text { base }]}{/tex}
= - log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.3}{/tex}
= - log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log 0.66
= 4.7447 - 0.176 = 4.5685
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 4.5686 = 9.43

questions.question: Which of the following salts will give the highest pH in water?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 2H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H2CO3 + 2OH-
Na2CO3 is salt of a strong base and weak acid. Thus pH > 7.

questions.question: Hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L in a solution of pH = 5.4 will be:
questions.explanation:
pH = 5.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -log H+ = 5.4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 3.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 M

questions.question: 0.1 mole of CH3NH2 (Kb = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) is mixed with 0.08 mole of HCl and diluted to 1 litre, The [H+] in solution is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{\underset{0.02}{0.1}}{CH_3NH_2}\;+\;\underset{\underset0{0.08}}{HCl}\;\rightarrow CH_3\underset{\underset{0.08}0}{NH_3^+Cl^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log Kb + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate acid }]}{[\text { Base }]}{/tex}
= - log 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 + log {tex}\frac{[0.08]}{0.02}{/tex}
= 3.9031
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-14}}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 M

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true?

options.option: In aqueous medium HF is a stronger acid than HCl
HClO_4 is a weaker acid than HClO_3
H_3PO_3 is a stronger acid than H_2SO_3
HNO_3 is a stronger acid than HNO_2

questions.explanation: Higher is the oxidation number of non-metal in oxoacid, more is acidic nature.

questions.question: The pKa of weak acid HA is 4.5. The pOH of an aqueous buffer solution of HA in which 50% of the acid is ionised:
questions.explanation:
pH = 4.5 + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} [Salt] = [Acid], since [A-] = [HA]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 9.5

questions.question: The first and second dissociation constant of an acid HA2 are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 respectively. The overall dissociation constant of acid is:
questions.explanation: K = K1 {tex}\times{/tex} K2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of a weak acid is 1.34% at 0.1 M concentration. Its dissociation constant is:
questions.explanation: Ka = {tex}C \alpha^{2}{/tex} = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2)2 = 1.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: The pKa of a weak acid HA, is 4.80. The pKb of a weak base, B OH, is 4.78. The pH of an aqueous solution of the corresponding salt, BA, will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{1-h}{B^+\;}+\;\underset{1-h}{A^-}\;+\;H_2O\;\rightleftharpoons B\underset HOH\;+\;H\underset hA{/tex}
HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
[H+] = {tex}\frac{K_{a} \times[\mathrm{H} A]}{\left[A^{-}\right]}=\frac{K_{a} \times h}{1-h}=K_{a} \times \sqrt{K_{H}}{/tex}
{tex}=K_{a} \times \sqrt{\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a} \times K_{b}}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \times K_{a}}{K_{b}}}{/tex}
or pH = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [pKb + pKa - pKb] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [14 + 4.8 - 4.78]
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 14.02 = 7.01

questions.question: 2.5 mL of {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} M weak mono acidic base (Kb = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 25oC) is titrated with {tex}\frac{2}{15}{/tex} M HCl in water at 25oC. The concentration of H+ at equivalent point is: (Kw = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 at 25oC)
questions.explanation:
BOH + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} BCl + H2O
At equivalent point: meq of BOH = meq of HCl
2.5 {tex}\times \frac{2}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = V {tex}\times \frac{2}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 7.5 mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total volume = 2.5 + 7.5 = 10 mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} meq of BCl formed = {tex}\frac{2.5 \times 2}{5}{/tex} = 1
[BCl] = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} = 0.1 M
B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+
[H+] = c {tex}\cdot{/tex} h
Also, {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{1-h}=\frac{K_{H}}{C}=\frac{K_{w}}{K_{b} \times c}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-12} \times 0.1}{/tex} = 0.1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = 0.27
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 0.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

questions.question: At 25oC, the solubility product of Mg(OH)2 is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11. At which pH, will Mg2+ ions start precipitating in the form of Mg(OH)2 from a solution of 0.001 M Mg2+ ions?
questions.explanation:
Ksp Mg(OH)2 = [Mg2+] [OH-]2
1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = 0.001 {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-]2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4
and pH = 10

questions.question: What is the pH of the resulting solution when equal volumes of 0.1 M NaOH and 0.01 M HCl are mixed?
questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} V
meq. of NaOH = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} meq. of NaOH left = 0.1 V - 0.01 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [NaOH] left = {tex}\frac{0.09 \mathrm{V}}{2 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.045 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log [OH-] = - log [0.045] = 1.35
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 12.65

questions.question: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion (C5H5N+H) in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution (Kb for C5H5N = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9) is:
questions.explanation:
C5H5N + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} C5H5N+H + OH-
For pyridine, C = 0.1 M and Kb = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{b}}{C}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1.7 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
%{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 0.013

questions.question:
Following solutions were prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations :

  1. 60 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 40 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}
  2. 55 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 45 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}
  3. 75 ml {tex}\frac{M}{5}{/tex} HCl + 25 mL {tex}\frac{M}{5}{/tex}
  4. 100 ml {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex} HCl + 100 mL {tex}\frac{M}{10}{/tex}

pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.explanation:
meq. of HCl = 75 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = 15
meq. of NaOH = 25 {tex}\times \frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 1 = 5
meq. of HCl left in resulting solution = 10
millimole of H+ left = 10
Molarity of [H+] in resulting mixture = {tex}\frac{10}{100}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
pH = -log [H+] = -log {tex}\left[\frac{1}{10}\right]{/tex} = 1.0

questions.question: Acetic acid and propionic acid have Ka values 1.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively at a certain temperature. An equimolar solution of a mixture of the two acids is partially neutralised by NaOH. How is the ratio of the contents of acetate and propionate ions related to the Ka values?

options.option: The ratio is unrelated to the K_a values
The ratio is unrelated to the pH of the solution
The ratio is related to the molarity
$\left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\right)=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left(\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are ionised fraction of the acids

questions.explanation:
In a given mixture, the ionisation of two acids can be written as: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} be degree of ionisation at same concentration.
{tex}\underset{1-\alpha}{CH_3COOH}\;\rightleftharpoons C\underset\alpha{H_3C}OO^-+\underset{\alpha+\beta}{H^+}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{1-\beta}{C_2H_5COOH}\;\rightleftharpoons C_2\underset\beta{H_5C}OO^-+\underset{\alpha+\beta}{H^+}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore K_{\mathrm{A.A.}}=\frac{[\alpha][\alpha+\beta] \cdot c}{[1-\alpha]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{P.A.}}=\frac{[\beta][\alpha+\beta] \cdot c}{[1-\beta]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\mathrm{A.A}}}{K_{\mathrm{P.A.}}}=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \times \frac{(1-\beta)}{\beta}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left[\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: The composition of an acidic buffer mixture made up of HA and NaA. of total molarity 0.29 having pH = 4.4 and Ka = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 in terms of concentration of salt and acid respectively is:
questions.explanation:
pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
LEt a mol litre-1 be a concentration of salt, then concentration of acid = (0.29 - a)
4.4 = -log 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 + log {tex}\frac{a}{(029-a)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 0.09
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Conjugate base] = [Salt] = 0.09 M
[Acid] = 0.29 - 0.09 = 0.20 M

questions.question: To prepare a buffer of pH 8.26, amount of (NH4)2 SO4 to be added into 500 mL of 0.01 M NH4OH solution [pKa {tex}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right){/tex} = 9.26] is:
questions.explanation:
Mixing of NH4OH and (NH4)2SO4 gives a basic buffer.
pOH = pKb + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right]}{/tex};
{tex}\mathrm{p} K_{a}{ }_{\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}}{/tex} = 9.26 {tex}\therefore \mathrm{p} K_{b_{\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}}}{/tex} = 4.74
5.74 = 4.74 + log {tex}\frac{a}{5}{/tex}; where a is millimole of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} obtained on mixing
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 50
{tex}\therefore{/tex} millimole of (NH4)2 SO4 = 25
or mole of (NH4)2 SO4 = 0.025

questions.question: Percentage ionisation of weak acid can be calculated using the formula:
questions.explanation:
For weak acid dissociation equilibria, degree of dissociation a is given as:
{tex}\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{C}} \therefore \% \alpha=100 \sqrt{\frac{K_{a}}{C}}{/tex}
Also, Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \cdot C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \cdot \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
log Ka = log H+ + log {tex}\frac{\alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
or pKa = pH + log {tex}\frac{(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}{/tex}
or pKa - pH = log {tex}\frac{(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}=10^{\mathrm{pK}_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{\alpha}=10^{\mathrm{pK}_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}{/tex} + 1
or {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{\left(1+10^{\mathrm{p} K_{a}-\mathrm{pH}}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question: The self ionisation constant for pure formic acid, K = [HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] [HCOO-] has been estimated as 10-6 at room temperature. The density of formic acid is 1.22 g/cm3. The percentage of formic acid molecules in pure formic acid are converted to formate ion:
questions.explanation:
Given density of formic acid = 1.22 g/cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of formic acid in 1 litre solution = 1.22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 g
Thus, [HCOOH] {tex}\frac{1.22 \times 10^{3}}{46}{/tex} = 26.5 M
Since in case of auto ionisation
[HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] [HCOO-] and [HCOO-] [HCOO{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}{/tex}] = 10-6
[HCOO-] = 10-3
Now% dissociation of HCOOH
{tex}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right] \times 100}{[\mathrm{HCOOH}]}=\frac{10^{-3}}{26.5} \times{/tex} 100
= 0.004% {tex}\left[\alpha=\frac{C \alpha}{C(1-\alpha)}\right]{/tex}

questions.question:
Liquid ammonia ionises to a slight extent. At -50oC, its self ionisation constant, {tex}K_{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex} = 10-30. How many amide ions, are present per cm3 of pure liquid ammonia?
Assume N = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023.
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}{/tex} (self ionisation) and K = {tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}\right]=\sqrt{K}=\sqrt{10^{-30}}{/tex} = 10-15 M
Number of amide ions in 103 cm3 = 10-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
Number of amide ions in one cm3
{tex}=\frac{10^{-15} \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{10^{3}}{/tex}
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 ions

questions.question: The ratio of dissociation constants of two weak acids HA and HB is 4. At what molar concentration ratio, the two acids will have same pH?
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{a}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} [H+] = [A-])
and {tex}K_{b}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[B^{-}\right]}{[H B]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{[\mathrm{H} B]}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} [H+] [B-])
Also, H+ are same
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{a}}{K_{b}}=\frac{[\mathrm{H} B]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]}{[\mathrm{HB}]}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: 50% neutralisation of a solution of formic acid (Ka = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) with NaOH would result in a solution having a hydrogen ion concentration of:
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
pH = pKa + log 1 ({tex}\because{/tex} 50% neutralisation)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -log H+ = -log 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
or H+ = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: pH of a mixture of 1 M benzoic acid (pKa = 4.20) and 1 M sodium benzoate is 4.5. In 300 mL buffer solution, volume of benzoic acid is (log 2 = 0.3):
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\left(300-V_{1}\right) \times 1}{1 \times V_{1}}{/tex}; Let V1 be volume of benzoic acid and thus (300 - V1) is volume of benzoate
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 45 = 4.2 + log{tex}\frac{300-V_{1}}{V_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{300-V_{1}}{V_{1}}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V1 = 100 mL

questions.question: If the equilibrium constant for the reaction of weak acid HA with strong base is 109, then pH of 0.1 M NaA is:
questions.explanation:
HA + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NaA + H2O;
or HA + OH- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A- + H2O
Keq. = 109 = {tex}\frac{\left[A^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
Also, HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\mathrm{eq.}}}{K_{a}}=\frac{1}{K_{w}}{/tex} or Ka = 109 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 = 10-5
Thus for A- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HA + OH-
[OH-] = Ch = {tex}\mathcal{C} \sqrt{\frac{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{K_{w} \cdot C}{K_{a}}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14} \times 0.1}{10^{-5}}}{/tex} = 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-9
and pH = 9

questions.question: 10 mL of 10-6 M HCI solution is mixed with 90 mL H2O. pH will change approximately:
questions.explanation:
pH of 10-6 M HCl = 6; on dilution
[HCl] = {tex}\frac{10^{-6} \times 10}{100}{/tex} = 10-7
Thus new pH of HCl is not 7 because it is acid.
For new solution,
pH : [H+] = 10-7 + 10-7 (from H2O) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 6.7

questions.question: What is the [OH-] in the final solution prepared by mixing 20.0 mL of 0.050 M HCl with 30.0 mL of 0.10 M Ba(OH)2?
questions.explanation: {tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = {tex}\frac{5}{50}{/tex} = 0.1

questions.question: How many mole of HCl are required to prepare one litre of buffer solution (containing NaCN + HCl) of pH 8.5 using 0.01 g formula weight of NaCN (KHCN = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10)?
questions.explanation:
NaCN + HCl is not a buffer but if HCl is in less amount then, it gives a buffer as it produces HCN.

This is buffer of HCN + NaCN
Let a moles of HCl be used for this purpose
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{0.01-a}{a}{/tex}
8.5 = -log 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 + log {tex}\frac{0.010-a}{a}{/tex}
a = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 moles of HCl

questions.question:
When CO2 dissolves in water the following equilibrium is established.
CO2 + 2H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex}  H3O+ + {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} for which the equilibrium constant is 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and pH = 6.0. The ratio of {tex}\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]{/tex}/ [CO2] is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{p}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}{/tex} = 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
and [H3O+] = 10-6
{tex}\therefore \frac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]}=\frac{3.8 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-6}}{/tex}
= 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 = 0.38

questions.question: If NaOH is titrated with HCl, a variation of conductance (y-axis) with the addition of HCl (x-axis) will be:

When CO2 dissolves in water the following equilibrium is established. CO2 + 2H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex}  H3O+ + {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} for which the equilibrium constant is 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 and pH = 6.0. The ratio of [HCO3] / [CO2] is:
questions.explanation: A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} D {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} E

questions.question: Find out the solubility of Ni(OH)2 in 0.1 M NaOH. Given that the ionic product of Ni(OH)2 is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−15.
questions.explanation:
Ni(OH)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons\mathop {N{i^2}}\limits_{(S)} + 2{\mathop {OH}\limits_{(2S)} {^-} }S{/tex} = molar conc. of Ni (OH)2
NaOH {tex}\to{/tex} Na+ + {tex}\mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_{(0.1M)} {/tex}
KSP = [Ni2+] [OH-]2
= (S) (2S + 0.1)2
= (S) (4S2 + 0.01 + 0.4S)
= 4S3 + 0.01S + 0.4S2
Neglecting higher power of S,
2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 = 0.01S
S = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 M

questions.question: pH of a saturated solution of Ca(OH)2 is 9. The solubility product (Ksp) of Ca(OH)2 is:
questions.explanation:
{tex} {Ca}({OH})_2 \rightleftharpoons \underset{(S)}{{Ca}^{+2}}+\underset{(2 {~S})}{2 {OH}^{-}}{/tex}

{tex}p H{/tex} of saturated solution {tex}=9{/tex}

{tex} p H=9,\left[H^{+}\right]=10^{-9}{/tex}

Hence {tex}\left[O H^{-}\right]=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-9}}=10^{-5}{/tex}

{tex} 2 s=10^{-5}, s=\frac{10^{-5}}{2} {/tex}
{tex} k_{s p} \text { for } C a(O H)_2 \Rightarrow 4 s^3 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 4 \times\left(\frac{(10)^{-5}}{2}\right)^3 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{10^{-15}}{2}=0.5 \times 10^{-15} {/tex}

questions.question:
Alkyl amine RNH2(g) shows the following equilibrium in water at 27°C and 0.5 bar pressure.
RNH2(g) + H2O(l) {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{RNH}_{3}^{+}{/tex}(aq) + OH- Kc = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
What is the pH of solution?

6HCHO {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C6H6O6). What is the degree of association of formaldehyde?
Which one is incorrect during titration of an acid against a base at the equivalent point?

options.option: The larger the K_b of base, more is alkaline nature
The smaller is K_a of the acid, lesser is alkaline nature
The weaker the acid, more is alkaline nature
The stronger is base, more is alkaline nature

questions.explanation: Ka is directly proportional to the acidity of the acid and inversely proportional to the alkaline nature, so, lesser the Ka ​value more will be alkaline nature.

questions.question:
Which statement is wrong about {tex}R - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} H - COOH{/tex}, if the following equilibrium exists?
{tex}\mathop {R - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{NH_3^ + } - COOH}\limits_{Conjugate\,\,acid\,\,of\,Zwitter\,\,ion} R - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{NH_3^ + } }\limits_{Zwitter\,ion} - CO{O^ - }R - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{N{H_2}} - CO{O^ - }}\limits_{Conjugate\,base\,\,of\,Zwitter\,ion\,} + {H^ + }{/tex}

options.option: The pH of aqueous solution depends upon concentration of $R - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} H - COOH$
The concentration of [H^+] for Zwitter ion can be calculated for any amphoteric salt such as $HCO_3^ - $.

questions.explanation: The pH of zwitterionic molecules do not dependend upon their concentration.

questions.question: Rate of diffusion of ozonised oxygen is {tex}\sqrt {0.80}{/tex} times that of pure oxygen. What is degree of ozonization for oxygen?
A buffer solution of lowest pH is obtained by mixing equimolar amount of:
questions.explanation: HCOOH + HCOONa

questions.question: The pH of a solution formed by mixing 40 mL of 0.1 M HCl with 10 mL of 0.45 M of NaOH is:
questions.explanation:
Meq. of HCl = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 4; Meq. of NaOH = 0.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 4.5
HCl + NaOH {tex}\to{/tex} NaCl + H2O
H+ and OH- neutralise each other
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Meq. of NaOH left = 4.5 - 4 = 0.5
Total volume (ml) = 50 ml
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = {tex}\frac{0.5}{50}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
pOH = -log [OH-]
= -log [10-2]
= 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 2 and pH = 12

questions.question: The pH of 10 MHCl aqueous solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = -log10 [H+]
pH = -1
A 10M HCl solution will not be fully dissociated (a {tex}\neq{/tex} 1). Therefore its pH will be less than zero but not -1.

questions.question: The relative strength of two weak acids HCOOH (Ka = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) and HF (Ka = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) is:
Which of the following will give maximum number of ions in solution?
questions.explanation:
FeCl3
The dissociation of potassium iodate gives two ions.
KI3 {tex}\to{/tex} K+ + {tex}{\text{I}}_{\text{3}}^{\text{ - }}{/tex}
Dissociation of cpper sulfate gives two ions.
CuSO4 {tex}\to{/tex} Cu2+ + {tex}{\text{SO}}_{\text{4}}^{\text{ 2- }}{/tex}
Dissociation of ferric chloride gives four ions.
FeCl3 {tex}\to{/tex} Fe3+ + 3Cl-
Dissociation of K2HgI4 gives three ions.
K2HgI4 {tex}\to{/tex} 2K+ + [HgI4]2-

questions.question: The initial rate of hydrolysis of methyl acetate (1M) by a weak acid (HA, 1M) is 1/100th of that of a strong acid (HX, 1M), at 25oC. The Ka of HA is:
questions.explanation:
Rate of hydrolysis in weak acid = {tex}\frac{1}{100} \times{/tex} Rate of hydrolysis in strong acid.
[H+] weak acid = {tex}\frac{1}{100} \times{/tex} [H+] strong acid = {tex}\frac{1}{100} \times{/tex} 1M = 10-2 M
[H+] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.01 M
1({tex}\alpha{/tex}) = 0.01
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.01
Ka = C{tex}\alpha{/tex}2 = 1(0.01)2 = 10-4

questions.question: The pH of 0.01 M solution of sodium salt of a substituted benzoic acid at 25oC is: (Ka = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 at 25oC)
questions.explanation:
The hydrolysis reaction of conjugate base of acid is
{tex}A_{(a q)}^{-}{/tex} + H2O {tex}\to{/tex} HO- + HA
Kh = {tex}\frac{K_{w}}{K_{a}}=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-4}}{/tex} = 10-10
Since, degree of hydrolysis is negligible;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = {tex}\sqrt{K_{h} C}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10^{-10} \times 10^{-2}}=\sqrt{10^{-12}}{/tex} = 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = {tex}10^{-\left[O H^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 6
pH = 14 - 6 = 8

questions.question: What is the ratio of F- and HCOO- in a mixture of 0.2 MHCOOH and 0.1 M HF? (Ka for HF = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 and Ka for HCOO- = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
Three aqueous solution A, B and C are prepared by adding NaCl, NaOH and HCl respectively. The ionic product of water will:

increase in C, decrease in A and B
increase in B, decrease in A and C
remains same in all the three

questions.explanation: remains same in all the three

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of PCl5({tex}\alpha{/tex}) obeying the equilibrium, PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g), is approximately related to the pressure at equilibrium by:
questions.explanation: {tex}\alpha \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of the solution at the neutralisation point of 0.1 M NH4OH with 0.1 NHCl would be:

questions.explanation: The hydrolysis of a salt formed from a strong acid and a weak base in solution is suppressed by the addition of the strong acid.

The pH of pure water at a given temperature is 6.8. If 100 mL of 0.02 M HCl and 100 mL of 0.02 M NH4OH are mixed at that temperature, the pH of mixture will be: [Ka of {tex}(NH_4^ + ){/tex} = 10-8]
Kw for pure water is 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 and 2.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 at 10oC and 34oC respectively. The heat of neutralisation of water is:

pH of 0.01 mol dm-3 CH3COOH (Ka = 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5):
questions.explanation:
pH for weak acid is given by:
pH = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [pKa - logC]
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [-log (1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) - log 0.01]

questions.question: 0.1 M aqueous solution of BOH (a weak base) has pH equal to 12 at 27oC. Assuming Kw at 27oC 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14, calculate the osmotic pressure of 0.1 M BOH(aq.)
Ka for CH3COOH is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and Kb for NH4OH is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. The pH of ammonium acetate will be:
questions.explanation:
pH = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (pKa - pKb)
Ka = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
pKa = -log (1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) = 5 - log 1.8
= 4.744
pKb = -log(1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) = 4.744
pH = 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(pKa - pKb) = 7

questions.question: Select the correct choice when 100 mL of 0.5 M hydrazoic acid (N3H having Ka = 3. 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) and 400 mL of 0.1 M cyanic acid (HOCN having Ka = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4) are mixed:
questions.explanation: all of these

questions.question: The pH of pure water at 25oC is 7.0. What will be the pH of pure water at 60oC?
questions.explanation: At higher temperature pH and pOH of pure water decreases so if will be less than 7. It doesn't mean water is acidic it is always neutral as pH = pOH

questions.question: The Ka values of acetic acid, hypochlorous acid and formic acid are 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 respectively. Which of the following orders of pH of 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions of these acids is correct?

options.option: Acetic acid > Hypochlorous acid > Formic acid
Formic acid > Hypochlorous acid > Acetic acid
Formic acid > Acetic acid > Hypochlorous acid
Hypochlorous acid > Acetic acid > Formic acid

questions.explanation:
Ka is a measure of the strength of the acid i.e., larger the value of Ka, the stronger is the acid.
Thus, the correct order of acidic strength is
HCOOH > CH3COOH > HClO
Ka: 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 > 1.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 > 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
Stonger the acid, lesser will be the value of pH. Hence, the correct order of pH is HClO > CH3COOH > HCOOH

  2. 0.1 M CH3COONH4

questions.explanation: {tex} \text { iv }\left({NH}_4 {Cl}\right)<{i}\left({CH}_3 {COONH}_4\right){/tex} {tex}<{ii}\left({HCOONH}_4\right)<{iii}\left({CH}_3 {COONa}\right){/tex}

pOH of 0.003 M HCl is:

questions.question:
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} _1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_ 2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} are the respective dissociations for the following reactions:
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS-
HS- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + S2-
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} _1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_ 2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + HS- {tex}K_{a_1}{/tex}
HS- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + S2- {tex}K_{a_2}{/tex}
H2S {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + S2- {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex}
{tex}K_{a_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[{H S}^{-}\right]\left[{H}^{+}\right]}{\left[{H}_{2} {S}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K_{a_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{HS}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_ 3}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_ 1} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} _2}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of a solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 0.1 M HCl and 10 mL of 0.01 M Sr(OH)2.
If heat of ionisation {tex}\Delta{/tex}iHo for HCN and {tex}\Delta{/tex}iHo for CH3COOH are 45.2 kJ mol-1 and 2.1 kJ mol-1, then which one is not correct?

options.option: acetic acid is stronger than HCN
K_a of HCN < K_a of CH_3COOH

questions.explanation: {tex}p K_{a_{\mathrm{HCN}}}<p K_{a \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}{/tex}
