questions.question: A solution of a weak monobasic acid has {tex}\wedge_{M}{/tex} for 0.1 N solution and {tex}\wedge_{\infty}{/tex} equal to 60 S cm2 mol-1 and 400 S cm2 mol-1 respectively. The pH of this solution and Ka of acid is:
questions.explanation: 1.82, 2.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question:
Given:

  1. NH3 + H+ {tex} \rightleftharpoons \,NH_4^ + {/tex}; {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = -52.21 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}So = 1.6 JK-1 mol-1
  2. H2O {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + OH-; {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ho = 54.70 kJ mol-1 and {tex}\Delta{/tex}So = -76.3 JK mol-1.

If R = 8.3 J mol-1, calculate the dissociation constant for NH3.
questions.explanation: e-10

questions.question: Dissociation constant of acids HA and HB are 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 and 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively. 20 mL of 0.5 M HA and 30 mL of 0.2 M HB are mixed. The [H+] and pH of resulting solution is:
questions.explanation: 14.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4, 2.83

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains 10% ammonia by mass and has a density of 0.99 g cm-1. Calculate hydroxyl and hydrogen ion concentration in this solution. Ka for {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex} = 5 .0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M.

The ionisation constant for pure formic acid, K = {tex}\left[\mathrm{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex}[HCOO-] has been estimated as 10-6 at room temperature. What percentage of formic acid molecules in pure formic acid are converted to formate ion? The density of formic acid is 1.22 g/cm3.
questions.explanation:
Density of formic acid = 1.15g/cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of formic acid in 1L solution = 1.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 103
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCOOH] = {tex}\frac{1.15 \times 10^{3}}{46}{/tex} = 25M
In case of auto ionisation,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex}[HCOO-] = 10-6
[HCOO-] = {tex}\left[\mathrm{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 10-6
Now % dissociation of HCOOH,
{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right] \times 100}{[\mathrm{HCOOH}]} \Longrightarrow \frac{10^{-3} \times 100}{25}{/tex} = 0.004%

questions.question: pH of 0.01 M monoprotic acid is 3.0. The pKa of acid is:
When rain is accompanied by a thunderstorm the collected rain water will have a pH value:

options.option: slightly lower than that of rain water without thunderstorm
depending on the amount of dust in air
slightly higher than that when the thunderstorm is not there

questions.explanation:
During thunderstorm, there is formation of NO which changes to NO2 and ultimately to HNO3 (acidrain)
N2 + O2 {tex}\to{/tex} NO {tex}\xrightarrow{{{O_2}}}{/tex} NO2
4NO2 + O2 + 2H2O {tex}\to{/tex} 4HNO3 [pH < 7]

questions.question: The correct order of increasing [H3O+] in the following aqueous solutions is:
questions.explanation: H2SO4 is strong acid having pH < 7. NaNO2 on hydrolysis gives alkaline solution of pH > 7. NaCl is neutral and H2S is weak acid.

questions.question: Fear or excitement, generally cause one to breathe rapidly and it results in the decrease of concentration of CO2 in blood. In what way it will change pH of blood?
questions.explanation: Blood buffers do not allow a change in pH. It contains serum protein which act as buffer.

questions.question: The rapid change of pH near the stoichiometric point of an acid-base titration is the basis of indicator detection. pH of the solution is related to the ratio of the concentration of conjugate acid (HIn) and base (In-) forms of the indicator by the expression:
questions.explanation: log{tex}\frac{\left[\ln ^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HIn}]}{/tex}= pH - pKa

questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is not true?

  1. The pH of 10-8 N HCl is 8
  2. 96500 coulomb of electricity is passed through a CuSO4 solution to deposit 1 g equivalent of Cu at cathode
  3. The conjugate base of {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex} is {tex}HPO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
  4. pH + pOH = 14 for all aqueous solution
questions.explanation: The pH of 10-8 N HCl is 8.

questions.question: An acid type indicator, HIn differs in colour from its conjugate base (In). The human eye is sensitive to colour differences only when the ratio [In]/[HIn] is greater than 10 or smaller than 0.1. What should be the minimum change in the pH of the solution to observe a complete colour change (Ka = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)?
For pure water which one is correct?

options.option: Both pH and pOH increase with rise in temperature
Both pH and pOH decrease with rise in temperature
pH decreases while pOH increases with temperature
pH increases while pOH decreases with temperature

questions.explanation:
Both pH and pOH decrease with rise in temperature
pH = -log [H+]
Also for water,
[H+] [OH-] = 10-14
pH + pOH = 14
As we increase temperature, number of ions will increase and both pH and pOH will decrease with rise in temperature.

questions.question: Ka for HCN is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 at 25oC. For maintaining a constant pH of 9, the volume of 5 M KCN solution required to be added to 10 mL of 2 M HCN solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[Salt]}{[Acid]}{/tex}
= pKa + log {tex}\frac{[KCN]}{[HCN]}{/tex} ...(i)
Let the volume of KCN solution required be V mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [KCN] = {tex}\frac{5 \times V}{V+10}{/tex} and [HCN] = {tex}\frac{10 \times 2}{V+10}{/tex}
Now from eqn. (i),
pH = -log (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) + log {tex}\left[\frac{5 \times V}{V+10} / \frac{10 \times 2}{V+10}\right]{/tex}
9 = -log (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) + log {tex}\frac{V}{4}{/tex}
On solving, V = 1.99 {tex}\approx{/tex} 2 mL

questions.question: An aqueous solution of 0.1 M NH4Cl will have a pH closer to:
questions.explanation: Ammonium chloride is a salt of strong acid HCl and weak base NH4OH. In aqueous solution, ammonium chloride hydrolyzes to form acidic solution. Thus, its pH will be less than 7.

questions.question: The pH of the solution produced when an aqueous solution of strong acid pH 5 is mixed with equal volume of an aqueous solution of strong acid of pH 3 is:
questions.explanation:
The concentrations of solutions having pH, 3 and 5 are 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−3 M and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−5 M respectively.
The concentration of resulting solution is
= {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}+1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}}{2}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
pH = -log[H+] = -log 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 = 3.2967

questions.question: A physician wishes to prepare a buffer solution at pH = 3.58 that efficiently resists a change in pH yet contains only small cone, of the buffering agents. Which one of the following weak acid together with its sodium salt would be best to use?
questions.explanation: Acetoacetic acid (pKa = 3.58)

questions.question: Kb for the hydrolysis reaction, B+ + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BOH + H+ is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6, the hydrolysis constant of the salt is:
A weak acid HX (Ka = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) on reaction with NaOH gives NaX. For 0.1 M aqueous solution of NaX, the % hydrolysis is:
questions.explanation:
For NaX
{tex}\mathop {{X^ - }}\limits_{1 - h} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons \mathop {HX}\limits_h + \mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_h {/tex}
The expression for degree of hydrolysis can be given as:
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}}{\mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5} \times 0.1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{10^{-8}}{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} %h = 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10-2 = 0.01

questions.question: In a mixture of CH3COOH and CH3COONa, the ratio of salt to acid concentration is increased by ten folds. The pH of the solution will increase by:
questions.explanation:
As we know,
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
If {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} increases by 10 times, then pH = pKa + 1
Hence, the pH of the solution will increase by 1.

questions.question: Acetic acid and propionic acid have Ka values 1.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 and 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively at a certain temperature. An equimolar solution of a mixture of the two acids is partially neutralised by NaOH. How is the ratio of the contents of acetate and propionate ions related to the Ka values and the molarity?

options.option: The ratio is unrelated to the pH of the solution
The ratio is unrelated to the molarity
The ratio is unrelated to the K_a values
$\left(\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\right)=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left(\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are ionized fractions of the acids

questions.explanation:
The equilibrium reaction for the ionization of acetic acid is as given below:
{tex}C{H_3}\mathop {CO}\limits_{1 - \alpha } OH \rightleftharpoons C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_\alpha O{O^ - } + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{\alpha + \beta } {/tex}
The equilibrium reaction for the ionization of propanoic acid is given below:
{tex}{C_2}{H_5}\mathop {CO}\limits_{1 - \beta } OH \rightleftharpoons {C_2}{H_5}\mathop C\limits_\beta O{O^ - } + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{\alpha + \beta } {/tex}
Let, represent the degree of ionization at same concentrations of acetic acid and propanioc acid respectively. The expression for the dissociation constant for acetic acid is as given below:
KAcetic acid = {tex}\frac{[{\alpha}][{\alpha}+{\beta}] \cdot {c}}{[{1}-{\alpha}]}{/tex}
The expression for the dissociation constant for propanoic acid is as given below:
KPropionic acid = {tex}\frac{[\beta][\alpha+\beta] \cdot c}{[1-\beta]}{/tex}
The ration of the acid dissociation constants is calculated as shown below:
{tex}\frac{{K}_{\text {Acetic acid }}}{{K}_{\text {Propionic acid }}}=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \times \frac{(\mathbf{1}-\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\boldsymbol{\beta}}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}=\frac{1.75}{1.3} \times\left[\frac{\beta}{1-\beta}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: If pKb for fluoride ion at 25oC is 10.83, the ionization constant of hydrofluoric acid in water at this temperature is:
questions.explanation: 6.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question: The correct relationship between pH of isomolar solution of sodium oxide (pH1), sodium sulphide (pH2), sodium solenoid (pH3) and sodium telluride (pH4) is:
questions.explanation:
The acidic strength increases from Na2O to Na2 Te and greater the acidic strength of a solution lesser will be its pH. thus,
PH of Na2O > pH of Na2S > pH of Na2Se > pH of Na2Te
pH1 > pH2 > pH3 > pH4

questions.question: For a weak electrolyte (HA) dissociation {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{c \to 0} {/tex}, then:

options.option: the interionic attraction diminishes to zero
electrolyte is assumed to be 100% ionised

questions.explanation:
For a weak electrolyte (HA) dissociation {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{c \to 0} {/tex}, then

  1. electrolyte is assumed to be 100% ionized
  2. its dissociation constant remains same
  3. the interionic attraction diminishes to zero as the molecules are far apart.

This is according to Ostwald dilution law.
According to this law, "In the limit when concentration approaches zero (infinite dilution), the degree of dissociation approaches 1. In other words, the entire weak electrolyte is present in the dissociated form".

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the nature of boric acid in aqueous medium?
questions.explanation:
According to Bronsted-Lowry concept of acids and bases, an acid is a substance which can give a proton and a base is a substance which accepts a proton.
According to Lewis concept of acids and bases, an acid is a substance which can accept a lone pair of electrons whereas a base is a substance which can donate a lone pair of electrons.
{tex}{H_3}B{O_3} + {H_2}O \to B(OH)_4^ - + {H^ + }{/tex}
A Lewis monobasic acid.

questions.question: The reverse process of neutralization is:
questions.explanation:
A neutralization reaction is when an acid and a base react to form water and salt and involves the combination of H+ ions and OH− ions to generate water. The neutralization of a strong acid and strong base has a pH equal to 7.
Hydrolysis is a reaction involving the breaking of a bond in a molecule using water. The reaction mainly occurs between an ion and water molecules and often changes the pH of a solution.
Neutralization is exothermic whereas hydrolysis is endothermic.

questions.question:
If equilibrium constant KC of the reaction:
2Ag(s) + 2I- + 2H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2AgI(s) + H2(g) + 2OH-
is 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 (dimensionless) at 25°. If concentrations of I- at equilibrium is 0.10 and pressure of H2 gas at equilibrium is 60 kPa, calculate the pH of solution at equilibrium.
pH of water is 7.0 at 25oC. If water is heated to 70oC, the:

options.option: pH will decrease but solution will be neutral
pH will decrease and solution becomes acidic

questions.explanation: On heating water, Kw​ increases and thus, pH scale for neutrality changes from 7 to some lower value.

questions.question: Ka for the acid HA is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6. The value of K for the reaction, A- + H3O+ {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HA + H2O is:
questions.explanation:
A- + H3O+ {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HA + H2O
Given: pKa = 6
To find: k = ?
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[{A}^{-}\right]\left[{H}_{3} {O}^{+}\right]}{[{H} {A}]}{/tex}
pKa = -log [Ka] = 6
Ka = 10-6
{tex}\frac{{1}}{{K} {a}}=\frac{[{H} {A}]}{\left[{A}^{-}\right]\left[{H}_{3} {O}^{+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{10^{-6}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]}{/tex}
106 = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]}{/tex} = K
K = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 106

questions.question: The degree of hydrolysis of a salt of weak acid and weak base in its 0.1 M solution is found to be 50%. If the molarity of the solution is 0.2 M, the percentage hydrolysis of the salt should be:
questions.explanation: The degree of hydrolysis of a salt of weak acid and weak base is independent of concentration of salt.

questions.question:
Which one of the following relation correctly represents the relation between Kp and degree of conversion ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of A into B at equilibrium pressure (P) for the reaction:
A(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} nB(g)
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{(n \alpha)^{n} \cdot P^{n-1}}{(1 \ - \ \alpha)(1 \ + \ n \alpha \ - \ \alpha)^{n-1}}{/tex}

questions.question: A certain ion B- has an dissociation constant for Arrehenius basic character (2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7). The equilibrium constant for Lowry-Bronsted basic character is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {{B^ - }}\limits_{Bronsted\,base} + {H^ + } \rightleftharpoons HB{/tex}; Kb = {tex}\frac{[{H B}]}{\left[{B}^{-}\right]\left[{H}^{+}\right]}{/tex} ...(i)
 {tex}\mathop {{B^ - }}\limits_{Arrhenius\,base} + {H^ + } \rightleftharpoons HB{/tex} + OH-; Ka = {tex}\frac{[{H B}]\left[{O H}^{-}\right]}{\left[{B}^{-}\right]}{/tex} ...(ii)
By Eqs. (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{{K}_{{a}}}{{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\frac{[{H} {B}]\left[{O} {H}^{-}\right]}{\left[{B}^{-}\right]} \times \frac{\left[{B}^{-}\right]\left[{H}^{+}\right]}{[{H B}]}{/tex} = [H+] [OH-] = Kw ...(iii)
By Eqs. (i) and (ii) and (iii),
Kb = {tex}\frac{{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{{K}_{\mathrm{w}}}=\frac{{2 . 8} \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-14}}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 107

questions.question: Which of the following conditions is correct for the [OH-] of either weak or strong monoacidic base?
questions.explanation: [OH-] = cb, if (pKb + 1) < pcb

questions.question:
The hydrolysis constant KH for the reaction: {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex} + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H3PO4 + OH- is 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12.
The ionisation constant for H3PO4 + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex} + H3O+ is:
questions.explanation:
The ion {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex} is present in the salt {tex}{H_2}PO_4^ - {/tex}. It is a salt obtained from mixture NaOH + H3PO4
The expression for the hydrolysis constant is as given below,
KH = {tex}\frac{{K}_{{w}}}{\left.{K}_{{a}\left({H}_{3} {P O}_{4}\right.}\right)}{/tex}
Substitute values in the above expression, we get
KH = {tex}\frac{10^{-14}}{1.4 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 7.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question: Which of the following indicate proton transfer equilibria within the glycine molecule shown below?
questions.explanation: I, II, III, IV

questions.question: Calculate the concentration of fluoroacetic acid which is required to get [H+] = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M. Ka of acid = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3.
questions.explanation:
Given, [H+] = c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol litre-1
{tex}\because \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{(\mathrm{c} \alpha)(\mathrm{c} \alpha)}{\mathrm{c}(1-\alpha)}=\frac{\mathrm{c} \alpha^{2}}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \alpha}{(1-\alpha)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\alpha{/tex} = 0.634
Now, c{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
C = {tex}\frac{1.50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.634}{/tex} = 2.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
Note: Since Ka is of the order of 10-3 M and thus, it is not advisable to use Ka = c{tex}\alpha{/tex}2. Because (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) is not equal to 1 since {tex}\alpha{/tex} is not small.

questions.question:

0.01 M NaOH

questions.question: NaOH is a strong base. What will be pH of 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 MNaOH solution? (log 2 = 0.3)
questions.explanation:
Given [OH-] = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
= - log 5 + 2 log 10 = 1.30
{tex}\because{/tex} pH + pOH = 14
{tex}\because{/tex} pH= 14 - pOH = 14 - 1.30 = 12.70

questions.question: A 0.004 M solution of Na2SO4 is isotonic with 0.010 M solution of glucose at same temperature. The percentage dissociation of Na2SO4 is
questions.explanation:
NOTE: For isotonic solutions, osmotic pressure is same.
{tex}\mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}}\limits_{(0.004 - x)} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {2{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}^ + }}\limits_{2x} + \mathop {{\text{SO}}_4^{2 - }}\limits_x {/tex}
Since both solutions are isotonic, therefore, 0.004 + 2x = 0.01; x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} % Discount = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.004} \times {/tex} 100 = 75 %

questions.question: The degree of dissociation of water at 25°C is 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7% and density is 1.0 g cm-3. The ionic constant for water is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}}\limits_{(1 - \alpha )c} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{{\text{H}}^ + }}\limits_{\alpha c} + \mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_{\alpha c} {/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 .9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7; Density of water = {tex}\frac{1.0 \mathrm{gm}}{\mathrm{cm}^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{18} \times 1000{/tex} = 55.56 moles/ litre
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 55.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 = 1.055 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 [{tex}\because{/tex} 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7% = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kw= [H+] [OH-] = (1.055 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7)2 = 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-14

questions.question: The compound whose 0.1 M solution is basic is:
questions.explanation: (a) is a neutral solution due to both cationic and anionic hydrolysis (Ka = Kb = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5); (b) is acidic solution due to cationic hydrolysis; (c) is acidic solution due to cationic hydrolysis; (d) is basic solution due to anionic hydrolysis.

questions.question: 100 mL of 0.1 MHCl is taken in a beaker and to it 100 mL of 0.1 M NaOH is added in steps of 2 mL and the pH is continuously measured. Which of the following graphs correctly depicts the change in pH?
questions.explanation: At equivalence point, pH is 7 and pH increases with addition of NaOH so correct graph is .

questions.question: In an acid base titration, 0.1 M HCl solution was added to the NaOH solution of unknown strength. Which of the following correctly shows the change of pH of the titration mixture in this experiment?
questions.explanation: The pH of NaOH is more than 7 and during the titration, it decreases, so this graph is correct.

questions.question:
Given 0.10 M solution of each of the following organic acids:
Acetic acid, Ka = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Citric acid, Ka = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Lactic acid, Ka = 8.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Oxalic acid, Ka = 5.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
Which of the following statement is INCORRECT?

options.option: The ratio of [conjugate base] to [conjugate acid] for a buffer solution of citric acid of pH 3.00 is 0.141.
Oxalic acid is an ideal choice to make a buffer solution of pH3.
Oxalic acid would produce a buffer of the lowest pH.
Acetic acid would produce a buffer of the highest pH.

questions.explanation:
Organic acid pKa
Acetic acid 4.74
Citric acid 3.85
Lactic acid 3.06
Oxalic acid 1.23

Lactic acid or Citric acid may be used to make a solution of pH3.

questions.question: The pH of 0.05 M aqueous solution of weak base, BOH is 12.0. Calculate Kb.
questions.explanation:
BOH + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} BH+ + OH-
Initial (M) 1 - 0 0
Change (M) {tex}-\alpha{/tex} - {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Equilibrium (M) C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) - C{tex}\alpha{/tex} C{tex}\alpha{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-2 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{0.05}{/tex} = 0.2
Now, Kb = {tex}\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}=\frac{0.05 \times(0.2)^{2}}{1-0.2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question:
Self ionization of formic acid at room temperature is:
[HCOOH] {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} [HCOO-]
If ionization constant is 10-6 and density of HCOOH is 1.22 g/cm3. What percentage of formic acid molecules are converted to formate ion?
questions.explanation:
Density of HCOOH = 1.22 g/cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Weight of HCOOH in one litre solution = 1.22 {tex}\times{/tex} 103g
Thus [HCOOH] = {tex}\frac{1.22 \times 10^{3}}{46}{/tex} = 26.5 mol = 26.5 M
For auto ionization, {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} = [HCOO-] and [HCOO-] {tex}\left[{HCOOH}_{2}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 10-6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [HCOO-] = 10-3
Now, % ionization of HCOOH
= {tex}\frac{\left[{HCOO}^{-}\right] \times 100}{[{HCOOH}]}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{26.5} \times 100{/tex} = 0.004%

questions.question: An acid solution HX (Ka = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) contains 0.1 mol of acid per 2.0 litre of solution. What is the percent dissociation of the acid?
questions.explanation:
Ka = {tex}\frac{{C} \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
Ka = {tex}C\alpha^2{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\alpha << 1{/tex}]
Now, C = {tex}\frac{0.1}{2.0}{/tex} = 0.05 mol/L
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.01659
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage dissociation = 1.66%

questions.question:
Arrange the following in the decreasing order of overall dissociation constant value.

Acid {tex}K_{a_1}{/tex} {tex}K_{a_2}{/tex} {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex}
Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
Arsenic acid (H3AsO4) 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
Citric acid (C6H8O7) 7.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7

options.option: Citric acid > Phosphoric acid > Arsenic acid
Citric acid > Arsenic acid > Phosphoric acid
Arsenic acid > Phosphoric acid > Citric acid
Phosphoric acid > Arsenic acid > Citric acid

questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} _1} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_2} \times \mathrm{K_{a_3}}{/tex}
{tex}{K}_{{a}\left({H}_{3} {PO}_{4}\right)}{/tex} = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
= 195.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24
{tex}{K}_{{a}\left({H}_{3} {AsO}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
= 249 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21
{tex}{K}_{{a}\left({H}_{3} {AsO}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = 7.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
= 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16

questions.question: For preparing a buffer solution of pH 4.76 by mixing sodium acetate and acetic acid, the ratio of the concentration of salt and acid should be ________. [Given pKa for acetic acid = 4.76]
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
4.76 = 4.76 + log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log10{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\frac{\text { [Salt] }}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex} = 10o
= 1

questions.question: What is the pH of millimolar solution of ammonium hydroxide which is 10% dissociated?
questions.explanation:
C = 1 mM = 10-3 M
For a weak base,
[OH-] = C {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}
[OH-] = 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
[OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
pOH = - log10[OH-] = -log10(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
pOH = 4.0
pH + pOH = 14
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 4 = 10.0

questions.question: In a mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate the ratio of concentration of the salt to the acid is decreased ten times. Then the pH of the solution ________.
questions.explanation: decreases by one unit

questions.question: Determine the pOH of 0.265 M HCIO solution. Ka of HClO is 2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8.
questions.explanation:
HCIO + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} ClO- + H3O-
Ka = {tex}\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
[HCIO] = C = 0.265 M
2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 = {tex}\frac{0.265 \alpha^{2}}{1}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} <<1, (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) {tex}\approx{/tex} 1]
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
[H3O+] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 8.766 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.057 and pOH = 9.943

questions.question: A weak acid HX has the dissociation constant 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3. It forms a salt NaX on reaction with alkali. The degree of hydrolysis of 0.05 M solution of NaX is ________.
questions.explanation:
Kh = {tex}\frac{{K}_{{w}}}{{K}_{{a}}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
h = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_{h}}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{0.05}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.001%

questions.question: pH of a saturated solution of Ca(OH)2 is 9. The solubility product (Ksp) of Ca(OH)2 is ________.
questions.explanation:
pH = 9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 14 - 9 = 5
[OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}Cs(OH)_{2(s)} \rightleftharpoons \underset S{Ca_{aq}^{2+}}+\;\underset{2S=\;1\;\times\;10^{-5}M}{2OH^-}{/tex}
Since 2S = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, S = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Now Ksp = [Ca2+][OH-]2
= S {tex}\times{/tex} (2S)2
= 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)2
= 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15

questions.question: 0.16 g of an unknown strong acid HA is dissolved to give 200 mL solution. If the measured pOH of the solution is 11.8, identify the acid. (Choose the closest answer).
questions.explanation:
pOH = 11.8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 2.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 6.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M = [Acid]
Now, 6.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\frac{mol}{L}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2L
= 1.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol of acid
Molar mass of acid = {tex}\frac{0.16 {g}}{1.26 \times 10^{-3} {mol}}{/tex}
= 126.98 g/mol
This value is closest to the molar mass of HI.

questions.question:
Dissociation of an ionic compound in water depends on which of the following factor/s?

  1. Dielectric constant of water.
  2. Electrostatic forces of attraction between the ions
  3. Temperature of the solution.
  4. Extent of solvation of ions.
questions.explanation: When an ionic compound is dissolved in water, the high dielectric constant of water reduces the electrostatic forces of attraction and the ions become free to move in the solution. The extent to which dissociation occurs depends on the temperature of the solution and the extent of solvation of ions produced.

questions.question: Which of the following is the CORRECT order of increasing pH for equimolar solutions of HNO3, KCl, CH3NH3F, KOH and NaC2H3O2? (Ka for HC2H3O2 is 1.80 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, Ka for HF is 3 .5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4, Kb for CH3NH2 is 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5).
questions.explanation:
HNO3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} strong acid
KCl {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} salt of strong acid and weak base.
Hence, KCl is a neutral salt.
KOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} strong base
NaC2H3O2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Salt of strong base and weak acid.
Hence, the salt is basic.
CH3NH3F is a salt of weak base and weak acid.
Hence, Kb of the base and Ka of the acid is compared.
Since Ka(HF) > Kb (CH3NH3), the solution will be acidic.

questions.question:
Find the CORRECT statement.

  1. Urea conducts electricity in its molten state.
  2. Degree of ionization for acetic acid is {tex}\approx{/tex} 1.
  3. Degree of ionization for CuSO4 is <<<< 1.
  4. Aqueous silver nitrate solution conducts electricity.
questions.explanation:
1. Urea is a non-electrolyte. It cannot conduct electricity in molten state.
  2. Acetic acid is a weak electrolyte so {tex}\alpha{/tex} <<<< 1.
  3. CuSO4 is a strong electrolyte, so {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 1.

questions.question: Identify the set containing only poor conductors of electricity?
questions.explanation: Weak electrolytes behave as poor conductors of electricity, i.e., CH3COOH, HCOOH, H2O, liq.SO2

questions.question: Which among the following has the highest degree of ionization?
questions.explanation: {tex}\alpha \propto \frac{1}{\text { Concentration of solution }}{/tex}

questions.question: How many moles of NaOH can be added to 1.0 L of solution of 0.1 M NH3 and 0.1 M NH4CI without changing pOH by more than one unit? [Given: pKb of NH3 = 4.75]
questions.explanation:
It is a basic buffer.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = pKb + log10{tex}\frac{[Salt]}{[Base]}{/tex}
= 4.75 + log10{tex}\frac{0.1}{0.1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 4.75
Let x mol of NaOH is added. pH will increase and pOH will decrease.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} New pOH = 4.75 - 1 = 3.75
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3.75 = 4.75 + log10{tex}[\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}]{/tex}
[On adding strong base, the concentration of weak base will increase and that of salt will decrease]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -1 = log10{tex}[\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 10-1 = {tex}\frac{0.1 - x}{0.1 + x}{/tex} ... [Taking antilog]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{0.1-x}{0.1+x}{/tex}
On solving, 11x = 0.9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{0.9}{11}{/tex} = 0.082 mol

questions.question: When NH4CI is added to NH4OH solution, the dissociation of ammonium hydroxide is ________.
questions.explanation: decreased

questions.question: At 25oC, Kb for XOH is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. 0.01 M solution of XOH has [OH-] = ________.
questions.explanation:
[OH-] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_{b}}{C}}=\sqrt{K_{b} \cdot C}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{1.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.01}{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 M

questions.question:
Consider a 0.1 M Ca2+ ion solution. Identify the CORRECT statement when Ca2+ ions begin to precipitate as Ca(OH)2. Given: Ksp of Ca(OH)2 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9.

  1. Concentration of OH- ion is 10-4 M
  2. pOH of the solution is 4
  3. pH of the solution is 10
  4. pH of the solution is 9
  5. pOH of the solution is 5
questions.explanation:
When Ca(OH)2 starts precipitating,
[Ca2+][OH-]2 = Ksp
[0.1][OH-]2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
[OH-] = 10-4 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = -log[OH-] = -log(10-4) = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 4 = 10

questions.question: HCl was passed through a solution of CaCl2, MgCl2 and NaCl. Which of the following compound(s) crystallise(s)?
questions.explanation: MgCl2 has least solubility among the given compounds and hence, it crystallizes first when HCl is passed through its aqueous solution.

questions.question:
Find the CORRECT order of decreasing acid strength based on the following data.

Acid Dissociation constant (Ka)
i. HCOOH 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
ii. CH3COOH 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
iii. C6H5COOH 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
iv. C6H5OH 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
questions.explanation: Greater the dissociation constant of a weak acid, greater is the amount of {tex}H_{(aq)}^{+}{/tex} produced and therefore stronger is the acid.

questions.question: Identify the CORRECT equation.
questions.explanation: Kw = [OH-] {tex}\times{/tex} 10-pH

questions.question: The dissociation constant (Ka) and the percent of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex} {H}^{+}={C}_0 \alpha=\sqrt{{K}_\alpha {C}_0} \Rightarrow 10^{-5}{/tex} {tex}=10^{-1} \times \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=10^{-4}{/tex}
Also; {tex}{K}_{{a}}={C}_{{o}} \alpha^2=10^{-9}{/tex}
Percentage degree of dissociation {tex}=\alpha \times 100 \%=10^{-2} \%{/tex}

questions.question: The percentage of pyridine (C5H5N) that forms pyridinium ion {tex}(C_5H_5\overset+NH){/tex} in a 0.10 M aqueous pyridine solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{Pyridine}{C_5H_5N} + \underset{Water}{H_2O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{array}{l}Pyridinium\\ion\end{array}}{C_5H_5\overset+NH}\;+\;OH^-{/tex}
Now, Kb = {tex}\frac{C a^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}C\alpha^2{/tex} ... ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 - {tex}\alpha \approx{/tex}1)
The degree of dissociation is given by
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{K}_{{b}}}{{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-9}}{0.10}}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percent dissociation = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.013%
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage of pyridine that forms pyridinium ion in the given solution = 0.013%

questions.question: For a triprotic acid (H3A), the acid dissociation constant ({tex}K_{a_2}{/tex}) is given by the equation ________.
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{\left[{H}^{+}\right]\left[{HA}^{2-}\right]}{\left[{H}_{2} {A}^{-}\right]}{/tex}

questions.question: Value of pH of 10-8 M HCl solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
10-8 M indicates a very dilute solution. Thus, H+ of water cannot be ignored. But dissociation of water is suppressed due to the common ion effect.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] {tex}\neq{/tex} 10-7 but less than 10-7.
{tex}H_2O\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{10^{-8}\;+\;\alpha}{H^+}+\;\underset\alpha{OH^-}{/tex}
Kw = (10-8 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}){tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = 10-8 + 0.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 6.9788

questions.question: Two weak acids HA1 and HA2 with identical concentrations have Ka1 = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 and Ka2 = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5. What is the relative strength of acids?
questions.explanation: Relative strength of weak acids = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{{K}_{{al}}}{{K}_{{a} 2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(2.1 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(1.1 \times 10^{-5}\right)}} \cong{/tex}4 : 1

questions.question: The dissociation constant for the decompostition of N2O4(g) into NO2(g) at 10 atm and 313 K is ________.
questions.explanation:
N2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2NO2(g)
Initial (mol) 1 0
Equilibrium (mol) 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}

Total number of moles = 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}{p}_{{N}_{2} {O}_{4}}=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \times 10{/tex},
{tex}{p}_{{NO}_{2}}=\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha} \times 10{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{\left\{\left[\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha}\right] .10\right\}^{2}}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \times 10}=\frac{4 \alpha^{2} \times 10}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)}=\frac{40 \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: What is the pOH of a solution that contains 73 grams of hydrochloric acid dissolved in 2.0 liters of the solution?
questions.explanation:
[HCl] = {tex}\frac{73 {g}}{2.0 {L}} \times \frac{1 {mol}}{36.5 {g}}{/tex} = 1 M
pOH = 14 - pH
= 14 - [- log(1)] = 14

questions.question: Calculate the pH of 0.05 M solution of calcium hydroxide.
questions.explanation:
[Ca(OH)2] = 0.05 M
{tex}\underset{0.05}{Ca(OH)_2}\;\; \rightleftharpoons Ca^{2+}\;+\;\underset{2(0.05)\;=\;0.1}{2OH-}{/tex}
pOH = -log[OH-] = -log(0.1) = 1
pH = 14 - 1 = 13

questions.question:
If degree of dissociation is {tex}\alpha{/tex} and total pressure at equilibrium is P for following reaction,
PCI5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g) find the INCORRECT options.
questions.explanation:
PCl5(g) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} PCl3(g) + Cl2(g)
Initial moles 1 0 0
Moles after dissociation 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}

Total number of moles = 1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
= 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}
The partial pressure (pA) of an individual gas component can be calculated as:
pA = xA {tex}\times{/tex} P
where P = Total pressure of gas mixture and xA is mole fraction of that gas.
{tex}{P}_{{PCl}_{5}}=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}, {P}_{{Cl}_{3}}=\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}, {P}_{{Cl}_{2}}=\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot {P}{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{P_{{PC} 1} \cdot {P}_{{Cl}_{2}}}{{P}_{{PCl}_{5}}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P} \times\left(\frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P}}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \cdot {P}}{/tex}
Kp = {tex}\frac{\alpha^{2} {P}}{1-\alpha^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Arrange the following in increasing order of basicity.

Drink Concentration (M)
Milk H3O+ = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Pepsi OH- = 2.51 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
Black coffee H3O+ = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Orange juice OH- = 1.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11

options.option: Orange juice < milk < pepsi < black coffee
Pepsi < black coffee < orange juice < milk
Pepsi < orange juice < black coffee < milk
Black coffee < pepsi < milk < orange juice

questions.explanation:
[H3O+] {tex}\times{/tex} [OH-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 M
Basicity increases with increase in [OH-].

questions.question: pH of a 100 mL solution is 2.0. When the solution is diluted to 1000 mL, the pH of the resulting solution is ________.
questions.explanation:
[H+] in mL = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M ({tex}\because{/tex} pH = 2.0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] in mL = {tex}\frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{10}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
pH = -log[H+] = -log(1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 3.0

questions.question:
Which of the following regarding pH of a solution is TRUE?

  1. A solution of pH = 1 has H+ concentration a hundred times more than that of a solution of pH = 3.
  2. pH of 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M HCl solution must be twice that of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M HCl solution.
  3. If the pH of 1 M solution of a strong acid is 0, then 3 M solution of the same acid should have a positive pH value.
  4. pH of 10-8 M HCl solution is 8.
questions.explanation: A solution of pH = 1 has H+ concentration hundred times more than that of a solution of pH = 3.

questions.question: The pH of neutral water at 25oC is 7.0. What will be the pH of pure water at 80oC?
questions.explanation:
At 25oC the pH of neutral water is 7.0.
The ionic product of water (Kw) increases with increase in temperature.
Hence,
At 25oC, [H+] = [OH-] = 10-7
and Kw = [H+] [OH-] = 10-14
On increasing the temperature, Kw = [H+] [OH-] > 10-14
As [H+] = [OH-]
[H+] > 10-7 M or pH < 7

questions.question: What is the pH of 0.35 M aqueous solution of weak acid (HA) if Ka is 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5? [Given, log (3.5) = 0.544]
questions.explanation:
For weak acid, Ka = {tex}\alpha^2 C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (0.35)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} = 10-4
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 10-2
[H+] = {tex}\alpha C{/tex} = 10-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 = 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
pH = - log10[H+] = -log10 (3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)
pH = 2.456

questions.question: The dissociation constant (Ka) and percent of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of a weak monobasic acid solution of 0.1 M with a pH = 5, are respectively ________.
questions.explanation:
pH = -log10[H+]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = antilog10(-pH)
= antilog10(-5)
[H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
A weak monobasic acid (HA) dissociates as,
HA(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}{H}_{({aq})}^{+}+{A}_{({aq})}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\alpha C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[{H}^{+}\right]}{{C}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percent of degree of dissociation = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2%
Ka = {tex}\alpha^2 C{/tex} = (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question: Find the CORRECT graph with reference to the value of degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}).
questions.explanation:
Strong electrolyte: Degree of ionization for this type of electrolyte is 1, i.e., {tex}\alpha \approx 1{/tex}.
E.g. HCl, H2SO4, NaCl, HNO3, KOH, NaOH, AgNO3, CuSO4.
Weak electrolyte: Degree of ionization for this type of electrolyte is much less than 1, i.e., {tex}\alpha{/tex} <<< 1.
E.g. H2O, HCOOH, CH3COOH, NH4OH, liq.SO2, HCN.

questions.question: When mixed in equal volumes, which of the given one will form a buffer solution?

options.option: 0.05 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH + 0.1 mol dm^-3 HCl
0.1 mol dm^-3 CH_3COONa + 0.1 mol dm^-3 NaOH
0.1 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH + 0.05 mol dm^-3 HCl
0.1 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH + 0.1 mol dm^-3 HCl

questions.explanation: When the concentration of NH4OH (weak base) is higher than the strong acid (HCl), a mixture of weak base and its conjugate acid is obtained, which acts as basic buffer.

questions.question: Four different substances of the generalized formula HA were dissolved in water, with the results shown in the diagrams. Which of the diagrams represents the substance that is the strongest electrolyte?
questions.explanation: Figure  represents the substance that is the strongest electrolyte since the extent of dissociation is maximum among the given options.

questions.question:
A chemist has the following data regarding three drugs:

Formula Drug PKa
C17H19NO3 Morphine 9.85
C18H21NO3 Codeine 8.2
C17H21NO4 Codeine 8.85

He prepares an aqueous solution of one of the above drug which has the following structure.

The prepared solution has a concentration of 0.005 M. Based on all the above information, identify the drug used by the chemist to prepare the solution and calculate the pH of the solution.
questions.explanation:
Based on the given structure, the drug used is codeine.
Codeine is a weak base.
pKa = 8.2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pKb = 5.8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kb = 1.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
[Codeine] = 0.005 M = C
Kb = {tex}\frac{C \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.58 x 10-6 = {tex}\frac{0.005 \alpha^{2}}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.0178
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = C{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
pOH = -log10[OH-] = 4.05
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 9.95

questions.question: According to Lewis concept, an acid is:
questions.explanation: Under the Lewis concept, the correct definition of an acid is electron pair acceptor.

questions.question: The dissociation constant of water is represented by K ={tex}\frac{ [H_3O^+] [OH^-]}{[H_2O]}{/tex} or Kw = [H+] [OH-] Kw is called

options.option: ionisation constant of acid and base

questions.explanation:
The dissociation constant is represented by
K = {tex}\frac{ [H_3O^+] [OH^-]}{[H_2O]}{/tex}
The concentration of water is omitted from the denominator as water is a pure liquid and its concentration remains constant. [H2O] is incorporated within the equilibrium constant to give a new constant, Kw, which is called the ionic product of water,
Kw = [H+][OH-]

questions.question: The ionisation of weak base MOH can be represented by equation, MOH(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} M+(aq) + OH-(aq) Kb = {tex}\frac{ [M^+] [OH^-]}{[MOH]}{/tex} where Kb is represented as

options.option: Both acidic and basic ionisation constant

questions.explanation:
The equilibrium constant for basic ionisation is called basic ionisation constant and is represented by Kb.
{tex}K_b=\frac{\left[M^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{[M \mathrm{OH}]}{/tex}.

questions.question: A 0.2 molar solution of formic acid is 3.2% ionised. Its ionisation constant is
questions.explanation: K = {tex}C \alpha^2{/tex} = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.032)2 = 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

  1. The pH of 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M HCl solution is 8.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}{/tex} is {tex}\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}{/tex}
  3. Kw increases with increase in temperature.
  4. When a solution of weak monoprotic acid is titrated against a strong base at half neutralisation point, pH = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}pKa
questions.explanation:
1. pH of 10-8 M HCl is in acidic range (6.98).
  2. Conjugate base of {tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-}{/tex} is {tex}\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-}{/tex}
  3. Kw increases with increasing temperature, as the temperature increases, the dissociation of water increases.
  4. At half neutralisation point, half of the acid is present in the form of salt.
    pH = Pka + log{tex}\frac 11{/tex} = Pka

questions.question: Class XII students were asked to prepare one litre of buffer solution of pH 8.26 by their chemistry teacher. The amount of ammonium chloride to be dissolved by the student in 0.2 M ammonia solution to make one litre of the buffer is (Given pKb (NH3) = 4.74; Molar mass of NH3 = 17 g mol-1; Molar mass of NH4Cl = 53.5 g mol-1)
questions.explanation:
pH + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 8.26
pOH = pKb + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5.74 = 4.74 + log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]}{0.2} \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]{/tex} = 2M
Molar mass of NH4Cl = 53.5 g mol-1
Amount of NH4Cl = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 53.5 = 107 g

questions.question:
20 mL of 0.1 MNH4OH is mixed with 40 mL of 0.05 MHCl. The pH of the mixture is nearest to:
(Given: Kb (NH4OH) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5, log 2 = 0.30, log 3 = 0.48, log 5 = 0.69, log 7 = 0.84, log 11 = 1.04)
questions.explanation:
C - concn. of salt, NH4Cl, produced by neutralization of NH4OH and HCl.
NH4Cl, no. of moles = 2m mol = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol
{tex}\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right]=\frac{2 \mathrm{mmol}}{(20+40) \mathrm{ml}}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}M
pH = {tex}7- \frac 12{/tex} pKb {tex}-\frac 12{/tex}logC
= {tex}7-\frac{1}{2} \times(+5)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{1}{30}\right){/tex} = {tex}7-\frac{5}{2}+\frac{1.48}{2}{/tex} = 5.24

questions.question:
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions (A), (B), and (C).

  1. H2C2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + {tex}\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}{/tex}
  2. HC2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + {tex}\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}{/tex}
  3. H2C2O4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}{/tex}

The relationship between {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} and {tex}K_{a_3}{/tex} is given as
questions.explanation:
H2C2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
H2C2O4 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + {tex}\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_3}{/tex} ...(iii)
addition of eq. (i) with eq. (ii) gives eq (iii),
So, {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_3}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_1} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_2}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl will be maximum in which of the following?
questions.explanation:
In deionized water no common ion effect will take place so solubility of AgCl will be maximum in it.
KCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K+ + Cl-
HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H+ + Cl-
AgNO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ag+ + {tex}\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}{/tex}

questions.question: When the hydrogen ion concentration [H+] changes by a factor of 1000, the value of pH of the solution ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 1000
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{pH}{/tex} = -log {tex}\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = -log 103
= -3; (-ve) sign indicates decrease in pH.

questions.question: On decreasing the pH from 7 to 2, the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt (MX) of a weak acid (HX) increased from 10-4 mol L-1 to 10-3 mol L-1. The pKa of HX is
questions.explanation:
Let the solubility of MX at PH 7 = s
{tex}MX(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{\mathrm S}{\mathrm M^+}+\underset{\mathrm S}{\mathrm X^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s2 ...(i)
Let the solubility of MX at pH = 2 = S1
{tex}MX(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{{\mathrm S}_1}{\mathrm M^+}+\underset{{\mathrm S}_1}{\mathrm X^-}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HX}]}{\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right] ;\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\frac{\mathrm{s}_1}{\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right] \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{s}_1^2}{10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Divide equation (ii) by equation (i)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{s}_1^2}{\mathrm{~s}^2 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{sp}}}{/tex};
{tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{\left(10^{-4}\right)^2 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{/tex}
Ka = 10-4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pKa = 4

questions.question: The Ksp for bismuth sulphide (Bi2S3) is 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-73. The solubility of Bi2S3 in mol L-1 at 298 K is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3 \rightleftharpoons \underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{Bi}^{3+}}+\underset{3 s}{3 \mathrm{~S}^{2-}}{/tex}
Ksp = (2s)2 + (3s)3 = 108 (s)5
(s)5 = {tex}\frac{1.08 \times 10^{-73}}{108} \Rightarrow{/tex} s = 10-15

questions.question:
The solubility of AgCN in a buffer solution of pH = 3 is x.
The value of x is:
[Assume: No cyano complex is formed; Ksp(AgCN) = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 and Ka(HCN) = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10]
questions.explanation:
AgCN {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ag+ + CN-
Solubility of AgCN is x.
pH = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]{/tex} = 10-3
HCN {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + CN-
Ka = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]} \Rightarrow \frac{\left[10^{-3}\right]\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]}{/tex} = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HCN}]}{/tex} = 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Each CN- hydrolyses to give one HCN as:
CN- + H2O {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} HCN + OH-
HCN is a weak acid, thus, [HCN] >> [CN-]
x = [Ag+] = [CN-] + [HCN]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = [Ag+] {tex}\approx{/tex} [HCN] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [CN-] = x {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
Ksp(AgCN) = [Ag+] [CN-]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 = (x) (x {tex}\times{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 3.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: A compound X is a weak acid and it exhibits colour change at pH close to the equivalence point during neutralization of NaOH with CH3COOH. Compound X exists in ionized form in basic medium. The compound X is:
questions.explanation: Phenolphathalein have colour change range between pH 8.3 and 10.5.

questions.question:
The solubility of Ca(OH)2 in water is:
[Given: The solubility product of Ca(OH)2 in water = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6]
questions.explanation:
Let s be the solubility of Ca(OH)2 in water
Ca(OH)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}\underset{s}{\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}} + \underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ca2+][OH-]2 = s {tex}\times{/tex} (2s)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 4s3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s3 = {tex}\frac {505}{4}\times10^{-6}{/tex}
s {tex}=\left[\frac{5.5}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

questions.question: Following four solutions are prepared by mixing different volumes of NaOH and HCl of different concentrations, pH of which one of them will be equal to 1?
questions.explanation:
75 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl + 25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}NaOH
25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}NaOH will neutralise 25 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl
75 - 25 = 50 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex}HCl will remain.
Total volume will be 75 + 25 = 100 mL
When 50 mL {tex}\frac M5{/tex} HCl is diluted to 100 mL, molarity will reduce to half, i.e., {tex}\frac {M}{10}{/tex}.
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=[\mathrm{HCl}]=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
pH = -log10[H+] = -log10{tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: 50 mL of 0.2 M ammonia solution is treated with 25 mL of 0.2 M HCl. If pKb of ammonia solution is 4.75, the pH of the mixture will be:
questions.explanation:
NH3 + HCl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NH4Cl
Moles of HCl = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 0.005
Moles of NH3 = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 0.01
Excess NH3 = 0.01 - 0.005 = 0.005 moles
1 mole ammonia = 1 mole NH4Cl
0.005 NH3 {tex}\equiv{/tex} 0.005 NH4Cl
Total volume = {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{HCl}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{NH}_3}{/tex} = 25 + 50 = 75 mL
[NH3] = [NH4Cl] = {tex}\frac{0.005 \text { mole }}{75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~L}}{/tex} = 0.066 M
pOH = pKb + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]}{/tex}
pOH = 4.75 + log{tex}\frac{[0.066]}{[0.066]}{/tex}
pOH = 4.75
pH = 14 - pOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pH = 9.25

questions.question: Equimolar solutions of the following compounds are prepared separately in water. Which will have the lowest pH value?
questions.explanation:
Metal halide on hydrolysis with water form corresponding hydroxides.
The basic strength of hydroxide increases as we move down in a group.
Hence, Be(OH)2 will have lowest pH.

questions.question: What is the pH of a 10-4 M OH- solution at 330K, if Kw at 330 K is 10-13.6?
questions.explanation:
Given at 330 K, Kw = 10-13.6
pKw = pH + pOH {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13.6 = pH + pOH
pOH = -log 10-4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pOH = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 13.6 - 4 = 9.6

questions.question:
Values of dissociation constant, Ka are given as follows:

Acid Ka
HCN 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
HF 7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
HNO2 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

Correct order of increasing base strength of the base CN-, F- and {tex}\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} will be:
questions.explanation:
Higher the value of Ka, higher will be the acidic nature. Further, since CN-, F- and NO2- are conjugate base of the acids HCN, HF and HNO2 respectively, hence the correct order of base strength will be: F- < {tex}\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} < CN-
({tex}\because{/tex} stronger the acid, weaker will be its conjugate base)

questions.question: A weak acid HX has the dissociation constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M. It forms a salt NaX on reaction with alkali. The percentage hydrolysis of 0.1 M solution of NaX is
questions.explanation:
h = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a \times c}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-5} \times 0.1}}{/tex} = 10-4;
Hence, % hydrolysis = 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 0.01

questions.question: 200mL of 0.01 MHCl is mixed with 400mL of 0.01 MH2SO4. The pH of the mixture is ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right)_{\mathrm{HCI}}+\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}\right)_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}}{\text { Total volume }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left(\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{HCl}} \times \mathrm{n}_{\text {factor }, \mathrm{HCl}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{HCl}}\right)+\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}}{600}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{(0.01 \times 1 \times 200)+(0.01 \times 2 \times 400)}{600}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2+8}{600}=\frac{10}{600}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex}
pH = -log{tex}\left[\frac{1}{60}\right]{/tex} = 1.78

questions.question: The number of hydrogen ions in 10 ml of a solution with pH = 13 is
questions.explanation:
pH = 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log [H+] = 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [H+] = 10-13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10-13 moles of H+ in 1L
or, 1000 mL solution... 10-13 moles
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 mL solution... {tex}\frac {10^{-13}}{100}{/tex} moles
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of H+ ions = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023

questions.question: Aqueous solution of which of the following compounds is the best conductor of electric current?
questions.explanation:
The strong electrolyte which furnishes ions completely in its aqueous solution will be the best conductor of electricity in aqueous solution. Fructose and ammonia are not an electrolyte while acetic acid is a weak electrolyte and HCl is the strong electrolyte
which completely furnishes H+ and CI- ions in aqueous solution thus, HCl is the best conductor of electricity in aqueous solution.

questions.question: Incorrect statement for the use of indicators in acid - base titration is:

options.option: Methyl orange is a suitable indicator for a strong acid vs weak base titration.
Phenolphthalein is a suitable indicator for a weak acid vs strong base titration.
Phenolphthalein may be used for a strong acid vs strong base titration.
Methyl orange may be used for a weak acid vs weak base titration.

questions.explanation: Methyl orange is a suitable indicator for a strong acid vs strong base titration, not for a weak acid vs weak base titration. In a weak acid vs weak base titration, the pH at the equivalence point is typically around 7, which is within the pH range where methyl orange undergoes a color change. Therefore, methyl orange is not suitable for use in weak acid vs weak base titrations.

questions.question: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.explanation: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called Buffer Solutions. Buffer solutions of known pH can be prepared from the knowledge of pKa of the acid or pK b of base and by controlling the ratio of the salt and acid or salt and base. A mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate acts as a buffer solution around pH 4.75 and a mixture of ammonium chloride and ammonium hydroxide acts as a buffer around pH 9.25.

questions.question: A certain buffer solution contains equal concentration of X- and HX. The Kb for X- is 10-10. The pH of the buffer is
questions.explanation:
{tex}K_{a}(\mathrm{HX})=\frac{K_{\mathrm{w}}}{K_{b}}=10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_{a}+\log \frac{\left[X^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HX}]}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pKa = 4 [{tex}\because{/tex} [X-] = [HX]]

questions.question: An acidic buffer solution can be prepared by mixing the solution of
questions.explanation: Acidic buffer is prepared by mixing weak acid with salt of its conjugate base. Therefore, acetic acid and sodium acetate can be used to prepare acidic buffer.

questions.question: Ostwald’s dilution law is applicable to only
questions.explanation:
Ostwald’s dilution law is the application of the law of mass action to weak electrolytes in solution.
Ostwald's dilution law is only applicable to weak electrolytes because in strong electrolytes the dissociation is almost complete and the extent of dissociation is almost equal to 1, hence Ka​ goes to infinity.
In weak electrolytes, it is finite as the extent of dissociation is small.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a buffer solution?
questions.explanation: Because HClO4 is a strong acid. While the buffer is a mixture of the weak acid and their salt.

questions.question: The addition of which salt will decrease the H+ concentration of HCN solution?
questions.explanation: The dissociation of HCN will decrease in presence of NaCN due to common ion effect.

questions.question: pKa values of four acids are given below at 25°C. The strongest acid is:
questions.explanation: More is Ka, lesser is pKa (pKa = -log Ka), more is acidic strength.

questions.question: Which one is not a buffer?
questions.explanation: HClO4 is strong acid

questions.question: The total number of different kind of buffers obtained during the titration of H3PO4 with NaOH are:
questions.explanation: NaH2PO4 + H3PO4; NaH2PO4 + Na2HPO4; Na2HPO4 + Na3PO4

questions.question: The pH of an acidic buffer mixture is:
questions.explanation: pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}; Ka for HCN = 10-8

questions.question: The most important buffer in the blood consists of:
questions.explanation: A buffer of H2CO3 and {tex}\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} is formed.

questions.question: Which may be added to one litre of water to act as a buffer?

options.option: One mole of HC_2H_3O_2 and 0.5 mole of NaOH
One mole of HC_2H_3O_2 and one mole of HCl
One mole of NH_4OH and one mole of NaOH
One mole of NH_4Cl and one mole of HCl

questions.explanation: and thus acts as buffer.

questions.question: Which of the following solution does not act as a buffer?
questions.explanation: Buffer contains weak acid + its conjugate base or weak base + its conjugate acid.

questions.question: A buffer solution is prepared by mixing 10 mL of 1.0 M acetic and 20 mL of 0.5 M sodium acetate and then diluted to 100 mL with distilled water. If the pKa of CH3COOH is 4.76, what is the pH of the buffer solution prepared?
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{\text { [Conjugate base }}{\text { [Acid] }}{/tex}
[Conjugate base] = {tex}\frac{20 \times 0.5}{30}{/tex}; [Acid] = {tex}\frac{10 \times 1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 4.76 + log {tex}\frac{1}{1}{/tex} = 4.76

questions.question: 1 M NaCl and 1 M HCl are present in an aqueous solution. The solution is:

options.option: not a buffer solution and pH < 7
not a buffer solution and pH > 7

questions.explanation: HCl is stronger acid has pH < 7

questions.question: An acidic buffer solution can be prepared by mixing the solutions of:
questions.explanation: Sodium acetate and acetic acid

questions.question: Which of the following pairs constitutes buffer?
questions.explanation: The mixture containing a weak acid (HNO2) and its conjugate base {tex}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}\right){/tex} acts as acidic buffer.

questions.question: In a buffer solution containing equal concentration of B- and HB, the Kb for B- is 10-10. The pH of buffer solution is:
questions.explanation:
pH = pKa + log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{B} .]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}{/tex}
= pKw - pKb + log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{C.B.}]}{[\mathrm{Acid}]}{/tex}
pH = 14 - 10 = 4

questions.question: Buffer solutions have constant acidity and alkalinity because:

options.option: they have large excess of H^+ or OH^- ions
acids and alkalies in these solutions are shielded from attack by other ions
they have fixed value of pH
these give unionised acid or base on reaction with added acid or alkali

questions.explanation:
Consider a buffer of CH3COOH + CH3COONa
Addition of Acid : {tex}H^++CH_3COO^-\rightarrow\underset{Weak\;acid}{CH_3COOH}{/tex}
Addition of alkali : {tex}OH^-+CH_3COOH\;\rightarrow\underset{Weak\;electrolyte}{H_2O}\;+\;CH_3COO^-{/tex}
Thus, addition of acid or alkali does not cause any change in pH.

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of solution is not an acidic buffer ?
questions.explanation: An acidic buffer is a mixture of a weak acid and its salt with a strong base. Among CH3COOH, H2CO3, H3PO4 and HCIO4. The HCIO4 is a strong acid while all other are weak acid thus, HCIO4 and NaClO4 does not constitute to form an acidic buffer.

questions.question: What volume of 1.0 M sodium formate solution should be added to 50mL of 0.05 M formic acid to produce a buffer solution of pH = 4.0 (pKa of formic acid = 3.80)?
questions.explanation:
LEt V mL of 0.1 M HCOONa be mised to 50 mL of 0.05 M HCOOH
{tex}\left[\because[\text { Molarity }]=\frac{\text { Total millimole }}{\text { Total volume }}\right]{/tex}
[HCOOH] = {tex}\frac{50 \times 0.05}{V+50}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In mixture [HCOONa] = {tex}\frac{0.1 \times V}{V+50}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} pH = -log Ka + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base }]}{[\text { Acid }]} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4.0 = 3.80 + log {tex}\frac{(0.1 \times V) /(V+50)}{25 /(V+50)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 39.62 mL

questions.question: Which will make a basic buffer?
