50 mL of 0.1 M NaOH + 25 mL of 0.1 M CH_3COOH
100 mL of 0.1 M CH_3COOH + 100 mL of 0.1 M NaOH
100 mL of 0.1 M HCl + 200 mL of 0.1 M NH_4OH

questions.explanation: 100 mL of 0.1 M HCl + 200 mL of 0.1 M NH4OH

questions.question: Select the wrong statement about buffer capacity {tex}(\beta){/tex} when CBOH and CHA represents the number of mole of strong base or strong acid added to 1 litre of buffer mixture.

options.option: In a buffer mixture, the salt is also hydrolysed to produce acid or base and thereby to influence buffer capacity
Buffer capacity is satisfactory over a pH range of pK_a $\pm$ 1

questions.explanation:
A solution that resists change in pH value upon addition of small amount of strong acid or base (less than 1 %) or when solution is diluted is called buffer solution. The capacity of a solution to resist alteration in its pH value is known as buffer capacity and the mechanism of buffer solution is called buffer action.
In a buffer mixture, the salt's hydrolysis will be reduced due to presence of weak acid or base so they can not influence buffer capacity.

questions.question: Which of the following will produce a buffer solution when mixed in equal volumes?

options.option: 0.05 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH and 0.1 mol dm^-3 HCl
0.1 mol dm^-3 CH_3COONa and 0.1 mol dm^-3 NaOH
0.1 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH and 0.1 mol dm^-3 HCl
0.1 mol dm^-3 NH_4OH and 0.05 mol dm^-3 HCl

questions.explanation: In 0.1 mol dm-3 NH4OH and 0.05 mol dm-3 HCl, total amount of HCl reacts with NH4OH to form NH4Cl and some NH4OH will be left unreacted. Thus, the resultant solution contains NH4Cl and NH4OH which will produce a buffer solution.

questions.question: How many mixed buffers can be obtained using KOH and H3PO4?

Two buffer solutions A and B made by 0.5 M HA and NaA have pH 4.0 and 6.0 respectively. The pH of buffer obtained by mixing equal volumes of A and B will be: (Given {tex}\mathrm{p} K_{a_{\mathrm{H} A}}{/tex} = 5)
Which can act as buffer?

options.option: 40 mL of 0.1 M NaCN + 20 mL of 0.1 MHCl

questions.explanation: All of these

questions.question: A certain buffer solution contains equal concentration of X- and HX. The Ka for HX is 10-8. The pH of the buffer is:
questions.explanation:
As the value of Ka is given to us, it is an indication that the given buffer is an example of an acidic buffer.
Hence, we use the following formula for calculating its pH.
pH = -log Kw + log {tex}\frac{[\text { Conjugate base]}}{\text { [Acid }]}{/tex}
pH = pKa + log10 {tex}\begin{array}{l} {\left[{X}^{-}\right]} \\ \hline[{H X}] \end{array}{/tex}= -log10 10-8 + log10 1 = 8
(As the concentrations of X- and HX are equal)

questions.question: Addition of sodium hydroxide solution to a weak acid (HA) results in a buffer of pH 6. If ionisation constant of HA is 10-5, the ratio of salt to acid concentration in the buffer solution will be:
questions.explanation:
HA {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + A−
(Unionized, weak acid and common ion effect)
HA + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na A + H2O
NaA {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na+ + A− (ionized)
{tex}K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}{/tex}
Given, pH = 6, [H+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{K_{\mathrm{a}}[\text { Acid }]}{[\text { Salt }]}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}=\frac{K_{\mathrm{a}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-6}}{/tex} = 10 : 1

questions.question: In some solutions, the concentration of H3O+ remains constant even when small amounts of strong acid or strong base are added to them. These solutions are known as:
questions.explanation: Solutions that resist the change in the value of pH when a small amount of acid or base is added to them are known as buffers.

questions.question: An acidic buffer is obtained on mixing:

options.option: 100 mL of 0.1M HCl and 200 mL of 0.1 M NaCl
100mL of 0.1 MCH_3COOH and 100 mL of 0.1 MNaOH
l00 mL of 0.1 MHCl and 200 mL of 0.1 MCH_3COONa
100 mL of 0.1 MCH_3COOH and 200 mL of 0.1 MNaOH

questions.explanation: A buffer solution contains CH3COONa (10 millimole) and CH3COOH (10 millimole) which is an acidic buffer.

questions.question: Which of the following combinations in solution can produce a buffer?
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{NaH_2PO_4}{H_3PO_4}{/tex}

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of solution is NOT an acidic buffer?
questions.explanation: HClO4 and NaClO4

questions.question: Which will make basic buffer?

options.option: 100 mL of 0.1 M CH_3COOH + 100 mL of 0.1 M NaOH
50 mL of 0.1 M NaOH + 25 mL of 0.1 M CH_3COOH
100 mL of 0.1 M HCl + 100 mL of 0.1 M NaOH
100 mL of 0.1 M HCl + 200 mL of 0.1 M NH_4OH

questions.explanation:
Basic buffer contains weak base and its salt. Half of the weak base NH4OH reacts with HCl to form salt NH4Cl. Hence, the reaction mixture contains an equal amount of weak base and its salt.
HCl + NH4OH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NH4Cl + H2O

questions.question: What change is caused by the addition of small amount of strong acid to a buffer containing F- and HF?
questions.explanation:
HF {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} H+ + F- (Buffer)
Addition of strong acid (H3O+):
H3O+ + F- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} HF + H2O

questions.question: If a buffer solution contains 0.2 M ammonia (Kb = 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5) and 0.4 M ammonium chloride, what is the pH? [log10 (2) = 0.3010]
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + log10{tex}\frac{\text { [conjugate acid] }}{[\text { base }]}{/tex}
= 4.4 + log10{tex}\frac{(0.4)}{(0.2)}{/tex}
= 4.701
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 9.3

questions.question: In a buffer containing 0.25 M NH3 and 0.45 M NH4CI, by how much will the pH change if 0.050 mol HCl is added to 1.00 L of the buffer?
questions.explanation:
pH1 = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{0.25}{0.45}{/tex} = pKa - 0.25
pH2 = pKa + log10{tex}\frac{0.2}{0.5}{/tex} = pKa - 0.39
pH1 - pH2 = pKa - 0.25 - pKa + 0.39 = 0.14
pH2 = pH1 - 0.14

questions.question:
Of the reactions depicted, which best represents

  1. What occurs when strong acid is added to an acidic buffer solution?
  2. What occurs when strong base is added to an acidic buffer solution?
questions.explanation:
Strong acid added:
H3O + A- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} HA + H2O
Strong base added:
OH- + HA {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O + A-

questions.question: 100 mL of a solution contains 0.1 M NH4OH and 0.1 M NH4Cl. The pH of the solution will not change on adding.
questions.explanation: pH of buffer does not change on adding distilled water as there are no ions present in it.

questions.question: One litre of a buffer solution containing 0.01 M NH4Cl and 0.1 M NH4OH having pKb of 5 has pH of
questions.explanation:
pOH = pKb + {tex}\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{\text { [base }]}{/tex}
= 5 + {tex}\log \frac{[\text { 0.01}]}{\text { [0.1 }]}{/tex} = 5 + log 10-1 = 5 - 1 = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - 4 = 10

questions.question: A solution of an acid has pH = 4.70. Find out the concentration of OH- ions (pKw = 14).
questions.explanation: 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question:
A student needs to prepare a buffer solution of propanoic acid and its sodium salt w ith pH 4. The ratio of {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} required to make buffer is ________
Given: Ka(CH3CH2COOH) =1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
questions.explanation:
According to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
pH = pKa + log{tex}\frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}{/tex}
4 = 5 - log 1.3 + log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} = log 1.3 - 1 = log{tex}\frac{1.3}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log A - log (B) = log{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{/tex} = 0.13

topics.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts

questions.question: The Ksp for Cr(OH)3 is 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30. The molar solubility of this compound in water is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\begin{array}{l}Cr(OH)_3=Cr^{3+}+3\;OH^-\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;s\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;3s\end{array}{/tex}
Ksp = (s)(3s)3 = 27s4
Now, 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30 = 27s4
{tex}{ \Rightarrow {s^4} = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 30}}}}{{27}}}{/tex}
{tex}{ \Rightarrow s = \sqrt[4]{{\frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 30}}}}{{27}}}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
pH + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 12 = 2
pOH = -log[OH-]
OH- = 10 - pOH = 10 - 2 .......... (1)
At equilibrium, Ba(OH)2 = Ba+2 + 2OH-
let [OH-] = x , therefore, From above equation; 2[OH-] = 2x  = 10 - 2 (from equation 1)
Therefore {tex}x = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{2} = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ba+2] [OH-]2 = [0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 2[10-2]2 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 6 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7

questions.question: The solubility of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{3}}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{/tex} in water is y moles/litre. Its solubility product is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {C{a_3}}\limits_y {\left( {P{O_4}} \right)_2} = \mathop {3C{a^{2 + }}}\limits_{3y} + \mathop {2PO_4^{3 - }}\limits_{2y} {/tex}     
Solubility product = {tex}K_{sp}=\;\lbrack Ca^{{}_{2+}}\rbrack^3\lbrack PO_4^{3-}\rbrack^2\;=(3y)^3\;(2y)^2\;=108y^5{/tex}

questions.question: What is the correct expression for the representation of the solubility product constant of Ag2CrO4?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}Ag_2CrO_4\rightarrow\;2\;Ag^+\;+\;CrO_4^{2-}{/tex}
Suppose solubility is 's' then Ksp= [2s]2[s] = 4s3

questions.question: A chemist dissolves an excess of BaSO4 in pure water at 25°C if its {tex}K_{sp}=1\times10^{-10}{/tex}. what is the concentration of barium in the water ?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
BaSO4 → Ba2+ + SO42-
Ksp =  [Ba+2] [SO4-2]
Ksp = x2= 10-10
x2 = 10-10
x= 10 -5

questions.question: {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+} ; K_{1}=3.5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} ; K_{2}=1.7 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} then the formation constant of [Ag(NH3)2]+ is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+} ; K_{1}=3.5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{+}\right]+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}^{+}\right] \quad K_{2}=1.7 \times 10^{-3}}{\text { Adding }: \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}^{+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}K=K_{1} \times K_{2}=5.95 \times 10^{-6}{/tex}

questions.question: The molar solubitlity of Cd(OH)2 is 1.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 m in water. The expected solubility of Cd(OH)2 in a buffer solution of pH = 12 is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Solubility of Cd(OH)2 (s) = 1.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
Given, pH = 12 [for Cd(OH)2 in buffer solution]
So, pOH = 2 ({tex}\because{/tex} pH + pOH = pAw)
12 + pOH = 14
pOH = 14 - 12 = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-2 in buffer solution.
For reaction {tex}\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{OH})_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cd}_{s}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=(S)(2 S)^{2}=4 S^{3}=4\left(1.84 \times 10^{-5}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=24.9 \times 10^{-15}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]=\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right]=\frac{24.9 \times 10^{-15}}{\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2}}=24.9 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{+4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ 24.9 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
[Cd2+] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M
The expected solubility of Cd(OH)2 in a buffer solution of pH = 12 is 2.49 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M.

questions.question: What is the molar solubility of Al(OH)3 in 0.2 M NaOH solution? Given the, solubility product of Al(OH)3 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}A B \rightleftharpoons A^{+}+B^{-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[A^{+}\right]\left[B^{-}\right]{/tex}

{tex}\mathrm{NaOH}\longrightarrow\underset{0.2}{\mathrm{Na}^+}+\underset{0.2}{\mathrm{OH}^-}{/tex}
Ksp of Al (OH)3 = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 (Given)
Ksp =[AI3+][OH-]3
2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 = [S'][3S + 0.2]3 [{tex}\because{/tex} 0.2 >> S]
2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 = [S] [0.008]
[S] = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22

questions.question: If solubility product of Zr3(PO4)4 is denoted by Ksp and its molar solubility is denoted by S , then which of the following relation between S and Ksp is correct?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For, Zr3(PO4)4,
{tex}{\mathrm{Zr}}_3{\left({\mathrm{PO}}_4\right)}_4(s) \rightleftharpoons \underset{3SM}{3\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}}(aq)+\underset{4SM}{4\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}(aq)}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}\right]^{3}\left[\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}\right]^{4}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{ap}}=(3 S)^{3}(4 S)^{4}=6912 \ S^{7} \text { or } S=\left(\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right)^{\frac{1}{7}}{/tex}
Thus, the relation between molar solubility(5) and solubility product (Ksp) will be
{tex}S=\left(\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{6912}\right)^{1 / 7}{/tex}

questions.question: If Ksp of Ag2CO3 is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, the molar solubility of Ag2CO3 in 0.1 M AgNO3 is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Let the solubility of Ag2CO3 is S. Now, 0.1 M of AgNO3 is added to this solution after which let the solubility of Ag2CO3
becomes S'.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+ ] = S + 0.1 and [{tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}] = S'
Ksp = (S + 0.1)2 (S') ...(i)
Given, Ksp = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
{tex}\because{/tex} Ksp is very small, we neglect S' against S in Eq. (i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp  = (0.1)2 S'
or 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 = 0.01 S'
or S' =  8 {tex}\times{/tex}​​​​​​​ 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex}​​​​​​​ 10 -10 M
Thus, molar solubility of Ag2CO3 in 0.1 M
AgNO3 is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 M

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 M solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500 mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Its given that the final volume is 500 mL and this final volume was arrived when 50 mL of 1 M a2SO4 was added to unknown Ba2+ solution.
So, we can interpret the volume of unknown Ba2+ solution as 450 mL i.e
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {450mL}\limits_{B{a^{2 + }}} }\limits_{Solution} + \mathop {\mathop {50mL}\limits_{N{a_2}S{O_4}} }\limits_{Solution} \to \mathop {\mathop {500mL}\limits_{BaS{O_4}\;} }\limits_{Solution} {/tex}
From this we can calculate the concentration of S04“ ion in the
solution via
M1V1 = M2V2
{tex}1 \times 50=M_{2} \times 500{/tex}
(as 1M Na2SO4 is taken into consideration)
{tex}M_{2}=\frac{1}{10}=0.1 \mathrm{M}{/tex}
Now for just precipitation,
Ionic product = Solubility product (Ksp)
i.e. {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=K_{\mathrm{sp}} \text { of } \mathrm{BaSO}_{4}{/tex}
Given Ksp of BaSO4 {tex}=1 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
So, {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right][0.1]=1 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
or {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]=1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
Remember This is the concentration of Ba2+ ions in final solution. Hence, for calculating the Ba2+] in original solution.
we have to use
M1V1 = M2V2
as {tex}M_{1} \times 450=10^{-9} \times 500{/tex}
So, {tex}M_{1}=1.1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{M}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility product constant (Ksp) of salts of types MX, MX2 and M3X at temperature T are 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8, 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 and 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15, respectively. Solubilities (mol dm-3) of the salts at temperature T are in the order
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
MX {tex}\longrightarrow{ }_s^{M_s^{+}}+\underset{s}{X^{-}}{/tex} (Where s is the solubility)
Then Ksp = s2 or s = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}{/tex}
Similarly for MX2 {tex}\longrightarrow M_s^{2+}+\underset{2 s}{2 X^{-}}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} (2s)2 = 4s3 or s = {tex}\left[\frac{K_{s p}}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
and for M3X {tex}\longrightarrow \underset{3 s}{3 M^{+}+\underset{s}{X^{-3}}}{/tex}
Ksp = (3s)3 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 27s4 or s = {tex}\left[\frac{K_{s p}}{27}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex}
From the given values of Ksp for MX, MX2 and M3X, we can find the solubilities of those salts at temperature, T.
Solubility of MX = {tex}\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Solubility of MX2 = {tex}\left[\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{4}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{32}{4} \times 10^{-15}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\left[8 \times 10^{-15}\right]^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex} or 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
Solubility of M3X = {tex}\left[\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-15}}{27}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left[10^{-16}\right]^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} or 10-4
Thus, the solubilities are in the order MX > M3X > MX2 i. e. the correct answer is (d).

questions.question: A solution which is 10-3 M each in Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+ and Hg2+is treated with 10-16 M sulphide ion. If Ksp of MnS, FeS, ZnS and HgS are 10-15, 10-23, 10-20and 10-54 respectively, which one will precipitate first?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Minimum S2- concentration would be required for precipitation of least soluble HgS.
For Hgs, S2- required for precipitation is {tex}\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]=\frac{K_{\mathrm{F}}}{\left[\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{10^{-54}}{10^{-3}}=10^{-51} \mathrm{M}{/tex}

questions.question: Identify the correct order of solubility of Na2S, CuS and ZnS in an aqueous medium.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Alkali metal salts are usually more soluble than the salts of transition metals. Also, CuS is less soluble than ZnS because of 3d9 configuration of Cu2+. Therefore, solubility order is Na2S > ZnS > CuS

questions.question: For a sparingly soluble salt ApBq, the relationship of its solubility product (Ls ) with its solubility (S) is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
ApBq(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} pA+q + qB-p
Ls = (pS)p {tex}\cdot{/tex} (qS)q = pp{tex}\cdot{/tex}qq{tex}\cdot{/tex}S(p+q)

questions.question: Amongst the following hydroxides, the one which has the lowest value of Ksp at ordinary temperature (about 25° C) is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: In case of hydroxides of Group II A, solubility increases down the group. Therefore, Be(OH)2 is least soluble, has lowest value of Ksp.

questions.question: When equal volumes of the following solutions are mixed, precipitation of AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) will occur only with
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For a precipitation to occur
Solubility product < Ionic product
Given Ksp = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
Calculating ionic products in each
Ionic product = [Ag+][Cl] = {tex}\frac{10^{-4}}{2} \times \frac{10^{-4}}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
which is greater than Ksp (1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10).

questions.question: The precipitate of CaF2, (Ksp =1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 ) is obtained, when equal volumes of which of the following are mixed?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For precipitation to occur, ionic product > solubility products
Given, KspCaF2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
CaF2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ca2+ + 2F-
Ionic product of CaF2 = [Ca2+] [F-]2
Calculate I.P. in each case

  1. I.P. of CaF2 = (10-4) {tex}\times{/tex} (10-4)2 = 10-12
  2. I.P. of CaF2 = (10-2) {tex}\times{/tex} (10-3)2 = 10-8
  3. I.P. of CaF2 = (10-5) {tex}\times{/tex} (10-3)2 = 10-11
  4. I.P. of CaF2 = (10-3) {tex}\times{/tex} (10-5)2 = 10-13

{tex}\because{/tex} I.P > solubility in choice (b) only.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ppt of CaF2 is obtained in case of choice (b) only.

questions.question: The solubility of BaSO4, in water, is 2.33 x 10-3 gram/ liter. Its solubility product will be (molecular weight of BaSO4 = 233)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
The solubility of BaSO4BaSO4 in g/litre is given 2.33×10
{tex}∴ In \ mole/ litre \ n= \frac{W}{m.wt.}= \frac{2.33×10^{−3}}{233}{/tex}
{tex}=1 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
Because BaSO4 is a compound
{tex}K_{sp}=S^2=[1×10^{−5}]^2=1×10^{−10}{/tex}

questions.question: The unit in which the solubility product of barium phosphate is expressed as
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Barium phosphate when dissolved in water:
{tex}Ba_3(PO_4)_2 \rightarrow 3Ba^{2+} + 2PO_4^{3-}{/tex} 
Let the solubility of {tex}Ba_3(PO_4)_2 {/tex} be s mol dm-3
Solubility product = (Ba2+)3 (PO43-)2 
{tex}= s^5 \ mo{l^5}\;d{m^{ - 15}}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility of {tex}CuBr{/tex} is {tex}2 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol/L{/tex} at {tex}25^\circ C.{/tex} The {tex}{K_{sp}}{/tex} value for {tex}CuBr{/tex} is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
The reaction for the dissociation of CuBr is {tex}C u B r \rightleftharpoons \underset{2 \times 10^{-4}}{C u^{+}}+\underset{2 \times 10^{-4}}{B r}{/tex}
The expression for the solubility product is {tex}K_{s p}=\left[C u^{+}\right]\left[B r^{-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}K_{s p}=\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right)=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{mol}^{2} L^{-2}{/tex}

questions.question: In which of the following solvents is silver chloride most soluble?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: AgCl + 2NH3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c} \left.\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl} \\ \text { Soluble } \end{array}\right.{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following species is more soluble in water?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
S of M (OH)3 = {tex}\sqrt[4]{\frac{K_{s p}}{27}}=\sqrt[4]{\frac{10^{-35}}{27}}{/tex} = 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
S of M (OH)2 = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{10^{-30}}{4}}{/tex} = 6.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11
S of MOH = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{10^{-26}}{/tex} = 10-13 and {tex}\sqrt{10^{-28}}{/tex} = 10-14

questions.question: If the solubility product of lead iodide (PbI2) is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8, its solubility will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = 4S3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4S3 = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M

questions.question: At 30oC the solubility of Ag2CO3 (Ksp = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12) would be greatest in one litre of:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Presence of common ion decreases the solubility of salt.

questions.question: Ksp of AgCl at 18oC is 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. If Ag+ of solution is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol/litre the Cl- that must exceed before AgCl is precipitated would be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = 1.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = [Ag+] [Cl-] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} [Cl-]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Cl-] = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 mol litre-1

questions.question: If the solubility of lithium sodium hexafluoroaluminate, Li3Na3 (AlF6)2 is a mol/litre, its solubility product is equal to:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Li3Na3 (AlF6)2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 3Li+ + 3Na+ + 2 Al{tex}\mathrm{F}_{4}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = (3a)3 (3a)3 (2a)2 = 2916a8

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl is 0.0015 g/litre. The solubility product of AgCl will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp = s2 = {tex}\left(\frac{0.0015}{143.5}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question: If the concentration of Cr{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} ion in a saturated solution of silver chromate be 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M, solubility product of silver chromate will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp of Ag2CrO4 = [Ag+]2 [Cr{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}]
Also, if [Cr{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Then Ag+ = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Ksp = (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)2 (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
= 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 M

questions.question: 0.1 millimole of CdSO4 are present in 10 mL acid solution of 0.08 N HCl. Now H2S is passed to precipitate all the Cd2+ ions. The pH of the solution after filtering off precipitate, boiling of H2S and making the solution 100 mL by adding H2O is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\therefore \begin{array}{r} \text { Millimoles of } \mathrm{H}^{+} \text {left }=0.8+0.1 \times 2=1.0 \\ \qquad \begin{aligned} (\text { form } \mathrm{HCl}) &\left(\text { from } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right) \end{aligned} \end{array}{/tex}
Total volume = 100 mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [H+] = {tex}\frac{1}{100}{/tex} = 10-2 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 2

questions.question: How many gram of CaC2O4 will dissolve in one litre of the saturated solution? Ksp of CaC2O4 is 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 mol-2 and its molar mass is 128 g mol-1:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}{/tex} = (2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9)1/2
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol litre-1
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{w}{128}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} w = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 g

questions.question: The pH at which Mg(OH)2 begins to precipitate from a solution containing 0.10 M Mg2+ ions (Ksp of Mg (OH)2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
When Mg(OH)2 starts precipitation, then,
[Mg2+] [OH-]2 = Ksp of Mg (OH)2
[0.1] [OH-]2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 10-5 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pH = 14 - pOH
pH = 14 - 5 = 9

questions.question: Basic lead carbonate is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: It is basic lead carbonate.

questions.question: When different types of salts have nearly the same solubility product constant Ksp but less than one the most soluble salt is that:

options.option: which produces more charge on ion
which produces less charge on ion
which produces a maximum number of ions
which produces a minimum number of ions

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For different salts such as AB, AB2, AB3, ... etc.
Ksp = s2, 4s3, 27s4, ... respectively. If Ksp is same (but less than one) for different salts, then s is more for the salt in which more number of ions.

questions.question: The concentration of hydroxyl ion in a solution left after mixing 100 mL of 0.1 M MgCl2 and 100 mL of 0.2 M NaOH (Ksp Mg(OH)2 = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Thus, 10 m mole of Mg(OH)2 are formed. The ionic product of [Mg2+] [OH-]2 is therefore = {tex}\left[\frac{10}{200}\right] \times\left[\frac{20}{200}\right]^{2}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 which is more than Ksp of Mg (OH)2 and thus solution will lead to precipitation of Mg (OH)2
Also, Ksp = 4S3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-11}}{4}}{/tex} = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [OH-] = 2S = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4

questions.question: The solubility of PbCl2 in water is 0.01 M at 25oC. Its maximum concentration in 0.1 M NaCl will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksb PbCl2 = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.01)3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
In NaCl solution for PbCl2;
Ksp = [Pb2+] [Cl-]2
or 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = [Pb2+] [0.1]2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Pb2+] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: Sulphide ions in alkaline solution react with solid sulphur to form polyvalent sulphide ions. The equilibrium constant for the formation of {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} from S and S2- ions are 1.7 and 5.3 respectively. Equilibrium constant for the formation of {tex}\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} from {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} and S is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\mathrm{S}(s)+\mathrm{S}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}, {tex}K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=1.7{/tex}
{tex}2 \mathrm{S}(s)+\mathrm{S}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}, \quad K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=5.3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}=1.7{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]}=5.3{/tex} ...(ii)
Now for {tex}\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}+\mathrm{S}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex}K_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By eqs. (2) and (1), {tex}\frac{K_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}_{3}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{S}_{2}^{2-}\right]}=K_{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} KC = 3.11

questions.question: The solubility products of Al(OH)3 and Zn(OH)2 are 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 and 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 respectively. If NH4OH is added to a solution containing Al3+ and Zn2+ ions, then the substance precipitated first is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: The solubility of Al(OH)3 is lesser than Zn(OH)2.

questions.question: Ksp = [A]3 [B]2 for the salt where A and B are the cation and anion as the case may be stands true for:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For each case Ksp = [A2+]3 [B3-]2
or Ksp = [A3+]2 [B2-]3

questions.question: If the sparingly soluble salts M2X, QY2 and PZ3 have the same solubilities, their Ksp values are related as:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp of M2X = 4S3
Ksp of QY2 = 4S3
Ksp of PZ3 = 27S4
Note that S is small and in the order 10-x because of sparingly soluble salt.

questions.question: The purification of NaCl by passing HCl gas through an impure solution of NaCl is based on the principle of:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: An increase in [Cl-] due to passage of HCl to NaCl solution makes [Na+] [Cl-] > KspNaCl and thus NaCl gets precipitated.

questions.question: The solubility product of Hg2I2 is equal to:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Hg2I2 {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}{/tex} + 2I-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = {tex}\left[\mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}\right]{/tex}[I-]2

questions.question: Ksp of CaSO4 is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 CaSO4 will show precipitation on mixing equal volumes of the following solutions:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Ca2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex} > Ksp for case (c)
{tex}\left[\frac{6 \times 10^{-6} \times V}{2 V}\right]\left[\frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times V}{2 V}\right]{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 > Ksp

questions.question: M(OH)x has Ksp = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 and solubility 10-14 M. The value of x is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 = [M+] [OH-]x = [10-4] [x {tex}\cdot{/tex} 10-4]x
= xx {tex}\cdot{/tex} (10-4)1+x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} This shows that x = 2

questions.question: The solubility products of MA, MB, MC and MD are 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 and 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 respectively. If a 0.01 M solution of MX is added dropwise to a mixture containing A-, B-, C- and D- ions, then the one to be precipitated first will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = [M+] [anion]; [A-] = {tex}\frac{10^{-10}}{0.01}{/tex}; [B-] = {tex}\frac{10^{-3}}{0.01}{/tex}; [C-] = {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{0.01}{/tex}; [D-] = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}{/tex}
Thus [A-] is smallest and the product of ionic concentration will exceed its Ksp in MA to show precipitation first.

questions.question: Which of the following will not produce a precipitate with dilute silver nitrate solution?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: AgF is soluble in water.

questions.question: The solubility product of a salt AB2 is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 at 373 K. The solubility of AB2 in boiling water will be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = 4s3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s3 = {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-9}}{4}{/tex} = 10-9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s = 10-3 M

questions.question: The solubility of BaF2 in a solution of Ba(NO3)2 will be represented by the concentration term:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Let s is the solubility of BaF2 in a solution of BaNO3, then
Ksp = [Ba2+] [F-]2;
Then [F-] = 2s;
or {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [F-] = s

questions.question: The precipitate of CaF2 (Ksp = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) is obtained when equal volumes of the following are mixed:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Ca2+] [F-]2 = {tex}\left[\frac{10^{-2} \times V}{2 V}\right] \times\left[\frac{10^{-3} \times V}{2 V}\right]^{2}{/tex}
= 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 which is more than Ksp

questions.question: The solubility of AgI in NaI solution is less than that in pure water because:

options.option: the temperature of the solution decreases
solubility product of Agl is less than that of NaI

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: An increase in product concentration give rise to a backward reaction.

questions.question: The hydroxide with the highest solubility product is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Co(OH)2 is not precipitated in III gp. or it more soluble and thus, has high Ksp.

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl (Ksp = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) in a 0.10 M NaCl solution is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp AgCl = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = [Ag+] [Cl-] = [s] [s + 0.1] where, s is solubility of AgCl
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M

questions.question: The solubility of A2X3 is y mol dm-3. Its solubility product is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp = [A3+]2 [X2-]3 = (2y)2 (3y)3 = 108 y5

questions.question: What is the correct representation for the solubility product of SnS2?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: SnS2 = Sn4+ + 2S2-

questions.question: For a sparingly soluble salt Ap Bq, the relationship of its solubility product (Ls) with its solubility (s) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}ApBq\;\leftrightharpoons\;\underset{p.s}{pA^+\;}+\;\underset{q.s}{qB^-}{/tex}; Let solubility be s mol/litre, Thus
Ksp = [A+]p [B-]q = (ps)p {tex}\cdot{/tex} (qs)q
= pqqq (s)p+q

questions.question: The solubility of an aqueous solution of Mg(OH)2 is X mol litre-1, then its Ksp is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp of Mg(OH)2 = 4X3

questions.question: The solubility in water of a sparingly soluble salt AB2 is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol/litre. Its solubility product is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: For AB2 : Ksp = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15

questions.question: The molar solubility (in mol L-1) of a sparingly soluble salt MX4 is s. The corresponding solubility product is {tex}K_{s p} \cdot s{/tex} is given in terms of Ksp by the relation:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
MX4(aq.) {tex}\rightleftharpoons M_s^{4+}+\underset{4s}{\;4X^-}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = [M4+] [X-]4 = s {tex}\times{/tex} (4s)4 = 256s5
or s = {tex}\left[\frac{K_{s p}}{256}\right]^{1 / 5}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility product of silver bromide is 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13. The quantity of potassium bromide (molar mass taken as 120 g mol-1) to be added to 1 litre of 0.05 M solution of silver nitrate to start the precipitation of AgBr is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Ag+] [Br-] = Ksp AgBr
[0.05] [Br-] = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Br-] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = [KBr]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mole of KBr = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11
Weigth of KBr = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 {tex}\times{/tex} 120
= 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 g

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 is 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 298 K. The solubility (in mol/L) of Ag2CrO4 in a 0.1 M AgNO3 solution is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ionic product of Ag2CrO4 = [Ag+]2{tex}\left[\mathrm{CrO}_{4}^{-2}\right]{/tex}
= (2s + 0.1)2 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
neglecting s as compared to 0.1
s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10

questions.question:
Given:
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; {/tex} {tex}K_{1}^{\circ}=1.4 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} ; {/tex} {tex}K_{2}^{\circ}=4.3 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
The instability constant of the complex Ag {tex}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}^{+}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ko for
{tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}
{tex}K^{\circ}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}K_{1}^{\circ}=1.4 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+2}}{/tex}
and {tex}K_{2}^{\circ}=4.3 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K^{0}=K_{1}^{o} \times K_{2}^{o}=1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.3 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}
{tex}=6.02 \times 10^{-8}{/tex}

questions.question: The compound insoluble in acetic acid is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Oxalate is a weaker base than acetate.

questions.question: When equal volumes of the following solutions are mixed, precipitation of AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) will occur only with:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Q for AgCl = [Ag+] [Cl] = {tex}\left[\frac{10^{-4} \times V}{2 V}\right]\left[\frac{10^{-4} \times V}{2 V}\right]{/tex}
= 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
{tex}\because{/tex} Q > Ksp, precipitation will occur.

questions.question: Among the following hydroxides, the one which has the lowest value of Ksp at ordinary temperature (about 25oC) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Be(OH)2 is insoluble in water.

questions.question: An aqueous solution contains an unknown concentration of Ba2+. When 50 mL of a 1 solution of Na2SO4 is added, BaSO4 just begins to precipitate. The final volume is 500mL. The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10. What is the original concentration of Ba2+?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ba2+ + {tex}\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}\rightleftharpoons B\underset{ppt}{aS}O_{4(s)}{/tex}
Final conc. of {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]=\frac{\text { milli mole }}{\text { Total volume }}=\frac{1 \times 50}{500}{/tex},= 0.1 M
Final cone, of [Ba2+] when BaSO4 start precipitating
Ksp = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex}
10-10 = [Ba2+] (0.1 M)
[Ba2+] = 10-9 M in 500 mL
Initial cone. [Ba2+]:
Initial volume = Final volume - Volume of Na2SO4 added
= 500 - 50 = 450 mL
Initial milli mole = Final milli mole
450 {tex}\times{/tex} M = 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} 500
{tex}\therefore{/tex} M = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M

questions.question: In which of the following solutions the solubility of AgCl will be maximum?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: The solubility of AgCl is maximum in water. In presence of a common ion, the solubility decreases.

questions.question: Which one of the following is most soluble?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: The salt which has highest Ksp value, must be most soluble. Thus, MnS has highest Ksp value among all the given salts which makes it to be the most soluble salt.

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl will be minimum in:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Cl-] in CaCl2 = 0.02
[Ag+] in AgNO3 = 0.001
[Cl-] in NaCl = 0.01

questions.question: Ksp of CuS, Ag2S and HgS are 10-31, 10-44 and 10-54 respectively. Select the correct order for their solubility in water:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: The solubility order in water is Ag2S > CuS > HgS.

questions.question: The solubility of a saturated solution of calcium fluoride is 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol/L. Its solubility product is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp of CaF2 = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3 = 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: Solubility of M2S salt is 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 mole litre-1 then find out solubility product.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp of M2S = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6)3 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 M3

questions.question: Solubility of Mx2 type electrolytes is 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mole/litre, then find out Ksp of electrolytes.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}MX_2\;\rightleftharpoons\underset sM\;+\;\underset{2s}{2X}{/tex}
Ksp = 4s3 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 M

questions.question: The solubility product of Agl at 25oC is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 mol2 L-2. The solubility of AgI in 10-4 N solution of KI at 25oC is approx. (in mol/L-1)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Ag+] [I-] = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
(s) (s + 10-4) = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 (s <<< 10-4)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 10-16
s = 10-12 M

questions.question: The solubility product of a sparingly soluble salt AX2 is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11. Its solubility (in mol/litre) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For AX2 : Ksp = 4s3
{tex}\therefore \quad s=\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-11}}{4}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol/litre

questions.question: H2S gas when passed through a solution of cations containing HCl precipitates the cations of the second group of qualitative analysis but not those belonging to the fourth group. It is because:

options.option: presence of HCl decreases the sulphide ion concentration
sulphides of group IV cations are unstable in HCl
presence of HCl increases the sulphide ion concentration
solubility product of group II sulphides is more than that of group IV sulphides

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp of II gp. sulphide < Ksp of IV group sulphide. Thus, less S2- are required to precipitate II group sulphides.

questions.question: If pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12, the value of its Ksp is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
pH = 12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 2
or [OH]- = 10-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ba2+] = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{2}{/tex}
Ksp Ba(OH)2 = [Ba2+] [OH-]2 = {tex}\frac{10^{-2}}{2} \times{/tex} (10-2)2
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M3

questions.question:
In qualitative analysis, the metals of group I can be separated from other ions by precipitating them as chloride salts. A solution initially contains Ag+ and Pb2+ at a concentration of 0.10 M. Aqueous HCl is added to this solution until the Cl- concentration is 0.10 M. What will the concentrations of Ag+ and Pb2+ be at equilibrium?
(Ksp for AgCl = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M, [Pb2+] = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For AgCl : [Ag+] [Cl-] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
[Ag+] [0.1] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
[Ag+] = 1.8  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
For PbCl2 : [Pb2+] [Cl-]2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
[Pb2+] [0.1]2 = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
[Pb2+] = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3

questions.question: pH of a saturated solution of Ba(OH)2 is 12. The value of the solubility product (Ksp) of Ba(OH)2 is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
pH = 12 {tex}\therefore{/tex} pOH = 2
[OH-] = 10-2
Now Ba(OH)2 = Ba2+ + 2OH-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = [Ba2+ + [OH-]2
{tex}=\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{2}\right]{/tex} [10-2]2 {tex}\because{/tex} [(Ba2+) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} (OH-)]
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7

questions.question: Using the Gibbs energy change, {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = + 63.3 kJ, for the following reaction, Ag2CO3(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2Ag+(aq.) + {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} (aq.) the Ksp of Ag2CO3(s) in water at 25oC is: (R = 8.314 JK-1 mol-1).
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = - 2.303 RT log Ksp
63300 = -2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 log Ksp
Ksp = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: Solubility product of MX2 at 298 K is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12. At this temperature concentration of M2+ ion in mol per litre is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Ksp = 4s3 or {tex}s=\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 10-4

questions.question: The solubility product Ksp, of a sparingly soluble salt AgIO3 is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 at a given temperature. What is the mass of AgIO3 (mol. mass = 283) contained in 100 mL solution at this temperature is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = s2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m/l = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 283 {tex}\times \frac{100}{1000}{/tex} mL
= 28.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g/100mL

questions.question: Ksp of salt AB, AB2 and A3B are 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8, 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 and 2.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 respectively at temperature T. The solubility order of these salts in water at temperature T (in mol litre-1) is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Let solubility be s1, s2, s3 for AB, AB2 and A3B respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s1 = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
s2 = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{4}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
s3 = {tex}\sqrt[4]{\frac{K_{s p}}{27}}=\sqrt[4]{\frac{2.7 \times 10^{-15}}{27}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M

questions.question: Solid Ba(NO3)2 is gradually dissolved in a 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M Na2CO3 solution. At which concentration of Ba2+ precipitate of begins to form? Ksp of BaCO3 = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp BaCO3 = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2}\right]{/tex} = [Ba2+] {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ba2+] = [Ba(NO3)2] = 5.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4, AgCl, AgBr and AgI are respectively, 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13, 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17. Which one of the following salts will precipitate last if AgNO3 solution is added to the solution containing equal moles of NaCl, NaBr, NaI and Na2CrO4?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
From the Ksp values of the given salts calculate the solubility values. Salt having highest solubility will precipitate at last.
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{{\mathrm{Ag}}_2{\mathrm{CrO}}_4}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{2\mathrm s}{2\mathrm{Ag}^+}+\underset{\mathrm s}{{\mathrm{CrO}}_4{}^{2-}{}}{/tex}
Ksp = (2s)2(s)
1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 = 2s3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgCl}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Cl}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgBr}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Br}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.71 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
{tex}\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{AgI}}\;\rightleftharpoons\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm{Ag}^+}\;+\;\underset{\mathrm s}{\mathrm I^-}{/tex}
Ksp = s {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 = s2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
Hence, solubility of Ag2CrO4 is maximum thus, it will precipitate at last.

questions.question: MY and NY3, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same Ksp values of 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 at room temperature, which statements would be true in regard to MY and NY3?

options.option: The addition of the salt of KY to solution of MY and NY_3 will have no effect on their solubilities
The molar solubility of MY in water is less than that of NY_3
The salts MY and NY_3 are more soluble in 0.5 M KY than in pure water
The molar solubilities of MY and NY_3 in water are identical

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = s . s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = s2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}s=\sqrt{6.2 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
= 7.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 mol L-1

Ksp = s.(3s)3 = 27s4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = 27s4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1
Hence, molar solubility of MY in water is less than that of NY3.

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl (s) with solubility product 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 in 0.1 M NaCl solution would be:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}AgCl\;\rightleftharpoons A\underset s{g^+}+\underset{s+0.1}{Cl^-}{/tex};
{tex}N\underset{\underset0{0.1}}{aCl(aq)}\rightarrow N\underset{\underset{0.1}0}{a^+(a}q)\;+\;C\underset{\underset{0.1}0}{l^-(a}q){/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Ksp = [Ag+] [Cl-]
1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = s {tex}\times{/tex} (s + 0.1)
= s {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 (s << 0.1)
s = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question: Concentration of the Ag+ ions in a saturated solution of Ag2C2O4 is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1. Solubility product of Ag2C2O4 is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}Ag_2C_2(s)\;\rightleftharpoons2\underset{2s}{Ag^+}C_2\underset s{O_4^{2-}}{/tex}
Given [Ag+] = 2s = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
s = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4
Ksp = (2s)2 {tex}\times{/tex} s = 4s3
= 4(1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3 = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: The solubility of BaSO4 in water is 2.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 gL-1 at 298 K. The value of its solubility product (Ksp) will be: (Given molar mass of BaSO4 = 233 g mol-1)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Solubility of BaSO4, s = {tex}\frac{2.42 \times 10^{-3}}{233}{/tex} (mol L-1)
= 1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 (mol L-1)
{tex}BaSO_4(s)\rightleftharpoons\underset{(s)}{Ba^{2+}}(aq.)+\underset{(s)}{SO_4^{2-}}(aq.){/tex}
Ksp = [Ba2+] {tex}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex} = s2
= (1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5)2
= 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 mol2 L-2

questions.question: Zn salt is mixed with (NH4)2S of molarity 0.021 M. The mass of Zn2+ remains unprecipitated in 12 mL of this solution (Ksp ZnS = 4.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24):
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[(NH4)2S] = 0.021 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [S2-] = 0.021 M
{tex}\because{/tex} At equilibrium [Zn2+] [S2-] = Ksp of ZnS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Zn2+] = {tex}\frac{4.51 \times 10^{-24}}{0.021}{/tex} = 2.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Zn2+] left in solution = 2.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 {tex}\times{/tex} 65 g/litre
{tex}=\frac{2.15 \times 10^{-22} \times 65 \times 12}{1000}{/tex} g/12 mL
= 1.677 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 g/12 mL

questions.question: A solution of Na2CO3 is added drop by drop to 1 litre of a solution containing 10-4 mole of Ba2+ and 10-5 mole of Ag+, if Ksp for BaCO3 is 8.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 and Ksp for Ag2CO3, is 6.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, then which is not true:

options.option: no precipitate of Ag_2CO_3 will appear until $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 6.9 $\times$ 10^-2 mol per litre
no precipitate of BaCO_3 will appear until $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 8.1 $\times$ 10^-5 mol per litre
a precipitate of Ag_2CO_3 will appear when $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]$ reaches 6.9 $\times$ 10^-5 mol litre^-1

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For precipitation of Ag2CO3;
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]=\frac{K_{s p}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}=\frac{6.9 \times 10^{-12}}{\left(10^{-5}\right)^{2}}{/tex},= 6.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 and for precipitation of BaCO3,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}\right]=\frac{K_{s p}}{\left[\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{8.1 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-4}}{/tex} = 8.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: A student wants to prepare a saturated solution of Ag+ ion. He has got only three samples AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10), AgBr (Ksp = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13) and Ag2CrO4 (Ksp = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12). Which compound he should use to have maximum [Ag+]?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=s_{\mathrm{AgCl}}=\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
= 1.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=s_{\mathrm{AgBr}}=\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}=\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
= 7.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M
{tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{4}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
Also, {tex}s_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}=2 \times s_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}}{/tex}
= 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M
This will give maximum value.

questions.question: The volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g of BaSO4 (Ksp = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10) at 25oC is:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Solubility of BaSO4 = {tex}\sqrt{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
= 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} wt. of BaSO4 = 1.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 233
or {tex}w_{\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}}{/tex} = 244.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 g/litre
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g BaSO4 is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{24437 \times 10^{-5}}{/tex} = 410 litre

questions.question: A solution is a mixture of 0.05 M NaCl and 0.05 M Nal. The concentration of iodide ion in the solution when AgCl just starts precipitating is equal to: (Ksp AgCI = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M2 : Ksp AgI = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 M2)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For precipitation
[I-] [Ag+] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
{tex}\because{/tex} [I-] = 0.05 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+] = {tex}\frac{4}{5} \times{/tex} 10-14 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15
[CI-] [Ag+] = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10
{tex}\because{/tex} [CI-] = 0.05 {tex}\therefore{/tex} [Ag+] = {tex}\frac{1}{5} \times{/tex} 10-8 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
Thus [I-] needed for precipitation is equal to 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 and it will continue up to the stage when AgCI begins to precipitate, i.e., 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 becomes the conc. of [Ag+]
Thus [I-] left = {tex}\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 M

questions.question:
Which statement is wrong about solubility of AgCl?

  1. More in pure water than in 0.1 M NaNO3 due to diverse ion effect.
  2. Less in pure water than in 0.1 M NaNO3 due to diverse ion effect.
  3. More in pure water than in 0.1 M NaNO3 due to the shielding of the dissociated ionic species which results in a decrease in activity.
  4. Ksp = {tex}\frac{K_{s p}^{o}}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}{/tex}, where {tex}K_{s p}^{\circ}=a_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}} \times a_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Diverse ion is the ion which is not common to the slightly soluble salt. The diverse ion effect causes the deviation of solute activity from concentration. This slightly decreases the solubility of ionic salts from water. Hence, solubility of AgCl is more in pure water than in 0.1 M NaNO3 ​ due to diverse ion effect.
aAg+ .aCl- = {tex}{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\mathbf{o}}{/tex} (Ksp at a defined state of ionic activity)
fAg+ [Ag+] . fCl- [Cl-] = {tex}{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\mathbf{o}}{/tex}
[Ag+] [Cl-] = {tex}\frac{{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\circ}}{{f}_{\mathrm{Ag}}+\cdot {f}_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{-}}{/tex} = Ksp

Ksp = (3s)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (3s)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (2s)2 = 2916 s8

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 is 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 at 298 K. The solubility (in mol/L) of Ag2CrO4 in a 0.1 M AgNO3 solution is:

questions.explanation:
Plan in presence of common ion (in this case Ag+ ion) solubility of sparingly soluble salt is decreased.
Let solubility of Ag2CrO4 in presence of 0.1 M
AgNO3 = x
Ag2CrO4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons \,{/tex}2Ag+ + {tex}CrO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
AgNO3 {tex} \rightleftharpoons \,{/tex} Ag+ + {tex}NO_3^{ - }{/tex}
Total [Ag+] = (2v + 0.1)M = 0.1 M
as x <<< 0.1M
{tex}[CrO_4^{2 }]{/tex} = xM
Thus [Ag-]2 {tex}[CrO_4^{2 }]{/tex} = Ksp
(0.1)2 (x) = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12
x = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 M

questions.question: When NaOH is dissolved in water, heat is evolved. If the temperature is raised, the solubility of NaOH:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Solubility is defined as the concentration of the solute in a saturated solution. So when considering the increase in solubility with temperature, you have to check the enthalpy of solution of NaOH in a saturated solution of NaOH.
When you dissolve NaOH in pure water, the process is highly exothermic, but as the concentration of NaOH increases, the process becomes less exothermic and eventually, near the saturation point it becomes endothermic (it becomes less favourable for more NaOH to dissolve).
So according to Le Chatelier’s Principle, although the dissolution process is overall exothermic since solubility is determined only at the saturation point, the solubility increases with increase in temperature.

questions.question:
Which one is correct decreasing order of solubility of AgCl?

  1. in water
  2. in 0.1 M NaCl
  3. in 0.01 M BaCl2
  4. in 0.1 M NH3
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
With ammonia, AgCl forms a complex [Ag(NH3)2] Cl. Hence AgCl is more soluble in ammonia than in water. When a common ion such as chloride ion is present, the solubility of AgCl is decreased. Greater is the concentration of chloride ion from strong electrolyte, greater will be the decreases in the solubility.
The chloride ion concentration in 0.1M NaCl solution is 0.1.
The chloride ion concentration in 0.1 M BaCl2 ​ solution is 0.2{tex}\alpha{/tex} where {tex}\alpha{/tex} is the degree of dissociation of BaCl2. Hence, the decreasing order of the solubility is (iv > i > ii > iii).

Thus, minimum [S2-] is for HgS and so it will be precipitated first.

questions.explanation: CaO, CaCO3 and Ca(OH)2 dissolved in CH3COOH due to the formation of (CH3COO)2Ca. But CaC2O4 does not dissolve as CH3COO− is a stronger conjugate base than {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}.

questions.question: The stoichiometry and solubility product of salt with the solubility curve given below is, respectively:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
From the given curve,
if [X] = 1 mM then [Y] = 2 mM
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Salt is XY2
Ksp = [X] [Y]2 = (10-3) (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M3

questions.question: Identify the CORRECT solubility equilibrium expression for the dissolution of calcium phosphate in water.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ca3(PO4)2(s) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}3 {Ca}_{({aq})}^{2+}+2 {PO}_{4({aq})}^{3-}{/tex}
Ksp = {tex}\left[{Ca}^{2+}\right]^{3}\left[{PO}_{4}^{3-}\right]^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The solubility product of AgCl is 1.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 at 100oC. The solubility of silver chloride in boiling water may be ________.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
AgCl(S) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} {tex}{Ag}_{({aq})}^{+}+{Cl}_{({aq})}^{-}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ag+][Cl-] = S {tex}\times{/tex} S = S2
S = {tex}\sqrt{{K}_{\text {sp }}}=\sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M

questions.question: The solubility of BaSO4 in water is 2.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 g L-1 at 298 K. The value of its solubility product (Ksp) will be ________. (Given: molar mass of BaSO4 = 233 g mol-1)
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}BaSO_4 \rightleftharpoons \underset S{Ba^{2+}}+\;\underset S{SO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
Ksp = S2
= {tex}\left(\frac{2.42 \times 10^{-3} {g} {L}^{-1}}{233 {g} {mol}^{-1}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
= (1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol L-1)2
= (1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 mol2 L-2

questions.question: Concentration of the Ag+ ions in a saturated solution of Ag2C2O4 is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1. Solubility product of Ag2C2O4 is ________.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}Ag_2C_2O_{4(s)} \rightleftharpoons \underset{2S}{2Ag_{aq}^+}+\;\underset S{C_2O_{4(aq)}^-}{/tex}
2S = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1
Ksp = 4S3 = 4(1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)3
Ksp = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12

questions.question: MY and NY3, two nearly insoluble salts, have the same Ksp values of 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 at room temperature. Which statement would be TRUE in regard to MY and NY3?

options.option: The molar solubility of MY is water is less than that of NY_3.
The addition of the salt of KY to solution of MY and NY_3 will have no effect on their solubilities.
The molar solubilities of MY and NY_3 in water are identical.
The salts MY and NY_3 are more soluble in 0.5 M KY than in pure water.

questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
For MY, x = 1, and y = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = {tex}x^{x} \cdot y^{y} \cdot {S}^{x+y}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} S2 = S2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = (6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13)1/2 = 7.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 mol dm-3
For NY3, x = 1, and y = 3,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = 11 {tex}\times{/tex}x 33 {tex}\times{/tex} S4 = 27S4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = {tex}\left(\frac{6.2 \times 10^{-13}}{27}\right)^{1 / 4}{/tex} = 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol dm-3
Thus, the molar solubility of MY in water is lesser than that of NY3.

questions.question: The Ksp of Ag2CrO4 , AgCl, AgBr and Agl are respectively, 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12, 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10, 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 and 8.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17. Which one of the following salts will precipitate last if AgNO3 solution is added to the solution containing equal moles of NaCl, NaBr, Nal and Na2CrO4?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp [Ag+]
Ag2CrO4 [Ag+]2 {tex}[CrO_4^{2-}]{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{{K}_{s {p}} /\left[{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{/tex}
AgCl [Ag+][Cl-] Ksp/[Cl-]
AgBr [Ag+][Br-] Ksp/[Br-]
Agl [Ag+][I-] Ksp/[l-]

Therefore, [Ag+] required for precipitation of Ag2CrO4 is the highest. Hence, Ag2CrO4 will precipitate last.

questions.question: The solubility product for a salt of the type AB is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8. What is the molarity of its saturated solution?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol/L

questions.question:
Which of the following salt is most soluble?

Salt Ksp
BaSO4 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
CoS 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22
PbSO4 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
AgBr 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: All the given salts are of type AB (1:1). Therefore, the larger the value of the KsP, the greater is the solubility.

questions.question: The solubility of AgCl(S) with solubility product 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 in 0.1 M NaCl solution would be ________.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset{\begin{array}{l}Consentration\\(M)\end{array}}{AgCl}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset S{Ag}\;+\;\underset{S\;+\;0.1}{Cl_{(aq)}^-}{/tex}
Ksp = [Ag+] [Cl-]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = (S) (S + 0.1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = S {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 ({tex}\because{/tex} S << 0.1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 M

questions.question: Solubility of calcium sulphate in pure water at a certain temperature is 1.14 grams per litre. The value of solubility product is ________.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}{CaSO}_{4(s)} \rightleftharpoons {Ca}_{({aq})}^{2+}+{SO}_{4({aq})}^{2-}{/tex}
Ksp = {tex}\left[{Ca}^{2+}\right]\left[{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex}
[CaSO4] = 1.14g {tex}\times \frac{1 {mol}}{136.14 {g}}{/tex}
= 8.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol
= 8.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M
= {tex}\left[{Ca}^{2+}\right]=\left[{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ksp = (8.37 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)2 = 7.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

questions.question: Kidney stones are formed by calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and calcium oxalate (CaC2O4). If the total concentration of calcium ions in serum that is in equilibrium with these two solids is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M, and the ratio of the solubility product constant of CaCO3 to that of CaC2O4 is 3, the solubility product of CaCO3 is ________.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex}\underset X{CaCO_3}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{\underset{(X\;+\;Y)}X}{Ca^{2+}}+\underset{\underset XX}{CO_3^{2-}}{/tex}
{tex}\underset Y{CaC_2O_4}\; \rightleftharpoons \;\underset{\underset{(X\;+\;Y)}Y}{Ca^{2+}}+\underset{\underset YY}{CO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{K}_{{sp}\left({CaCO}_{3}\right)}}{{K}_{{sp}\left({CaC}_{2} {O}_{4}\right)}}=\frac{{X}}{{Y}}{/tex} = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 3Y
(X + Y) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
= total Ca2+ ion concentration
(3Y + Y) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
Y = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
X = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
{tex}{K}_{{sp}\left({CaCO}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = [Ca2+] {tex}[CO_3^{2-}]{/tex}
= 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
= 48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9

questions.question:
Calculate the difference in molar solubility of chromate ion at 298 K from the information given below:
{tex}{K}_{\text {sp }\left({A}_{{B} 2} {CrO}_{4}\right)}=1.1 \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
{tex}{K}_{{sp}\left({BaCro}_{4}\right)}=1.2 \times 10^{-10}{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt[3]{0.275}{/tex} = 0.65
{tex}\sqrt{1.2}{/tex} = 1.1
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
1. {tex}\underset{K_{sp}=4S^3}{Ag_2CrO_4}\;=\;\underset{2S}{2Ag^+}\;+\;\underset S{CrO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
    S = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-12}}{4}}{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
    {tex}\left[{CrO}_{4}^{2-}\right]{/tex} = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
  2. {tex}{BaCrO_4}\;=\;\underset{S}{Ba^{2+}}\;+\;\underset S{CrO_4^{2-}}{/tex}
    Ksp = S2
    S = {tex}\sqrt{{K}_{{sp}}}=\sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M
    Difference = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 - 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
    = 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 M

questions.question: The maximum amount of Zn2+ ions that can be mixed in 16 mL solution of (NH4)2S having molarity = 0.026 M, when no precipitation occurs is ________. [Ksp(ZnS) = 3.54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22]
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[(NH4)2S] = 0.026 M
[S2-] = 0.026 M
At equilibrium, [Zn2+] [S2-] = Ksp of ZnS
[Zn2+] = {tex}\frac{3.54 \times 10^{-22}}{0.026}{/tex} = 1.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum amount of Zn2+
= 1.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 mol/L = {tex}\frac{1.36 \times 10^{-20} \times 65 \times 16}{1000}{/tex}
= 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 g/16mL

questions.question: Silver chloride tends to be more soluble in only one of the solvents given below. Select the best one.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: AgCl is most soluble in Aqueous ammonia solution. AgCl reacts with it to form a complex, [Ag(NH3)2]+Cr-.

questions.question: Calculate the molar solubility (S) of a salt like zirconium phosphate of molecular formula (Zr4+)3{tex}\left(\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right)_4{/tex}.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
[Zr4+] = 3S and {tex}\left[\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-}\right]{/tex} = 4S
and Ksp = (3S)3(4S)4 = 6912(S)7
or S = {tex}\left\{\frac {K_{\mathrm{sp}}}{\left(3^3 \times 4^4\right)}\right\}^{\frac17}{/tex} = {tex}(\frac{K_{sp}}{6912})^\frac{1}{7}{/tex}

questions.question: The addition of NaCl to AgCl decreases the solubility of AgCl because
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: [Cl-] increases but Ksp = [Ag+][Cl-] remains constant. So, [Ag+] decreases.

questions.question: The solubility product of a sparingly soluble salt AB at room temperature is 1.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6, its molar solubility is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: S = {tex}\sqrt{K_{s p}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1.21 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M

questions.question: Solubility of calcium phosphate (molecular mass, M) in water is W g per100 mL at 25°C. Its solubility product at 25°C will be approximately.
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
S = {tex}\frac{10 \mathrm{~W}}{M}{/tex}moL/L
Ksp of Ca3(PO4)2 = 108 S5
{tex}= 108\left(\frac{10 W}{M}\right)^5{/tex} {tex}= 10^7\left(\frac{W}{M}\right)^5{/tex} (approximately)

questions.question: The solubility product Ksp of the sparingly soluble salt Ag2CrO4 is 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-12. The molar solubility of the salt is
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1

questions.question: The solubility product of CuS, CdS and HgS are 10-31, 10-44,10-54 respectively. The solubility of these sulphides are in the order:
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Higher the value of the solubility product of the salt, higher will be the solubility of the salt. Thus, the solubility of the sulphide salts are in the order CuS > CdS > HgS.

questions.question: In which of the following, the solubility of AgCl will be minimum?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
{tex} AgCl \rightleftharpoons Ag ^{+}+ Cl ^{-}{/tex}
{tex}NaCl \rightleftharpoons Na ^{+}+ Cl ^{-}{/tex}
Thus, due to common ion, Cl- the equilibrium of dissociation of AgCl shifts in backward direction which decreases its solubility.

questions.question: HCI was passed through a solution of CaCl2, MgCl2 and NaCl. Which of the following compound(s) crystallise(s)?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation: Since CaCl2 and MgCl2 are more soluble than NaCl, on passing HCI(g) through a solution containing CaCI2, MgCl2 and NaCl, then NaCI crystallizes out (common ion effect).

questions.question: The solubility product for a salt of the type AB is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8. What is the molarity of its standard solution?
questions.topicName: Solubility Equilibria of Sparingly Soluble Salts
questions.explanation:
Ksp = s2
So, Solubility,
{tex}s =\sqrt{ K _{ sp }}=\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol/L
